title: Abhisamācārikadharma
consensus_date: ca. 1st c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda recension; earlier than the Mahāvastu)
basis: M. Prasad, A Comparative Study of Abhisamācārikā (1984)
source_file: cleaned_Comparative Study of Abhisamacarika_Maulichand Prasad, 1984.txt
recension_note: The text is the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda Vinaya appendix on monastic conduct, surviving only in a single Nepalese BHS MS tradition (critical ed. Abhisamācārika-Dharma Study Group, Taisho Univ. 1998-). Prasad dates the composition layer to after 1st c. BCE and before the Mahāvastu; this is the composition date of this Lokottaravāda recension, the only recension extant.
note: Current median 74 (range -100 to 200) sits within Prasad's scholarly window; not off.

title: Bhikṣuṇī-Karmavācanā
consensus_date: ca. 6th-7th c. CE (Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda recension; the extant Oxford/Bodleian Sanskrit MS, akin to Gilgit material)
basis: Schmidt 1993 (ed. of Bodleian MS Sansk. c.25); cf. Prebish, Survey of Vinaya Literature (1994); Emms 2012 on Gilgit/Sarvāstivādin material
source_file: cleaned_Survey of Vinaya Litterature_Prebish, 1994.txt
recension_note: Karmavācanā (formulary for ecclesiastical acts, here the nuns' ordination) is procedurally old as a genre, but our text is Schmidt's edition of a single Sanskrit MS (Bodleian Sansk. c.25) belonging to the Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda tradition, closely related to the Gilgit Karmavācanā material that Wille/Banerjee date to the 5th-6th c. and Emms to ~6th c. CE. Date the surviving Sanskrit MS recension, not the early formulary.
note: Median 89 (range -100 to 200) dates the genre's origin; the extant Sanskrit MS recension is mid-1st-millennium CE. Off by >75y; corrected date low-confidence (no recension-specific date statement, inferred from cognate Gilgit material).

title: Bhikṣuṇīvinaya
consensus_date: 1st-5th c. CE (BHS Ārya-Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādin recension; composed in the period of, and resembling, the Mahāvastu)
basis: Nolot, Règles de discipline des nonnes bouddhistes (1991); edition: Roth, Bhikṣuṇī-Vinaya (1970)
source_file: cleaned_Regles de Discipline des Nonnes Bouddhistes_Edith Nolot, 1991.txt
recension_note: Nolot: the Lokottaravādin Bhikṣuṇīvinaya is not a 'datable harmonious whole' and shows traces of reshaping; she dates it by analogy with the Mahāvastu to the 1st-5th c. CE, and notes the sole (Beijing) manuscript is palaeographically not later than the 11th-12th c. The pre-sectarian disciplinary core is older, but our Roth edition is this specific BHS Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādin recension, dated to the 1st-5th-c. composition span. Midpoint ~250 CE.
note: cur_med 53 sits at the early (1st-c.) edge; Nolot's 1st-5th-c. composition span centers ~250 CE, >75y later.

title: Bhikṣukarmavākya
consensus_date: 2nd-4th c. CE (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya karmavācanā recension)
basis: Schopen (Buddhist Monks and Business Matters, 2004) per Clarke; cf. Vogel/Vinaya-redaction debate in French & Nathan (Buddhism and Law, 2014)
source_file: cleaned_Clarke_Family Matters in Indian Buddhist Monasticisms.txt
recension_note: The Bhikṣukarmavākya is an ordination (upasampadā) karmavācanā formulary extracted from the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya; Banerjee edited it from a late Nepalese MS, but the formulary belongs to the MSV Vinaya recension. Scholarship is split: Schopen places the bulk of the MSV-vinaya in NW India in the 1st-2nd c. CE, while Vogel's lunisolar-calendar argument (cited in French & Nathan) would push redactional closing to the 4th-5th c. I date the recension to the overlapping 2nd-4th c. span. The late Nepalese MS is a transmission witness, not the composition date.
note: cur_med 462 reflects only Vogel's late-closing extreme; consensus MSV-vinaya recension is earlier (2nd-4th c.), so median pulled down.

title: Bodhisattvaprātimokṣasūtra
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (early Yogācāra Mahāyāna milieu)
basis: Davidson, Pure realms and hidden abodes (textual sharing with Bodhisattvabhūmi); Asaṅga c. 315-390 (Apple, Nakamura)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Pure realms and hidden abodes.txt
recension_note: Mahāyāna ethics/prātimokṣa text (ed. Dutt 1931, IHQ VII). It shares textual material with Asaṅga's Bodhisattvabhūmi (c. 4th c.; Asaṅga c. 315-390) and incorporates sections of the Upāliparipṛcchā, placing the Sanskrit text squarely in the 4th-5th c. CE early-Yogācāra milieu rather than the 7th c. The Dutt edition is the only Sanskrit recension; its liturgical format later spawned the Chinese apocryphal Fanwang jing. Dating the extant Sanskrit, anchored by its dependence on the Bodhisattvabhūmi.
note: cur_med 638 (7th c.) far too late; text depends on 4th-c. Bodhisattvabhūmi and Upāliparipṛcchā. Corrected to 4th-5th c. CE (medium confidence).

title: Prātimokṣasūtra (Mahāsāṃghika — per edition; metadata's 'Mūlasarvāstivāda' is an error)
consensus_date: 1st c. BCE - 1st c. CE (final compilation of the Mahāsāṃghika Sanskrit recension)
basis: Prebish, Buddhist Monastic Discipline: The Sanskrit Prātimokṣa Sūtras of the Mahāsāṃghikas and Mūlasarvāstivādins, 1975/1996-2002
source_file: cleaned_Buddhist Monastic Discipline. Sanskrit Pratimoksa Sutras of Mahasamghikas and Mulasarvastivadins_Charles S. Prebish, 1996-2002.txt
recension_note: Edition is Pachow & Mishra's Mahāsāṃghika Prātimokṣa (GRETIL header confirms Mahāsāṃghika, contradicting the item's 'Mūlasarvāstivāda' label). Prebish dates the final compilation of the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādin Prakrit-cum-quasi-Sanskrit Prātimokṣa to 1st c. BCE-1st c. CE from the Mathurā-inscription language parallels. The surviving Mahāsāṃghika MSS (cf. Roth's Bhikṣuṇī-Vinaya) are 11th-12th c. copies, but the language fixes the recension to the turn of the era; dated to that recension.
note: Actually Mahāsāṃghika (Pachow ed.); recension compiled 1st c. BCE-1st c. CE per Prebish; cur_med 142 too late by ~140y.

title: Vinayasūtra [1: Pravrajyāvastu]
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (authored text)
basis: Brunnhölzl, Luminous Heart (2009); Crystal Mirror v.5 (1991); Davidson
source_file: cleaned_Luminous Heart. Third Karmapa on Consciousness, Wisdom, and Buddha Nature_Karl Brunnholzl, 2009.txt
recension_note: Single-author Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya digest (sūtra-style aphorisms) by Guṇaprabha, condensing the much older MSV Vinaya (esp. the Pravrajyāvastu ordination material). Date refers to Guṇaprabha's own composition, not the underlying Vinaya which is centuries older. Bapat-Gokhale 1982 ed. (Tibetan Sanskrit Works) prints the recovered Sanskrit sūtra text. Sources place Guṇaprabha 6th (Brunnhölzl) to 7th c. (Crystal Mirror).
note: Guṇaprabha 6th-7th c.; cur_med 612 within range

title: Śayanāsanavastu
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (extant MSV-vinaya, Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: E. Frauwallner, Earliest Vinaya (1956); G. Schopen, Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks (1997)
source_file: cleaned_Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks..Archaeology, Epigraphy and Texts of Monastic Buddhism in India_Gregory Schopen, 1997.txt
recension_note: DSBC re-encoding of Gnoli's Gilgit MSV-vinaya Śayanāsanavastu (same recension as vinv15_u). Old Mathurā core, but our text is the expanded MSV recension dated 4th-5th c.; Gilgit MS itself 6th-7th c. Date the redacted Sanskrit recension, not the early layer.
note: Duplicate of vinv15_u; 4th-5th c.; cur_med 331 within range.

title: Vajrasatvapārājikā [Vajrasattvapārājikā]
consensus_date: c. 10th-11th c. CE (late tantric/Nepalese recension, Ādikarmapradīpa corpus)
basis: O'Neill, Later Mahāyāna References to Book Worship; Bajracharya, Monasticism in Buddhism of Nepal-Mandala (2018) on Anupamavajra; LVP, Bouddhisme: études et matériaux (1898)
source_file: cleaned_O'Neill_Later Mahāyāna References to Book Worship.txt
recension_note: The Vajrasattvapārājikā is the short verse text on the tantric root downfalls (mūlāpatti) that La Vallée Poussin edited (1898) within his Ādikarmapradīpa volume; the Ādikarmapradīpa is by Anupamavajra (dated c. 1098 CE by Bajracharya; 10th-11th c. by O'Neill). The 14-root-downfalls doctrine for a vajra practitioner is firmly late Vajrayāna, so this text belongs to the late-tantric Nepalese ritual corpus, not the 8th c. — not an early sūtra/vinaya despite the 'pārājikā' label.
note: cur_med 719 far too early; late tantric root-vow text tied to Anupamavajra's Ādikarmapradīpa, c. 10th-11th c.

title: Saddharmapārājikā
consensus_date: None
basis: Nakamura (1980), incidental notice only
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Not an early canonical vinaya/sūtra. The Saddharmapārājikā is a late Nepalese (Newar) Buddhist adaptation of Brahmanical Dharmaśāstra (prāyaścitta/vrata material), known only from Nepalese MSS; the library offers no recension-specific date. Such Nepalese Pārājikā compositions are medieval (post-1000 CE) rather than ancient, but no scholar in the library fixes a year, so left null.
note: Late Nepalese Dharmaśāstra adaptation; no sourced date, left null (not corrected to a high/med date)

title: Śrīlokeśvarabhāṣitaparājikā
consensus_date: None
basis: no recension-specific scholarship located in library
recension_note: An obscure esoteric text 'spoken by Śrī Lokeśvara' (Avalokiteśvara), edited only on the DSBC (Dhadphale) with no printed critical edition. No scholar in the reference library discusses its date or recension. Thematically it belongs to the developed esoteric Avalokiteśvara cult (broadly 7th-8th c.), but with no sourced statement a date is not asserted; current median 605 is retained as the prior, not endorsed.
note: Nothing recension-specific in library; consensus_date null, confidence none.

title: Maṃjuśrīpārājikā [Mañjuśrīpārājikā]
consensus_date: post-12th c. CE (late-medieval Newar Vajrayāna ritual text)
basis: David Gellner, 'Introduction: Newar Buddhist ritualism' (in P. Williams ed., Buddhism: Critical Concepts vol. 6); text quoted within the Newar Jana Jīvan Kriyā Paddhati (Kathmandu 1962)
source_file: cleaned_Buddhism. Critical Concepts in Religious - Volume 6_Paul Williams.txt
recension_note: Not an early Indian sūtra/vinaya despite the 'pārājikā' in the title. The Mañjuśrīpārājikā is a Newar Buddhist tantric ritual text cited (alongside the Saṃvaratantra) in Newar Vajrayāna saṃskāra/life-cycle ritual manuals (jātakarma, śrāddha, etc.). The surviving Sanskrit/DSBC text belongs to the late-medieval-to-modern Nepalese (Newar) Vajrayāna tradition, which presupposes the Saṃvara/Yoginī tantras (post-10th c.) and the developed Newar ritual system. No library source dates this specific text; range reflects the Newar Vajrayāna ritual horizon.
note: Newar Vajrayāna lifecycle-ritual text, not an early vinaya; cur_med 563 far too early.

title: Lokeśvarapārājikā
consensus_date: Newar (Nepalese-tradition) composition, no scholarly anchor
basis: Locke, Karunamaya (1980) — text edited there; cult dating: Lévi/Locke-derived notes in library
source_file: cleaned_Szántó_Siddhas.txt
recension_note: Obscure Newar Buddhist text printed only in the appendix to John Locke's 1980 study of the Avalokiteśvara/Matsyendranātha (Karuṇāmaya = Bunga-dyo) cult of the Kathmandu Valley. The library has no scholarship dating this specific text; it belongs to the medieval/late-medieval Newar liturgical-legendary tradition of the Karuṇāmaya cult (the cult itself placed from c. 10th c. onward). Our DSBC Sanskrit text is the Newar-recension text, not an Indian original; no firm date can be assigned, so bounds are wide and confidence none.
note: Newar cult text; cur_med 747 is an unsupported guess but no sourced date available to correct it to a high/medium value

title: Upasampadājñapti
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (extant Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya Sanskrit recension)
basis: E. Frauwallner, Earliest Vinaya (1956); G. Schopen, Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks (1997)
source_file: cleaned_Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks..Archaeology, Epigraphy and Texts of Monastic Buddhism in India_Gregory Schopen, 1997.txt
recension_note: Ordination (upasampadā) formulary excerpted from the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya. Frauwallner traces an old Mathurā core, but the enormously expanded MSV-vinaya was known to the Mahāprajñāpāramitopadeśa (Chinese ~402-406 CE), and Schopen notes the surviving Sanskrit witnesses are no earlier than the 5th c. (Gilgit MSS later). Date the developed Sanskrit recension, not the early core.
note: MSV-vinaya ordination text; tightened to 4th-5th c.; cur_med 325 within 75y.

title: Bhikṣuṇīvinaya
consensus_date: 1st-5th c. CE (BHS Ārya-Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādin recension; composed in the period of, and resembling, the Mahāvastu)
basis: Nolot, Règles de discipline des nonnes bouddhistes (1991); edition: Roth, Bhikṣuṇī-Vinaya (1970)
source_file: cleaned_Regles de Discipline des Nonnes Bouddhistes_Edith Nolot, 1991.txt
recension_note: Same Roth (1970) Ārya-Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravādin recension as bhivin_u (duplicate work-item). Nolot dates the text by analogy with the Mahāvastu to the 1st-5th c. CE; sole manuscript palaeographically not later than 11th-12th c. Date is for the BHS Lokottaravādin recension, not the older pre-sectarian disciplinary core. Midpoint ~250 CE.
note: cur_med 62 at the early edge; Nolot's 1st-5th-c. span centers ~250 CE, >75y later. Duplicate of bhivin_u.

title: Prātimokṣasūtra
consensus_date: Sarvāstivāda canonical recension, fixed by the early centuries CE; c. 1st c. BCE - 2nd c. CE (Turfan Sanskrit witnesses are later copies)
basis: Frauwallner, The Earliest Vinaya (1956); Prebish, A Survey of Vinaya Literature (1994); von Simson's Turfan edition
source_file: cleaned_Earliest Vinaya and the Beginnings of Buddhist Literature_Frauwallner, 1956.txt
recension_note: Unlike the Mahāyāna sūtras here, the Prātimokṣa is a canonical Vinaya recitation text whose Sarvāstivāda recension was stabilized early (verse core near the second council per Frauwallner; the school's redaction by the early centuries CE). Von Simson 1986/2000 edits the Sarvāstivāda recension from the Turfan Sanskrit manuscripts (Central Asian copies, palaeographically 5th-8th c.), but these witness the much older fixed canonical recension. Date = the Sarvāstivāda recension, not the manuscript copies. (Title note 'Mūlasarvāstivāda' is loose; the edition is the Sarvāstivāda Prātimokṣa.)
note: cur_med ~128 lies within the defensible Sarvāstivāda-recension window; kept.

title: Prātimokṣasūtra (Mūlasarvāstivāda)
consensus_date: 6th c. CE (finalization of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension; Gilgit MS in Gupta script 5th-6th c.)
basis: Prebish, Buddhist Monastic Discipline: The Sanskrit Prātimokṣa Sūtras of the Mahāsāṃghikas and Mūlasarvāstivādins, 1975/1996-2002
source_file: cleaned_Buddhist Monastic Discipline. Sanskrit Pratimoksa Sutras of Mahasamghikas and Mulasarvastivadins_Charles S. Prebish, 1996-2002.txt
recension_note: Banerjee's edition (our text) is based on the Gilgit birch-bark manuscript, written in Gupta characters of the 5th-6th c. CE. Although the Prātimokṣa as a genre goes back to the early Sangha (the rule-list is far older), Prebish dates the FINALIZATION of this specific Mūlasarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension to the later end of AD 100-600, i.e. c. 6th c., because the school-name Mūlasarvāstivādin is not attested before the 7th c. and the manuscript itself is 5th-6th c. The earlier-layer rule list must NOT be used to date this recension early.
note: Gilgit Mūlasarvāstivāda recension finalized c. 6th c. CE per Prebish; cur_med 132 too early by ~400y.

title: Prātimokṣasūtra
consensus_date: ca. 6th c. CE (Gilgit Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension)
basis: Emms, 'Evidence for two Mūlasarvāstivādin Vinaya Traditions in the Gilgit Prātimokṣa-Sūtras' (2012), citing Wille and Banerjee; ed. Lokesh Chandra 1960 (Gilgit fragment)
source_file: cleaned_Evidence for two Mulasarvastivadin Vinaya Traditions in the Gilgit Pratimoksa-Sutras_Christopher Emms, 2012.txt
recension_note: The Prātimokṣa code as such is among the very oldest Buddhist texts, but our edition is Lokesh Chandra's GILGIT fragment, i.e. the Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension preserved in the Gilgit MSS (Gilgit/Bāmiyān Type II script, 6th-10th c.; Banerjee's MS 5th-6th c.). Emms dates the Gilgit prātimokṣa material to ~6th c. CE. Date this Gilgit Sanskrit recension, not the early disciplinary core.
note: Median 108 (range -200 to 300) dates the early Vinaya layer; the extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension is ~6th c. CE. Off by >75y, corrected with high confidence per Emms 2012.

title: Prātimokṣasūtra (Lokottaravāda-Mahāsāṅghika)
consensus_date: 1st c. BCE - 1st c. CE (final compilation of the Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda Sanskrit recension)
basis: Prebish, Buddhist Monastic Discipline: The Sanskrit Prātimokṣa Sūtras of the Mahāsāṃghikas and Mūlasarvāstivādins, 1975/1996-2002 (recension also in Tatia 1976)
source_file: cleaned_Buddhist Monastic Discipline. Sanskrit Pratimoksa Sutras of Mahasamghikas and Mulasarvastivadins_Charles S. Prebish, 1996-2002.txt
recension_note: Tatia's edition (our text) is the Lokottaravāda-Mahāsāṅghika Prātimokṣa, the same Prakrit-cum-quasi-Sanskrit recension Prebish treats; its final compilation is fixed to 1st c. BCE-1st c. CE by the Mathurā-inscription language parallels. Hirakawa notes the companion Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda manuscripts (Roth) are 11th-12th c. physical copies, but the recension's wording dates to the turn of the era. Dated to the recension, not the late manuscript.
note: Lokottaravāda-Mahāsāṅghika recension 1st c. BCE-1st c. CE per Prebish; MSS are 11th-12th c.; cur_med 143 too late by ~140y.

title: Upasaṃpadājñapti
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (extant Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya Sanskrit recension)
basis: E. Frauwallner, Earliest Vinaya (1956); G. Schopen, Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks (1997)
source_file: cleaned_Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks..Archaeology, Epigraphy and Texts of Monastic Buddhism in India_Gregory Schopen, 1997.txt
recension_note: Duplicate (DSBC/Chung-Jinananda) of the MSV-vinaya ordination formulary. Old Mathurā core (Frauwallner), but the expanded MSV-vinaya is attested only from ~early 5th c. (Mahāprajñāpāramitopadeśa, Chinese 402-406) and our Sanskrit MSS no earlier than the 5th c. (Schopen). Date the developed Sanskrit recension.
note: Same text as n2954u; 4th-5th c.; cur_med 326 within range.

title: Vinayasūtra
consensus_date: 7th c. CE
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980): Guṇaprabha later than Sthiramati
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: The Vinayasūtra is Guṇaprabha's original Mūlasarvāstivāda summa, edited from Tibetan-Sanskrit MSS (Sāṅkṛtyāyana 1981). Being an authored śāstra it has no earlier sūtra layer; its date is the author's floruit. Guṇaprabha is placed after Sthiramati (mid-6th c.), hence 7th c. CE. Current median 601 is at the early edge but within range.
note: Guṇaprabha post-Sthiramati = 7th c.; root sūtra (not commentary); current 601 acceptable.

title: Pravrajyāvastu (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
consensus_date: 3rd-5th c. CE (Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya redaction; extant Sanskrit on Turfan/Gilgit fragments)
basis: Bass, Meditation in an Indian Buddhist Monastic Code (2013); Schopen, Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks (1997)
source_file: cleaned_Meditation in an Indian Buddhist Monastic Code_Jeffrey W Bass, 2013_.txt
recension_note: The Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya as we have it is a large, late-redacted code; Schopen/Bass place its full redaction in the early-to-mid first millennium CE (commonly 3rd-5th c., Kuṣāṇa-Gupta). Our Pravrajyāvastu is reconstructed from Sanskrit Turfan fragments (Vogel & Wille) plus the Gilgit MSS; these witnesses are 5th-7th c., consistent with a redaction of the text c. 3rd-5th c. CE rather than any early canonical layer.
note: cur_med 311 sits inside the 3rd-5th-c. MSV redaction window; not flagged.

title: Poṣadhavastu (Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya compilation; ed. from Gilgit MSS)
basis: Lamotte, History of Indian Buddhism p. 657 (compilation of the MSV Vinaya in the 4th-5th c.), as cited in the library
source_file: cleaned_Divine Stories  Divyavadana, Part 2_Andy Rotman, 2017.txt
recension_note: The Poṣadhavastu is a chapter of the large Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya. Although some sections quote earlier (even pre-5th c.) material and the broad redaction may begin earlier, Lamotte dates the compilation of this fully-developed MSV Vinaya to the 4th-5th c.; Hu-von Hinüber's 1994 edition rests on the Gilgit manuscripts (6th-7th c. copies). Date here is the 4th-5th-c. recension represented by the Gilgit text, not the much older common Vinaya stock.
note: FLAG: cur_med 516 too late by >75y; Lamotte places MSV compilation 4th-5th c. (midpoint ~400). Corrected to 300-500.

title: Pravāraṇāvastu
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (extant Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya Sanskrit recension)
basis: E. Frauwallner, Earliest Vinaya (1956); G. Schopen, Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks (1997)
source_file: cleaned_Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks..Archaeology, Epigraphy and Texts of Monastic Buddhism in India_Gregory Schopen, 1997.txt
recension_note: The Pravāraṇā-vastu of the MSV-vinaya (Chung's edition). Has Sarvāstivādin/Pāli parallels for the rite's old core, but our text is the expanded MSV recension attested from ~early 5th c. (Mahāprajñāpāramitopadeśa) with Sanskrit witnesses no earlier than the 5th c. (Schopen). Date the developed Sanskrit recension, not the canonical prototype.
note: MSV-vinaya vastu; 4th-5th c.; cur_med 328 within range.

title: Carmavastu
consensus_date: 4th-6th c. CE (Gilgit MSS recension of the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya)
basis: Schopen, Buddhist Monks and Business Matters (2004)
source_file: cleaned_Buddhist Monks and Business Matters. Still More Papers on Monastic Buddhism in India (Ch.I-IV)_Gregory Schopen, 2004.txt
recension_note: Our text is the Carmavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinayavastu in Dutt's edition of the Gilgit MSS. Although the MSV contains much early material (Schopen, Bones Stones), its redaction is 'late', set by Schopen between the mature Kuṣāṇa period and the 5th-6th c. CE; the Gilgit codex itself was copied c. 6th-7th c. We date the extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension, not the early narrative layers.
note: Schopen 'early medieval' redaction; cur_med 397 within range

title: Bhaiṣajyavastu
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (Kuṣāṇa-period redaction of the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya; Gilgit Sanskrit MS itself 5th-6th c.)
basis: Schopen, Hierarchy and Housing... Śayanāsanavastu (2000); Cabezón (2017) summarizing Schopen's Encyclopedia of Buddhism entry
source_file: cleaned_Hierarchy and Housing in a Buddbist Monastic Code. Tr. of Sayanasanavastu of Mulasarvastivada-vinaya.  Part 1_Gregory Schopen, 2000.txt
recension_note: The Bhaiṣajyavastu is a vastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya. Schopen's consensus: the MSV was redacted in the Northwest (Gandhāra-Mathurā) in the Kuṣāṇa period, i.e. roughly 1st-3rd c. CE (Cabezón 2017: 'first or second century of the Common Era'), explicitly rejecting Lamotte's late 4th-5th c. dating. The MSV nonetheless contains material of varied date. Our edition is Dutt's Gilgit Manuscripts vol. III: the extant Gilgit Sanskrit MS is itself c. 5th-6th c., but it transmits the Kuṣāṇa-period redacted recension, which is what is dated here.
note: MSV redaction = Kuṣāṇa period (Schopen); Gilgit MS later. Current median 241 within Kuṣāṇa range; no flag.

title: Cīvaravastu
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (extant Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya recension, Gilgit MSS)

I anchor the date to the MSV literary redaction (4th-5th c.); the surviving Gilgit recension is later, not the early disciplinary layer.

title: Saṅghabhedavastu 1
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya redaction; extant Gilgit Sanskrit MS later still)
basis: Lamotte, History of Indian Buddhism, p.657 (via Shaw 2006); Cabezón, Sexuality in Classical South Asian Buddhism (2017), n.351
source_file: cleaned_Buddhist Goddesses of India_Mirenda Shaw, 2006.txt
recension_note: The Saṅghabhedavastu is the 17th and last section of the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya, edited by Gnoli from the Gilgit manuscript. Current opinion (Cabezón): the MSV was 'first redacted in the first or second century ... in northwest India, although the partial Sanskrit manuscript available to us is much later (fifth century).' Lamotte places the compilation in the 4th-5th c. Our text is precisely that extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension — not the 1st-2nd c. core — and the Gilgit codex itself is 6th-7th c. Dating to the redaction period, 4th-5th c.
note: Date the MSV redaction (Lamotte 4th-5th c.) / extant Gilgit Sanskrit, not the early core.

title: Saṅghabhedavastu 2
consensus_date: Kuṣāṇa period, ca. 1st–3rd c. CE (Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya redaction; extant Gilgit MS 6th–7th c.)
basis: Gregory Schopen, Hierarchy and Housing in a Buddhist Monastic Code (Sayanāsanavastu tr.), 2000
source_file: cleaned_Hierarchy and Housing in a Buddbist Monastic Code. Tr. of Sayanasanavastu of Mulasarvastivada-vinaya.  Part 1_Gregory Schopen, 2000.txt
recension_note: The Saṅghabhedavastu is Section 17 of the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya. Gnoli's edition rests on the Gilgit birch-bark MS, whose script dates the physical copy to the 6th–7th c.; but the text it transmits is the MSV recension whose redaction Schopen and the post-Lamotte consensus place in the Kuṣāṇa period (ca. 1st–3rd c. CE), in the Gandhāra–Mathurā Northwest. The MSV is itself the latest and most expansive of the surviving vinayas (drawn on by the Avadānaśataka and Divyāvadāna), so this is not an early layer but a developed Sanskrit recension; Lamotte's 4th–5th-c. dating is now rejected.
note: Kuṣāṇa-period MSV redaction; Gilgit MS later. cur_med 274 within range.

title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-2 [Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]

Kimura's edition rests on the Nepalese manuscript tradition, which Schopen notes is much later than the NW/Gilgit recensions; the developed large text itself is conventionally placed ca. 2nd–4th c. title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-3
consensus_date: c. 200-400 CE (largest/latest 'expansion-phase' PP; Nepalese MS recension)

recension_note: The Śatasāhasrikā (100,000-line) is the most maximally expanded of the large PP and the latest of those expansion-phase recensions, produced by spelling out the repetitive lists of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā. Kimura's edition is based on the late Nepalese Sanskrit manuscript tradition; the Sanskrit recension we read is therefore the developed/transmitted text, placed in the later part of the expansion phase (c. 200-400 CE), not the early Aṣṭa- core.

title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā I [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
consensus_date: ca. 4th–5th c. CE (revised 'Larger' recension aligned to the Abhisamayālaṃkāra; via late Nepalese MSS)
basis: Hajime Nakamura, Indian Buddhism, 1980 (Larger PP as expansion of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā); standard view of the AA-revised Pañcaviṃśati
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: The Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā (25,000-line) is a 'Larger' Prajñāpāramitā expansion of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā (original ca. 1st c. CE). Kimura's edition (I-1/I-2) gives the recension that was recast to follow the eight-padārtha structure of the Abhisamayālaṃkāra (4th–5th c.), and it survives through the late Nepalese manuscript tradition that Schopen flags as much later than the Gilgit forms. Thus our recension is the AA-revised, medieval-transmitted text, not the earlier base 25,000 nor the 1st-c. Aṣṭa layer. Library lacks a sharp recension date, hence low confidence.
note: cur_med 284 near the 3rd–4th-c. expansion; AA-revised recension slightly later, within tolerance.

title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā II-III [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
consensus_date: 4th–5th c. CE (revised Sanskrit recension structured per the Abhisamayālaṅkāra)
basis: Conze/Dutt (via Nakamura, Indian Buddhism 1980); Apple, Abhisamayālaṃkāra; Walser, Nāgārjuna in Context
source_file: cleaned_Apple_Abhisamayālaṃkāra.txt
recension_note: Kimura's edition (continuing Dutt 1934) is the REVISED recension of the 25,000-line PP — the version recast and re-divided to conform to the eight abhisamayas of the Abhisamayālaṅkāra (Conze titled the series 'The Large Sutra on Perfect Wisdom with the Division of the Abhisamayālaṃkāra'). The original (unrevised) Large PP existed by the 3rd c. (Chinese by Mokṣala 291 CE / Dharmarakṣa 286 CE), but this revised Sanskrit recension was reorganized AFTER the Abhisamayālaṅkāra (Maitreyanātha, ca. 350 CE) and is preserved in late MSS; hence it dates to the 4th–5th c. CE or later, NOT the 3rd-c. original.
note: cur_med 201 dates the original Large PP; Kimura's edition is the Abhisamaya-divided REVISED recension, post-350 CE. Corrected to 4th–5th c. CE.

Earlier unrevised Larger PP behind Dharmaraksa's 286 Chinese is far older, but is not our edited text.
note: Same as psp_5u; cur_med 172 dates the early layer, not our revised recension.

Makransky dates this redaction after Arya Vimuktisena (early 6th c.); the Sanskrit witness is the Gilgit-type MS tradition (6th-7th c.). The earlier UNREVISED Large PP behind the Chinese of Dharmaraksa (286)/Moksala (291)/Kumarajiva (404) is much older, but our edited text is the post-Vimuktisena revised recension.

title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
consensus_date: 6th-8th c. CE (revised/recast Sanskrit recension adjusted to the Abhisamayālaṅkāra)
basis: John J. Makransky, Buddhahood Embodied (1997), on the revised PP (rP); cf. Conze, Large Sutra; Sparham, Abhisamayālaṃkāra with Vṛtti and Āloka (2006) confirms Conze/Dutt edition is the recast version

Far later than the original unrevised PP layer (extant in earlier Chinese, e.g. Mokṣala 291 CE) that the current median of 221 reflects. Date the Dutt/rP recension.
note: Dutt = AA-recast recension; 6th-8th c. redaction, not the 2nd-3rd c. unrevised layer.

title: Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
consensus_date: 6th-8th c. CE (revised/recast Sanskrit recension adjusted to the Abhisamayālaṅkāra)
basis: John J. Makransky, Buddhahood Embodied (1997), pp. on the revised PP (rP); cf. Conze, Large Sutra; on Vimuktisena's date cf. Apple/Sparham (6th c.)
source_file: cleaned_Buddhahood Embodied. Sources of Controversy in India and Tibet (Abhisamayalankara)_John J Makransky, 1997.txt
recension_note: Dutt's 1934 Sanskrit edition is the 'revised' Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā (rP) — the recast version in which Abhisamayālaṅkāra topic-titles are inserted into the sūtra. It was redacted AFTER Ārya Vimuktisena (early 6th c. CE) and is ascribed (Tibetan colophon) to Haribhadra (fl. late 8th c.) as redactor. This is centuries later than the original unrevised 25,000-line PP layer (extant in earlier Chinese, e.g. Mokṣala 291 CE), which the current median of 212 reflects. Per the cardinal rule, we date the Dutt/rP recension, not the early layer.
note: Dutt = AA-recast recension; date the 6th-8th c. redaction, not the 2nd-3rd c. unrevised layer.
