title: Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā [Aṣṭasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
consensus_date: 1st c. BCE - 1st c. CE (prose core); transmitted via the Nepalese recension with Haribhadra's Āloka (late 8th c.)
basis: Conze (development of the PP), reported in Whitehead (1976); Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Sinicization of Buddhism. Study of Vimalakirtinirdesa Sutra and its  Interpretations in China from 3. through 6. Century_James Douglas Whitehead, 1976.txt
recension_note: The Aṣṭa is the earliest Mahāyāna sūtra; Conze places the prose expansion in the 1st c. BCE, and Lokakṣema's Chinese T224 (179 CE) is a terminus ante quem for an early version. Vaidya's edition is the NEPALESE Sanskrit recension printed together with Haribhadra's Āloka (late 8th c. CE); the Nepalese MSS preserve essentially the developed standard text but the manuscript transmission and commentarial frame are much later than the 1st-c.-BCE core. Date given is for the text the developed Nepalese recension carries.
note: Earliest Mahayana sutra; cur_med ~26 within Conze's core-layer range. Edition = late Nepalese recension + Haribhadra.

title: Daśasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā
consensus_date: c. 100-300 CE (large PP 'expansion phase')
basis: Westerhoff, Golden Age of Indian Buddhist Philosophy (2018), ch. on Prajñāpāramitā; Conze (via Nakamura 1980)
source_file: cleaned_Golden Age of Indian Buddhist Philosophy in the First Millennium CE_Jan Westerhoff, 2018.txt
recension_note: The Daśasāhasrikā (10,000-line) PP is one of the expanded 'large' Prajñāpāramitā recensions derived from the Aṣṭasāhasrikā/Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā, belonging to the expansion phase (c. 100-300 CE), later than the early Aṣṭa- core (~100 BCE-100 CE). Konow's edition restores only the first two chapters from the Khotanese and Tibetan witnesses, so it reflects the Central-Asian transmitted recension rather than an independent early Sanskrit.
note: Expansion-phase large PP, c. 100-300 CE; current median ~271 within range.

title: Ratnaguṇasaṃcayagāthā
consensus_date: late 8th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension: Haribhadra's reordering); core verses ~100 BCE
basis: Buswell & Lopez, Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism (2014), s.v. Ratnaguṇasaṃcayagāthā
source_file: cleaned_Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism_Buswell & Lopez 2014_.txt
recension_note: CARDINAL CASE: the first verses may go back to ~100 BCE (Conze, Deleanu) and a Chinese translation exists from 991 (and verse-core much earlier), but the ONLY extant Sanskrit recension is Haribhadra's late-8th-c. reordering to fit the Abhisamayālaṃkāra. Vaidya's Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha text (= Yuyama/Obermiller's Nepalese-tradition edition) reproduces exactly this Haribhadra-recension. Date the recension to c. 750-800 CE, NOT the ~100 BCE verse-core.
note: cur_med 316 off by >400y: our Sanskrit text is Haribhadra's late-8th-c. recension, not the BCE verse-core

title: Suvikrāntavikrāmiparipṛcchā [Sārdhadvisāhasrikā prajñāpāramitā]
consensus_date: 4th c. CE (later 'condensed/restatement' phase of Prajñāpāramitā; extant Sanskrit)
basis: Hikata, ed. Suvikrāntavikrāmi-paripṛcchā (1958, Introd.); framework Winternitz, Hist. Indian Lit. II (1933); Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_History of Indian Literature II_MAURICE WINTERNITZ, 1933.txt
recension_note: A 2500-line (Sārdhadvisāhasrikā) text belonging to the later condensed/restated class of Prajñāpāramitā, not the base Aṣṭa (~1st c.) nor the large expansions (2nd-3rd c.). Hikata's edition/study treats it as a comparatively late, derivative PP work; the Vaidya text reprints that Sanskrit. Dated later than the Aṣṭa and the large texts, hence c. 4th c. CE.
note: cur_med 285 (range 100-400) within tolerance of a 4th-c. estimate; left unflagged but text is on the late side of that band.

title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
consensus_date: Nepalese recension, ca. 11th–17th c. MSS (text-form much later than the 6th–7th-c. Gilgit version; original ca. 150–200 CE)
basis: Gregory Schopen, The Manuscript of the Vajracchedikā Found at Gilgit (on the lateness of the Nepalese MS tradition); Nakamura 1980 for the original
source_file: cleaned_Schopen_Manuscript of the Vajracchedika.txt
recension_note: Vaidya's edition (in the Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha) reproduces essentially Max Müller's text, which rests on the late, Pāṇini-normalised Nepalese manuscript tradition that Schopen explicitly contrasts with, and dates much later than, the 6th–7th-c. Gilgit recension. The original Diamond Sūtra is ca. 150–200 CE (Nakamura/Shizutani), but the recension our edition transmits is the medieval Nepalese text-form (surviving MSS roughly 11th c. onward); the library has no sharper date for this specific recension, hence low confidence.
note: Nepalese recension is medieval, far later than cur_med 277 (original layer).

title: Saptaśatikā prajñāpāramitā
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Chinese trans. Mandrasena/Saṅghapāla early 6th c. (T232)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: The Mañjuśrī-prajñāpāramitā (Saptaśatikā) is one of the latest of the smaller PP sūtras (Conze 6th c.); first Chinese versions by Mandrasena (c.503) and Saṅghapāla (c.506-520) give a terminus ante quem for some version. Vaidya's edition rests on later Nepalese MSS, so the extant Sanskrit recension is best placed 5th-6th c., later than the cur_med 397.
note: Late smaller-PP text; Chinese trans early 6th c.; cur_med 403 too early

title: Prajñāpāramitāhṛdayasūtra
consensus_date: early 8th c. CE (extended/long Sanskrit recension, vistaramātṛkā)
basis: Jayarava Attwood, 'Preliminary Notes on the Extended Heart Sutra in Chinese'; Jan Nattier, 'The Heart Sūtra: A Chinese Apocryphal Text?' (1992)
source_file: cleaned_Attwood_Preliminary Notes on the Extended Heart Sutra in Chinese.txt
recension_note: Vaidya's 'vistaramātṛkā' = the EXTENDED/long recension preserved in Nepalese Sanskrit MSS. Nattier shows the short Heart Sutra was back-translated from Chinese into Sanskrit ~7th c.; Attwood shows the text was extended twice in the early 8th c., the second (Sanskrit-composed) extension being the one underlying the Nepalese long version we edit. Our recension is therefore early-8th-c. Sanskrit, NOT the 3rd-5th c. implied by the current median.
note: cur_med 339 off by ~370 yr: that is the Large Sūtra's age, not the extended Heart Sūtra recension. Corrected to early 8th c.

title: Pañcaśatikāprajñāpāramitā [Pañcaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
consensus_date: None
basis: no recension-specific scholarship located in library (Li & Fujita 2016 ed. not held)
recension_note: Short ('500-line') Prajñāpāramitā sūtra; our edition is Li Xuezhu & Fujita Yoshimichi 2016 (Sanskrit + Tibetan). The reference library holds material only on the distinct Tantric Prajñāpāramitā-naya-śatapañcaśatikā (Adhyardhaśatikā, the Rishukyō), NOT on this short Prajñāpāramitā. No recension-specific date could be sourced; retained the existing wide 100-600 CE band rather than inventing a date.
note: No recension-specific evidence in library; band left wide, confidence none.

title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā
consensus_date: Gilgit Sanskrit recension, c. 6th-7th c. CE (composition c. 4th c.; Chinese tr. 401 CE)
basis: Westerhoff (2018) PP 'contraction phase' 300-500 CE; Schopen, The Manuscript of the Vajracchedikā Found at Gilgit (Gómez & Silk eds. 1989)
source_file: cleaned_Golden Age of Indian Buddhist Philosophy in the First Millennium CE_Jan Westerhoff, 2018.txt
recension_note: Composition of the condensed Vajracchedikā is placed in the PP 'contraction phase' (Westerhoff 300-500 CE; Conze 4th c.), and Kumārajīva's Chinese translation (401 CE) caps the original. But OUR edition is Schopen's edition of the actual Gilgit manuscript, part of the Gilgit deposit dated c. 6th-7th c. CE; the Gilgit text differs verbally from Conze's Nepalese text. Dating reflects this Gilgit recension/manuscript, hence later than the ~4th c. composition.
note: Edition is the Gilgit MS (Schopen); recension 6th-7th c., later than ~4th c. composition / 401 CE Chinese tr.

title: Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā [Vajracchedikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra]
consensus_date: 5th–6th c. CE (extant Gilgit/Bāmiyān Sanskrit recension; original text ca. 150–200 CE)
basis: Gregory Schopen, The Manuscript of the Vajracchedikā Found at Gilgit; Schøyen catalogue (Harrison & Watanabe ed.); Nakamura 1980 for the original
source_file: cleaned_Schopen_Manuscript of the Vajracchedika.txt
recension_note: This item is Harrison & Watanabe's edition of the Schøyen Collection Vajracchedikā MS, written in Gilgit/Bāmiyān Type I script (per the Schøyen exhibition catalogue), i.e. a NW Sanskrit recension of ca. 5th–6th c. The original Diamond Sūtra text is far older — Nakamura/Shizutani place its origin ca. 150–200 CE — but our edited recension is this early-medieval Gandhāran/Bāmiyān prose form, distinct again from the much later Nepalese recension (Müller/Vaidya). Dated here to the extant Schøyen recension, not the 2nd-c. original layer.
note: cur_med 276 = original layer; extant Schøyen Gilgit/Bāmiyān-script recension is 5th–6th c.

title: Triśatikāyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ kārikāsaptatiḥ (Vajracchedikā-śāstra-kārikā)
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Asaṅga's floruit)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Tucci, Minor Buddhist Texts I (1956, the edition)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Asaṅga's 70 kārikās summarizing the Vajracchedikā-prajñāpāramitā, edited by Tucci from the Sanskrit. This is the authored śāstra-verse (a commentary on the older Vajracchedikā), not the root sūtra; dating follows Asaṅga himself (c. 310-390), distinct from the much older Vajracchedikā it expounds.
note: Asaṅga's kārikās; cur_med 369 fine; no change.

title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
consensus_date: extant Sanskrit (Nepalese) recension, several centuries after the 2nd-3rd c. CE original; c. 4th-7th c. CE or later
basis: Nattier, 'The Indian Roots of Pure Land Buddhism' (Pacific World); Gómez, Land of Bliss (1996)
source_file: cleaned_Nattier_The Indian Roots of Pure Land Buddhism.txt
recension_note: This is the LARGER Sukhāvatīvyūha (Vaidya 1961). Earliest Chinese (Dai amida kyō) is late 2nd c. CE and Zhi Qian's mid-3rd c.; the most-used Chinese version (Buddhabhadra/Baoyun) is early 5th c. Gómez: composed NW India 'at least seventeen hundred years ago.' Our Sanskrit text descends from the late Nepalese recension (Müller-Nanjio basis); Nattier notes the surviving Sanskrit postdates the Chinese by several centuries. Date is the extant Sanskrit recension, not the early original.
note: cur_med ~123 dates the original; our edition is the later Nepalese Sanskrit recension.

title: Fragments of Prajñāpāramitā texts
consensus_date: c. 5th-8th c. CE (paleographic date of the Sanskrit MS fragments)
basis: manuscript paleography (Gilgit/Central Asian finds); Hinüber via Apple; Williams
source_file: cleaned_Apple_Candrakīrti and the Lotus sutra.txt
recension_note: Not a single work but Klaus Wille's compilation of physical Sanskrit MS fragments from Gilgit and Eastern Turkestan (Konow 1942, Watanabe 1912, Bidyabinod 1927, Karashima 2004, etc.). The PP literature was composed far earlier (Gāndhārī Aṣṭasāhasrikā c. 1st c. CE), but these fragments are dated by the age of the manuscripts themselves, paleographically c. 5th-8th c. CE — the period of the Gilgit/Central Asian birch-bark and palm-leaf finds. Date is of the surviving witnesses, not the PP composition.
note: MS-fragment compilation; dated by paleography, not composition; cur_med 613 within range

title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā]
consensus_date: c. 1st-2nd c. CE (earliest recension; terminus ante quem 286 CE)
basis: Stefano Zacchetti, In Praise of the Light (2005) — the editorial premise of this very text
source_file: cleaned_Nattier_The Names of Amitābha:Amitāyus in Early Chinese Buddhist Translations_1.txt
recension_note: This is the deliberate EXCEPTION: Zacchetti's edition is a synoptic edition explicitly keyed to Dharmaraksa's Guang zan jing (286 CE), the EARLIEST Chinese translation of the Larger PP. The Sanskrit it presents corresponds to that earliest recoverable recension (1st-2nd c. CE), with 286 CE as terminus ante quem — deliberately NOT the later revised (Kimura/Gilgit) recension. Hence dated early, unlike the other PPP items in this batch.
note: cur_med 188 fits the early recension Zacchetti's edition targets; ante 286 CE.

title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā [Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā]
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (Gilgit manuscript witness of the Larger Prajñāpāramitā)
basis: von Hinüber (2014) and standard palaeography of the Gilgit MSS, via Apple, Williams, Gómez & Silk
source_file: cleaned_Apple_Candrakīrti and the Lotus sutra.txt
recension_note: Choong's edition is based directly on the Gilgit Large-PP manuscript (fol. 202r-205r, GBM 571-577). The text itself is a developed Larger PP, but the recension we are editing is fixed by the physical Gilgit witness, dated palaeographically to the 6th-7th c. (Hinüber: 7th-8th; most scholars 6th-7th, 'Gilgit/Bamiyan type I'). Far later than the 1st-2nd c. composition layer of the Larger PP.
note: cur_med 182 dates the textual prototype; our edited witness is the 6th-7th c. Gilgit MS.

title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
consensus_date: extant Sanskrit (Nepalese) recension, several centuries after the 2nd-3rd c. CE original; c. 4th-7th c. CE or later
basis: Nattier, 'The Indian Roots of Pure Land Buddhism' (Pacific World); Gómez, Land of Bliss (1996)
source_file: cleaned_Nattier_The Indian Roots of Pure Land Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Larger Sukhāvatīvyūha in the Müller-Nanjio (1883) edition, based on late Nepalese manuscripts. Original c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (early Chinese 2nd-3rd c.); the surviving Sanskrit is the later medieval Nepalese recension, postdating the Chinese by several centuries (Nattier). Date is the extant Sanskrit recension, not the original.
note: cur_med ~128 dates the original; edition rests on late Nepalese Sanskrit MSS.

title: Kāśyapaparivartasūtra [Kāśyapaparivarta]
consensus_date: 9th c. CE (extant Central Asian Sanskrit manuscript edited by Staël-Holstein); text composed 3rd-5th c. CE
basis: Pagel, The Bodhisattvapiṭaka (1995); Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Bodhisattvapitaka_Ulrich Pagel, 1995_OCR.txt
recension_note: The KP was first translated into Chinese by Lokakṣema (late 2nd c.), and the text was composed 3rd-5th c. CE. But our editions (Staël-Holstein 1926 and Vorobyova-Desyatovskaya et al. 2002) both edit the single surviving Sanskrit witness, a NINTH-century Central Asian (Khotan) manuscript. Nakamura: 'The Sanskrit edition, published by Stael-Holstein, came into being later.' Date is that 9th-c. recension, not the early original.
note: cur_med ~114 dates the lost early text; our edited Sanskrit MS is 9th c. (Pagel).

title: Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchā [Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchāsūtra]
consensus_date: late 6th c. CE (extant Sanskrit/Nepalese recension; final form)
basis: Daniel Boucher, Bodhisattvas of the Forest and the Formation of the Mahayana (2008)
source_file: cleaned_Bodhisattvas of the Forest and the Formation of the Mahayana. Rastrapalapariprccha-sutra_Daniel Boucher, 2008.txt
recension_note: Our edition (Vaidya, reproducing Finot's Sanskrit, based on Nepalese MSS) reflects the FINAL form of the text. Boucher shows Dharmaraksa's Chinese (270 CE) translates a much earlier, shorter version; the text was progressively fleshed out through the Gupta period and 'largely coincides in structure and content' with Jnanagupta's late-6th-c. Chinese (585-592) and the Nepalese Sanskrit MS tradition. Date OUR Sanskrit = the late-6th-c. final recension, NOT the 3rd-c. layer behind Dharmaraksa.
note: cur_med 166 dates the lost early layer; extant Sanskrit is ~4 centuries later.

title: Vinayaviniścaya Upāliparipṛcchā [Vinayaviniścaya upāliparipṛcchā sūtra]
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (early Mahāyāna; presupposed by the Bodhisattvabhūmi)
basis: Pagel, The Bodhisattvapiṭaka (1995); Silk, Origins and Early History of the Mahāratnakūṭa (1994)
source_file: cleaned_Bodhisattvapitaka_Ulrich Pagel, 1995_OCR.txt
recension_note: The Mahāyāna Vinayaviniścaya-Upāliparipṛcchā (= Bodhisattva-prātimokṣa-sūtra), a Mahāratnakūṭa-tradition text containing the 35-buddha confession (shared with the Triskandha). It is presupposed by the Bodhisattvabhūmi (early Yogācāra, ~3rd-early 4th c.) and quoted by Candrakīrti (7th c.) and Śāntideva's Śikṣāsamuccaya (8th c.), so composed by ~2nd-3rd c. CE. NOT to be confused with the separate Sthaviravāda Upāliparipṛcchā (a Parivāra parallel known only in Chinese, discussed by von Hinüber/Stache-Rosen). Our edition is Dutt's fragmentary Bodhisattva Prātimokṣa Sūtra (1931, Nepalese-tradition Sanskrit) — a later witness of this early Mahāyāna recension.
note: Mahāyāna Upāliparipṛcchā, presupposed by Bodhisattvabhūmi; ~2nd-3rd c. composition; current median 249 within range, no flag.

title: Guṇakāraṇḍavyūhasūtra
consensus_date: early-mid 15th c. CE (Nepalese Sanskrit composition; the only extant recension)
basis: Tuladhar-Douglas, Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal (2006), ch. 2 'Form, genre and dating'; agreeing with von Rospatt
source_file: cleaned_Tuladhar-Douglas_Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal.txt
recension_note: The GKV is itself a verse 'Garland' (mālā) recension/expansion of the much older prose Kāraṇḍavyūha (4th-5th c. CE). It is NOT that early text: it is an original Nepalese Vajrayāna composition written in the Kathmandu Valley, 'among the last Buddhist sūtras to be written in Sanskrit,' by 15th-c. Newar priests, with evidence (a dated MS) placing it some decades before 1493. Lokesh Chandra's 1999 edition prints this single, stable Nepalese recension. The earlier Kāraṇḍavyūha is its source, not its date.
note: Current median 1366 is ~74y below the consensus midpoint (~1440), just under the 75y threshold; range 1300-1500 already brackets the correct date.

title: Kāśyapaparivartasūtra [Kāśyapaparivarta]
consensus_date: 9th c. CE (extant Central Asian Sanskrit manuscript, Vorobyova-Desyatovskaya facsimile ed.); text composed 3rd-5th c. CE
basis: Pagel, The Bodhisattvapiṭaka (1995); Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Bodhisattvapitaka_Ulrich Pagel, 1995_OCR.txt
recension_note: Vorobyova-Desyatovskaya, Karashima & Kudo (2002) is the romanized/facsimile edition of the SAME surviving Sanskrit witness Staël-Holstein used: a ninth-century Central Asian (Khotan) manuscript. Lokakṣema's Chinese (late 2nd c.) and composition 3rd-5th c. CE concern the original; our edited Sanskrit recension is 9th c.
note: cur_med ~119 dates the lost early text; our Sanskrit MS is 9th c.

title: Daśabhūmikasūtra
consensus_date: c. 3rd c. CE (extant Sanskrit/Nepalese recension; terminus ante quem 297 CE)
basis: Williams, Mahayana Buddhism (2009); Vetter via Kragh (ed.), Foundation for Yoga Practitioners (2013)
source_file: cleaned_Foundation for Yoga Practitioners. Yogacarabhumi and Its Adaptation in India, East Asia, and Tibet_Ulrich Timme Kragh, ed., 2013.txt
recension_note: Vaidya's edition reproduces the Nepalese Sanskrit recension (Rahder/Kondo, Dasabhumisvara, as incorporated into the Avatamsaka). First Chinese translation by Dharmaraksa in 297 CE gives a firm terminus ante quem for this developed form. Japanese scholarship posits a 1st-2nd c. early layer, but Western scholars (Vetter) hold the text 'as we have it today' is NOT an early Mahayana text; the extant Sanskrit is the 3rd-c. developed recension.
note: cur_med 187 within the late-2nd/3rd-c. range for the extant recension; terminus 297 CE.

title: Gaṇḍavyūhasūtra
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (extant expanded Sanskrit recension; Nepalese MSS)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Buddhabhadra's Avataṃsaka tr. 418-420 CE (Hamar, Reflecting Mirrors, 2007)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: A pre-Nāgārjuna core exists, and the work entered the Avataṃsaka rendered by Buddhabhadra 418-420 CE (terminus ante quem for a version). But our standalone Sanskrit (Vaidya, after Suzuki-Idzumi, on Nepalese MSS) is the developed recension: Khotanese 'ysa' in the Arapacana, Maitreya as the 52nd kalyāṇamitra, the Bhadracarī appended. We date that 4th-5th-c. expanded Sanskrit text, not the early core.
note: cur_med 289 reflects an early-core date; the extant expanded Sanskrit recension is 4th-5th c. (mid ~400), off by >75y.

title: Adbhutadharmaparyāya [Adbhutadharmaparyāyasūtra]
consensus_date: ca. 5th-6th c. CE (Gilgit Sanskrit recension; Bentor's 3 Gilgit MSS)
basis: Bentor, 'The Redactions of the Adbhutadharmaparyāya from Gilgit', JIABS 11.2 (1988); Schopen, Bones, Stones (1997)
source_file: cleaned_Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks..Archaeology, Epigraphy and Texts of Monastic Buddhism in India_Gregory Schopen, 1997.txt
recension_note: Our edition is Bentor's, made from three Gilgit MSS (the title of her article is literally 'The Redactions ... from Gilgit'). This short stūpa-cult merit sūtra survives in the Gilgit recension; the Gilgit MSS are written in Gilgit/Bāmiyān script datable to the 5th-7th c. CE. Date this Gilgit Sanskrit recension; any earlier oral/Middle-Indic version is not what the edition represents.
note: Median 108 (range -50 to 220) is far too early for the extant Gilgit recension (5th-6th c. CE). Off by >75y.

title: Ahorātravratacaityasevānuśaṃsāvadāna
consensus_date: late-medieval Nepalese Sanskrit avadāna (12th-15th c. CE recension)
basis: Handurukande 2000 (ed.); Bronkhorst, Buddhism in the Shadow of Brahmanism 2011
source_file: cleaned_Buddhism in the Shadow of Brahmanism_Johannes Bronkhorst, 2011.txt
recension_note: A caitya-worship avadāna of the type collected and transmitted in the late Nepalese Buddhist avadāna literature; its fully-developed four-varṇa ritual stipulations (Bronkhorst) point to a late composition. Such Nepalese caitya-cult avadānas are late-medieval (post-12th-c.) productions, far later than the cur_med 689 implies. Library gives no firm century; confidence low but the recension is clearly late-medieval Nepalese, not 7th c.
note: Late Nepalese caitya-worship avadāna; cur_med ~689 far too early. Low confidence on exact century so NOT counted as a corrected high/medium date.

title: Anityatāsūtra [Āryānityatāsūtra]
consensus_date: Mūlasarvāstivāda recension, extant Sanskrit by c. 6th-7th c. CE
basis: Silk, Managing Monks (2008), citing Yamada; ed. Yamada 1972
source_file: cleaned_Managing Monks. Administrators and Administrative Roles in Indian Buddhist Monasticism_Jonathan A. Silk, 2008.txt
recension_note: Short canonical sūtra (the 'Tri-skandhaka' impermanence recitation) transmitted within the Mūlasarvāstivāda and quoted in the MSV Vinayasaṃgraha. Yamada's 1972 ed. is of the extant Sanskrit. The sūtra's content is old āgama material, but the Sanskrit MSV recension we edit belongs to the same milieu as the developed Mūlasarvāstivāda corpus (Gilgit-era Sanskrit, c. 6th-7th c.). Library has no edition-specific date, so bounds are wide; confidence low.
note: MSV sūtra; old content, Sanskrit recension Gilgit-era; cur_med 617 within wide range

title: Arthaviniścayasūtra + Nibandhana
consensus_date: late 8th c. CE (Nibandhana commentary, which our Samtani edition bundles with the sūtra)
basis: N. H. Samtani, ed., The Arthaviniścaya-sūtra and its Commentary (Nibandhana), 1971 (introduction)
source_file: cleaned_Arthaviniscaya-Sutra & its Commentary ( Nibandhana )_N. H. Samtani, 1971.txt
recension_note: The Arthaviniścayasūtra itself is an early mainstream (śrāvakayāna) doctrinal compendium, older than its commentary. But OUR edition is Samtani's combined sūtra-plus-Nibandhana, and the Nibandhana was composed by Bhikṣu Vīryaśrīdatta at Nālandā under Pāla king Dharmapāla (r. c. 775-810 CE), i.e. late 8th / very early 9th c. The edition is therefore anchored to this 8th-c. commentarial recension, far later than the root sūtra's earliest layer.
note: Edition includes 8th-c. Nibandhana by Vīryaśrīdatta of Nālandā; cur_med 543 ~230y too early.

title: Avadānaśataka
consensus_date: 5th–7th c. CE (well-developed extant Sanskrit recension; Nepalese MSS behind Speyer's ed. fixed ca. 8th–9th c.)
basis: monograph on the Avadānaśataka summarising Mitsuyo Demoto (1999 diss.), 21st c.
source_file: cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
recension_note: Speyer's (and Vaidya's) edition rests on Nepalese manuscripts, which transmit the fully expanded recension that, per Demoto, was revised by the end of the 8th c. and fixed by the early 9th c. (when the largely concordant Tibetan translation was made). The Sanskrit is markedly more developed than the Chinese Zhuanji baiyuan jing (no longer securely Zhi Qian; Demoto: mid-5th–late-6th c.) and even fuller than the 8th-c. Turfan fragments. The 2nd–5th-c. 'initial redaction' is only the earliest layer; our extant Sanskrit recension is the well-developed NW version of the 5th–7th c. carried into the medieval Nepalese MSS — hence dated here to the 5th–7th c.
note: cur_med 275 (early layer) too early for extant developed Sanskrit recension; raised to 5th–7th c.

title: Āyuḥparyantasūtra
consensus_date: 6th–7th c. CE (extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Matsumura 1989 (Gilgit MS edition); Gilgit MS dating per Williams/Walser/Nattier
source_file: cleaned_Analayo_The Dawn of Abhidharma.txt
recension_note: Our Sanskrit text is the (Mūla-)Sarvāstivāda recension preserved in the Gilgit manuscript (ed. Matsumura 1989, supplemented by Central Asian/Turfan fragments), physically datable c. 6th–7th c. CE. The doctrinal content — graded lifespans in the cosmological realms — is old canonical āgama material with Pāli and early-Chinese parallels centuries earlier; per the cardinal rule the date here is of the EXTANT Gilgit Sanskrit recension, not of that early layer.
note: Gilgit recension 6th–7th c.; cur_med 608 coincidentally within range. Content far older.

title: Āryānityatāsūtra [Anityatāsūtra]
consensus_date: Mūlasarvāstivāda redaction horizon, by c. 700 CE (TAQ Yijing); extant Sanskrit later
basis: Silk, Managing Monks (2008), on the Tridaṇḍaka-Anityatāsūtra and its MSV affiliation; Lamotte (MSV dating) via Schopen
source_file: cleaned_Silk_Managing Monks.txt
recension_note: Short funeral/impermanence sūtra of the Tridaṇḍaka recited in Mūlasarvāstivāda practice; it is embedded in / quoted by the MSV, so its redaction belongs to the MSV horizon (Lamotte: 4th-5th c.). A 'Sūtra on Impermanence' (無常經) was translated by Yijing (701 CE), giving a TAQ for a version. Our extant Sanskrit (Yamada 1972, from RAS London & Tokyo Univ. MSS) is a later-transmitted witness of this MSV liturgical sūtra, not the earliest canonical layer.
note: MSV-affiliated liturgical sūtra; redaction 4th-5th c., TAQ Yijing 701. cur_med 657 within plausible MSV-transmission range; low confidence, broad bounds.

title: Arthaviniścayasūtra
consensus_date: 3rd-5th c. CE (digest of Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda āgama categories)
basis: Samtani, Arthaviniścaya-sūtra & Commentary (1971); cited in Cox, Disputed Dharmas (1995)
source_file: cleaned_Disputed Dharmas. Early Buddhist Theories on Existence…Sanghabhadra's Nyayanusara_Collett Cox, 1995.txt
recension_note: A non-Mahāyāna doctrinal digest (mātṛkā) drawing its formulas from the Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda āgamas and combining Nikāya and Sarvāstivāda phrasings (van den Broeck). Quoted by 5th-c. authors; compiled in the early centuries CE. Vaidya's edition (Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha) reproduces this single short text; library offers no sharp recension date, so confidence is low.
note: Sarvāstivāda āgama digest; cur_med 407 within broad range; weak evidence

title: Ajitasenavyākaraṇa
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: von Hinüber (1986/7) on the Gilgit colophons, via Neelis 2011; text identified as a Gilgit-only Mahāyāna sūtra (Schopen 1997; Nakamura 1980)
source_file: cleaned_Neelis_Long-distance Trade and the Transmission of Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Our edition is N. Dutt's Gilgit Manuscripts, i.e. the Gilgit codex itself (Schopen cites GM i, 129; the sūtra survives ONLY in the Gilgit corpus, with a colophon among the dated Gilgit texts noted by Hinüber 1980). The text was probably composed earlier in Kashmir (Nakamura: 'This work seems to have been composed in Kashmir'), but the dated Sanskrit witness we edit is the Gilgit manuscript, written in the 6th-7th c. CE; date the codex, not the lost earlier composition.
note: Gilgit-only sutra; date the 6th-7th c. Gilgit MS our edition reproduces, not the earlier Kashmir composition. Median 500 too early.

title: Āryabuddhabalādhānaprātihāryavikurvāṇanirdeśanāma Mahāyānasūtra
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: von Hinüber 2014b / Apple (Candrakīrti and the Lotus sūtra); Schopen 1997 (Bones, Stones)
source_file: cleaned_Apple_Candrakīrti and the Lotus sutra.txt
recension_note: Our edition (Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts IV) is the Gilgit recension; Schopen edited 'The Five Leaves of the Buddhabalādhāna...sūtra'. The work is an early-to-middle Mahāyāna sūtra whose composition layer is older, but the EXTANT Sanskrit witness is the Gilgit MS, palaeographically dated 7th-8th c. We date that recension, not the Ur-text.
note: Gilgit MS recension; cur_med within range.

title: Maitreyavyākaraṇa [Maitreyavyākaraṇasūtra]
consensus_date: Gilgit Sanskrit recension, ca. 6th-7th c. CE MSS (text composed earlier, by ~3rd-4th c.)
basis: Lamotte, History of Indian Buddhism (1988/1958); Gilgit MS dating per Schopen/von Hinüber
source_file: cleaned_History of Indian Buddhism_Etienne Lamotte, 1988 (1958).txt
recension_note: The Maitreya-prophecy text exists in many near-identical versions; earliest Chinese renderings go back to Dharmarakṣa (ca. 303 CE) and Kumārajīva (ca. 402 CE), giving an early terminus for SOME version. This item (Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts IV) prints the GILGIT Sanskrit recension, whose manuscripts are dated ca. 6th-7th c. CE (von Hinüber/Schopen). Date assigned to that extant Gilgit recension, not to the ~3rd-4th c. ur-prophecy underlying the early Chinese.
note: Gilgit Skt recension (6th-7th c. MSS) is much later than median 364 (off by >75y); corrected upward.

title: Bhavasaṅkrāntisūtra
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (early Mahāyāna sūtra; Sanskrit a modern restoration)
basis: Inada, Nāgārjuna (1993) on the restored Sanskrit; Nakamura (1980)
source_file: cleaned_NAGARJUNA. Translation of his Mulamadhyamakakarika with an Introductory Essay_Kenneth K. Inada, 1993.txt
recension_note: Short Ārya-Bhavasaṅkrānti-nāma-mahāyānasūtra, doctrinally tied to Nāgārjuna's circle (its companion Bhavasaṅkrāntiśāstra is attributed to Nāgārjuna). No extant Sanskrit MS: Aiyaswami Sastri's (1938) 'Sanskrit' is a retroversion from the Tibetan/Chinese. Date is for the original early-Mahāyāna composition (2nd-3rd c.); the printed Sanskrit is a modern reconstruction, not a surviving recension.
note: cur_med 261 within early-Mahāyāna window; Sanskrit is reconstructed, hence low confidence.

title: Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka(sūtra) [Sarvajñatākāradhāraṇī]
consensus_date: late 4th-5th c. CE (extant Sanskrit; Nepalese MSS)
basis: Yamada, ed. Karuṇāpuṇḍarīka / Mahākaruṇāpuṇḍarīka Sūtra (1968); cf. Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Yamada's edition rests on Nepalese Sanskrit MSS of the developed sūtra. Its earliest Chinese rendering (the Dabei jing tradition, assoc. with Dharmakṣema, c. 419 CE) gives a terminus ante quem for SOME version; the polished Sanskrit recension we read reflects the late-4th/5th-c. mature text, not an early-3rd-c. layer.
note: cur_med 286 too early for the extant Nepalese Sanskrit recension (late 4th-5th c.); off by >75y.

title: Kāraṇḍavyūha Sūtra (prose; Avalokiteśvaraguṇa-Kāraṇḍavyūha)
consensus_date: late 4th-early 5th c. CE (prose recension, Vaidya ed.)
basis: Studholme, The Origins of Oṃ Maṇipadme Hūṃ (SUNY Press, 2002)
source_file: cleaned_Studholme_Origins of Om Manipadme Hum.txt
recension_note: Our edition (Vaidya, Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha 1961) is the PROSE Kāraṇḍavyūha, the text Studholme analyses directly. This must be distinguished from the much later verse Guṇakāraṇḍavyūha, a Nepalese metrical redaction (treated by Tuladhar-Douglas) of the medieval period. The prose KV's terminus ante quem is supported by Dharmakṣema's use of a primitive version (arrived China 414 CE); the extant prose Sanskrit recension is dated late-4th/early-5th c.
note: cur_med 463 falls inside Studholme's late-4th/early-5th-c. window for the prose recension; correct.

title: Saddharmalaṅkāvatārasūtra [Laṅkāvatārasūtra]
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (expanded Nepalese Sanskrit recension, Nanjio/Vaidya)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism. A Survey (1980), pp. on Laṅkāvatāra
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Vaidya (BST 3, 1963) reprints Nanjio's edition of the LATE expanded Nepalese recension (with Rāvaṇādhyeṣaṇā opening chapter, the Sagāthaka verses, and Dhāraṇī/Māṃsabhakṣaṇa chapters). This is markedly later and longer than Guṇabhadra's 4-fascicle Chinese translation of 443 CE (terminus ante quem for AN EARLIER, shorter version). The Nepalese recension our edition follows reflects 5th-6th c. accretions; do not date it to the c.400 core layer.
note: cur_med 418 tracks the early core; the Vaidya/Nepalese recension is later (5th-6th c.) but median stays within tolerance.

title: Lalitavistara [Lalitavistarasūtra]
consensus_date: 3rd c. CE (extant Sanskrit/Nepalese recension; TAQ 308 CE)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Ohnuma, Ties That Bind (2012); Wong/Shi Jue Wei (2012); Pande, Origins of Buddhism (1974)
source_file: cleaned_Acculturation as Seen Through Buddha’s Birthday Parades in Northern Wei Luoyang...Making of Buddhism as a World Religion_Poh Yee Wong (Shi Jue Wei), 2012.txt
recension_note: The Lalitavistara contains demonstrably old strata (a Buddha-biography core of ~1st c. CE style, noted since Windisch 1882), but in its present form it is an expanded Mahāyāna sūtra (Pande: 'in its present form a Mahāyāna sūtra but containing some clearly old passages'). The extant Sanskrit (Nepalese MSS, ed. Lefmann/Vaidya) is this developed recension. The shorter 3rd-c. Chinese Puyao jing (T186, Dharmarakṣa 308 CE) gives a terminus ante quem for a version close to it; a further-expanded 7th-c. Chinese (T187) shows continued growth. The date here is the extant Sanskrit recension (~3rd c. CE), NOT the 1st-c. biographical core.
note: cur_med 197 reflects the early core; extant Sanskrit recension is ~3rd c. CE (TAQ 308). Difference under 75y, so not flagged, but bounds shifted to the recension.

title: Madhyamakaśālistambasūtra
consensus_date: 1st-2nd c. CE (Mahāyāna sūtra core; no independent Sanskrit MS — text reconstructed from quotations)
basis: Schoening, The Śālistamba-sūtra and its Indian Commentaries (1991); Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Salistamba-sutra and its Indian Commentaries_Jeffrey Davis Schoening, 1991.txt
recension_note: Schoening: no independent Sanskrit recension survives; the 'Madhyamaka-śālistamba-sūtra' (the form printed by Vaidya in the Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha) is itself a Sanskrit reconstruction assembled chiefly from 7th-c. śāstra quotations (~90% of the text). The sūtra belongs to the earliest stratum of Mahāyāna; earliest Chinese (T708) is mid-3rd c. CE, a terminus ante quem for the content, not for our reconstructed Sanskrit. Dated here to the early-Mahāyāna composition layer the reconstruction represents.
note: Reconstructed Sanskrit, not a single witnessed recension; early-Mahayana core. cur_med ~0 within range.

title: Mahāsannipātaratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra [Ratnaketuparivarta, Ratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra]
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: N. Dutt, Ratnaketuparivarta, Gilgit Manuscripts IV (1959) — the Gilgit recension; on Gilgit MSS date cf. Sander (6th-7th c.)
source_file: cleaned_Ajanta. History and Development. Vol, II, Arguments About Ajanta_Walter M. Spink, 2006.txt
recension_note: Dutt's edition in Gilgit Manuscripts IV is the same Gilgit Sanskrit recension as Kurumiya's. The Gilgit MSS are palaeographically 6th-7th c. CE; the Sanskrit recension attested there is later than the work's earliest layer (Mahāsaṃnipāta material has 5th-6th c. Chinese renderings). Date refers to the extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension, not the earliest layer behind the current median of 218. Low confidence given thin library coverage.
note: Same Gilgit recension as ratnakeu; later than current-median layer; low confidence.

title: Nairātmyaparipṛcchāmahāyānasūtra
consensus_date: ca. 8th-10th c. CE (extant Nepalese Sanskrit recension)
basis: Tucci, Rin chen bzaṅ po (Tib. tr. by Kamalagupta & Rin-chen-bzang-po, 958-1055); Li Rongxi, Lives of Great Monks (Chin. tr. by Sūryakīrti)
source_file: cleaned_Tucci_Rin chen bzaṅ po.txt
recension_note: Short Mahāyāna sūtra ('Sūtra of a Nirgrantha Inquiring into Non-ego'). The Tibetan translation by Kamalagupta with Rin-chen-bzang-po (958-1055) dates ca. 1000 CE, and the Chinese (Taishō 1643) by Sūryakīrti is Song-period (mid-11th c.) — both LATE, giving a terminus ante quem ~1000 for some version. The extant Sanskrit we edit (Vaidya, from Nepalese MSS) is the late-medieval Nepalese transmission of this sūtra; an early-8th-c. date (cur_med 742) is at best the upper edge of the composition window.
note: Sanskrit attested late (Tib. ~1000, Chin. Song); cur_med 742 within plausible composition window, kept; low confidence

title: Pratītyasamutpādamahāyānasūtra
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (short Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-sūtra; Nepalese Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha recension)
basis: Scherrer-Schaub (cited in Almogi, Rong-zom-pa's Discourses on Buddhology 2009)
source_file: cleaned_Almogi_Rong zom pa's Discourses on Buddhology.txt
recension_note: A short Mahāyāna sūtra built around the 'ye dharmā hetuprabhavā' / pratītyasamutpāda-hṛdaya formula, addressed to Avalokiteśvara — distinct from the early āgama Pratītyasamutpādasūtra. Our text is Vaidya's edition from the Nepalese Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha; this proto-tantric short dhāraṇī-sūtra type is relatively late (7th-8th c.). Library has no recension-specific date, so confidence is low.
note: Short late-Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-sūtra; ~7th-8th c.; cur_med within range, low confidence.

title: Śālistambasūtra
consensus_date: 1st-2nd c. CE (Mahāyāna sūtra core; no independent Sanskrit MS — reconstructed from quotations)
basis: Schoening, The Śālistamba-sūtra and its Indian Commentaries (1991)
source_file: cleaned_Salistamba-sutra and its Indian Commentaries_Jeffrey Davis Schoening, 1991.txt
recension_note: Identical situation to bsu023: Vaidya's printed Sanskrit (Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha) is a reconstruction from quotations, not an independently transmitted Sanskrit recension. The sūtra is among the earliest Mahāyāna works; earliest Chinese T708 mid-3rd c. CE is a terminus ante quem for the content. Dated to the early-Mahāyāna composition layer the reconstruction represents.
note: Reconstructed Sanskrit; early-Mahayana core. cur_med ~0 within range.

title: Sukhāvatyamṛtavyūha [Sukhāvatī(amṛta)vyūhasūtra], the Smaller Sukhāvatīvyūha
consensus_date: extant Sanskrit (Nepalese) recension, several centuries after the 2nd-3rd c. CE original; ed. text reflects late transmission, c. 4th-7th c. CE or later
basis: Nattier, 'The Indian Roots of Pure Land Buddhism' (Pacific World); Gómez, Land of Bliss (1996)
source_file: cleaned_Nattier_The Indian Roots of Pure Land Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Composition of the shorter Sukhāvatīvyūha is placed in NW India c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (Kumārajīva's Chinese tr. early 5th c. = terminus ante quem for an early form). Our Vaidya 1961 text descends from the late Nepalese Sanskrit recension first edited by Müller-Nanjio (1883); Nattier states the surviving Sanskrit MSS postdate the Chinese by several centuries. Date here is the extant Sanskrit recension, NOT the 2nd-c. original.
note: cur_med ~113 dates the lost original; our edition is the much later Nepalese Sanskrit recension.

title: Samādhirājasūtra
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (Nepalese-MSS recension; Vaidya edition)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Nakamura's chronology of versions: (1) earliest Chinese (not before 400 CE); (2) Narendrayaśas's Chinese + the Gilgit MS; (3) Tibetan; (4)-(5) Nepalese MSS B then A. Vaidya's edition (BST 2) rests on the LATE Nepalese-manuscript recension, the most developed/expanded form, downstream of the Gilgit MS (c. 5th-6th c.) and the 557 CE Chinese terminus. So our edition reflects the 5th-6th c. (and later-copied) Nepalese recension, not the pre-400 earliest layer.
note: cur_med 375 reflects an early layer; the Vaidya Nepalese recension is later, c. 5th-6th c. Corrected to 425-575.

title: Saddharmapuṇḍarīkasūtra
consensus_date: ca. 4th-5th c. CE (developed Nepalese Sanskrit recension, Vaidya ed.)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Karashima, Who Composed the Lotus Sutra; on recensions
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Vaidya 1960 prints the NEPALESE recension (Kern-Nanjio type), the latest and fullest textual form, with the developed/interpolated Devadatta chapter and expanded dhāraṇī/avadāna material. The core sūtra (chs. up to XXII) was complete by ~200 CE in the Central Asian (oldest) line, but the fully expanded Nepalese recension that our edition follows represents a later redactional stage (4th-5th c. and after); date this developed Nepalese Sanskrit recension, not the 1st-2nd c. proto-Lotus.
note: Median 101 (range -100 to 200) dates the early/proto layer; the extant Nepalese Sanskrit recension (Vaidya) is the latest expanded form. Off by >75y.

title: Sarvatathāgatādhiṣṭhānavyūhasūtra
consensus_date: 5th-7th c. CE (extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts I (74); Gilgit MS dating in Hinüber (per Apple, Receptacle of the Sacred); Hamar, Faith in Buddhism
source_file: cleaned_Holz_The Bhadrakarātrī-sūtra_Apotropaic Scriptures in Early Indian Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Duplicate of SA_K10_n526u (same Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-vyūha sūtra, same Dutt Gilgit edition). Our text = Gilgit Sanskrit recension; the Gilgit corpus dates c. 6th-7th c. CE, dating the recension we have. No separately recoverable earlier layer.
note: Same work/edition as n526u; cur_med 633 within Gilgit window. Low confidence.

title: Triskandhasūtra
consensus_date: 3rd-4th c. CE (composition of the confession liturgy; extant Nepalese Sanskrit recension later)
basis: Pagel, The Bodhisattvapiṭaka (1995); Apple, Jewels of the Middle Way (2019)
source_file: cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way  Madhyamaka Legacy of Atisa and His Early Tibetan Followers_James B  Apple, 2019.txt
recension_note: The Mahāyāna 'three-section' confession liturgy (pāpadeśanā, anumodanā, pariṇāmanā) with the 35 buddhas. The Triskandhaka is cited in Śāntideva's Śikṣāsamuccaya, the Ugraparipṛcchā and Vimaladattāparipṛcchā, and listed in the Mahāvyutpatti; a short Triskandhaka was rendered into Chinese by Jñānagupta (6th c.). The composition of the core liturgy is plausibly 3rd-4th c. CE, but OUR edition (M.B. Shakya 2000, Buddhist Himalaya X.1) rests on a transmitted NEPALESE Sanskrit MS, a much later witness of the text; the library offers no recension-specific date, hence low confidence.
note: 35-buddha confession liturgy; cited by Śāntideva; Nepalese Skt recension undatable precisely; current median ~230 kept (within plausible range).

title: Saṃghāṭa Sūtra
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Schopen/Granoff-Shinohara vol. (Sins and Sinners, 2012) on the Gilgit Sanskrit MSS and Canevascini's 5th-c. Khotanese terminus
source_file: cleaned_Sins and Sinners. Perspectives from Asian Religions_Phyllis Granoff and Koichi Shinohara, ed, 2012_A.txt
recension_note: Hinüber's critical edition rests on the SANSKRIT manuscripts found at Gilgit, which date to the 5th-6th c. CE; Schopen stresses there is no fixed Urtext, the text being fluid across its many copies. The underlying composition predates the first-half-5th-c. Khotanese translation (Canevascini), but our edited object is the Gilgit Sanskrit recension itself, hence 5th-6th c.
note: Gilgit Sanskrit recension 5th-6th c.; median 353 low by ~120y.

title: Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra [Vimalakīrtinirdeśa]
consensus_date: 1st-2nd c. CE (composition; our text is a modern Sanskrit retranslation from Tibetan)
basis: Lamotte 1962 (introd. p.293) via Nakamura 1980; edition = Joshi & Pāsādika, CIHTS Sarnath 1981 (reconstruction)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: This GRETIL/DSBC text (Joshi & Pāsādika 1981) is NOT a manuscript but a modern Sanskrit 'restoration' from the Tibetan version (de Jong: 'Bhikkhu Pāsādika also restored the Sanskrit text of the Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra from the Tibetan version'). The Tibetan reflects the same 1st-c.-CE composition (Lamotte); the reconstruction's wording is 20th-c. but represents that recension. Dated to the underlying text, with low certainty because the Sanskrit is editorial.
note: Modern reconstruction from Tibetan; underlying text 1st c. CE; cur_med 136 consistent.

title: Daśabalasūtra
consensus_date: 5th-7th c. CE (extant Central Asian Sanskrit recension)
basis: Holz, The Bhadrakarātrī-sūtra: Apotropaic Scriptures in Early Indian Buddhism; ed. Waldschmidt, Sanskrithandschriften aus den Turfanfunden
source_file: cleaned_Holz_The Bhadrakarātrī-sūtra_Apotropaic Scriptures in Early Indian Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Our text is Waldschmidt's edition of the Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit Daśabalasūtra from the Turfan finds. The canonical core (Majjhima/Saṃyukta-āgama, with Pāli/early parallels far older) lacks the rakṣā/mantra material; the protective layer that defines OUR recension was added only in Central Asia. The Turfan Sanskrit witnesses are post-Gupta Brāhmī manuscripts of the 5th-8th c.; the rakṣā-expanded Central Asian recension is dated here, not the early āgama layer.
note: cur_med 473 falls within the Central-Asian recension window; not off by >75y.

title: Aśokāvadāna
consensus_date: 2nd c. CE (Aśokāvadāna narrative; Mūlasarvāstivāda Sanskrit, in the later Divyāvadāna anthology)
basis: Strong, The Legend of King Aśoka, Princeton 1983 (cited in Reynolds & Carbine, The Life of Buddhism)
source_file: cleaned_Reynolds and Carbine_The_Life_of_Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Our text is Avadāna 29 of the Divyāvadāna (Mukhopadhyaya 1963). Strong dates the Aśokāvadāna narrative itself to the 2nd c. CE (NW India / Mathurā milieu). The Divyāvadāna ANTHOLOGY into which it is embedded is a later Mūlasarvāstivāda compilation (Rotman: 'stories written in Sanskrit from the early centuries of the Common Era,' closely tied to the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya), so the framing collection is 3rd-4th c. or later, but the Aśokāvadāna unit our item isolates is the 2nd-c. text. Dated to that unit.
note: Aśokāvadāna 2nd c. CE narrative (Strong); cur_med 154 consistent.

title: Śārdūlakarṇāvadāna
consensus_date: 2nd-4th c. CE (story-cycle 2nd-3rd c.; extant Sanskrit recension Gupta-period, transmitted in the Divyāvadāna)
basis: Nakamura (1980) on the Mātaṅgī-sūtra/Śārdūlakarṇa material and its 3rd-c. Chinese translation; Rotman, Divine Stories Part 2 (2017) and Schopen (1997) on the Divyāvadāna / Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya recension
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: The core Śārdūlakarṇa/Mātaṅgī narrative (with its jyotiṣa/astronomical section) had a version translated into Chinese already in the 3rd c., giving a 2nd-3rd c. terminus for an early layer. But OUR Sanskrit is the recension embedded in the Divyāvadāna anthology, which draws heavily on the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya (Schopen/Rotman: Kuṣāṇa-to-Gupta redaction) and survives only through a single 17th-c. Nepalese manuscript (Bendall). The extant Sanskrit recension is therefore Gupta-period or later even though the story is older; range widened to span the early layer through the anthology's redaction. Not flagged off: the 2nd-3rd c. content date (current median 208) is well supported by the 3rd-c. Chinese parallel, though the surviving Sanskrit is later.
note: Early story-cycle (Chinese 3rd c.); extant Sanskrit is later Divyāvadāna/MSV recension. Median within range.

title: Divyāvadāna
consensus_date: 4th-6th c. CE (compiled Sanskrit collection, MSV-vinaya based)
basis: A. Rotman, Divine Stories (2017); C. Prebish, Survey of Vinaya Literature (1994)
source_file: cleaned_Survey of Vinaya Litterature_Prebish, 1994.txt
recension_note: A composite anthology: individual stories range from the early centuries CE (Rotman: 'written in Sanskrit from the early centuries of the Common Era'), but the bulk is drawn from / shares the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya, and the collection as a compiled whole, in our Cowell-Neil / Vaidya recension based on late Nepalese MSS, is later. Date the compiled collection (mid-1st millennium), not the oldest individual avadānas.
note: Compiled MSV-related avadana anthology; Nepalese-MSS recension; cur_med 331 within range.

title: Dvāviṃśatyavadānakathā
consensus_date: medieval, c. 11th–12th c. CE (Nepalese avadānamālā compilation)
basis: J. H. Tatelman, Translation and Study of the Pūrṇāvadāna (1988), on the late avadānamālā group; Okada 1993 ('ein mittelalterlicher … Text')
source_file: cleaned_Translation and Study of the Purnavadana_Joel Howard Tatelman, 1988.txt
recension_note: Not an early avadāna text: it belongs to the 'much later avadānamālā' group (with Kalpadrumāvadānamālā, Aśokāvadānamālā etc.), late compilations that recycle the Avadānaśataka and other sources within the Upagupta-to-Aśoka frame. Okada's own subtitle calls it a medieval ('mittelalterlicher') Nepalese devotional text. Comparable garlands (e.g. Bhadrakalpāvadāna) are placed by Speyer 'eleventh century C.E. at the earliest'; the 597 CE median is centuries too early for this medieval recension.
note: Late/medieval avadānamālā compilation, c. 11th c.+, not 6th.

title: Devatāsūtra
consensus_date: 2nd-4th c. CE ((Mūla)sarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension, Gilgit fragments)
basis: A. Mette, Zwei kleine Fragmente aus Gilgit (1981); contextualized by Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Pali Literature…Canonical Literature in Prakrit and Sanskrit of all Hinayana Schools of Buddhism_K. R. Norman, 1983.txt
recension_note: Our text is Mette's edition of the Gilgit Sanskrit fragments (input Klaus Wille), i.e. the (Mūla)sarvāstivāda canonical recension. The Pāli Devatā-saṃyutta parallel is several centuries older; the surviving Sanskrit recension preserved in the Gilgit manuscript find (6th-7th c. MSS) reflects the school's settled Sanskrit canon of roughly the 2nd-4th c. CE — date the Sanskrit recension, not the early-canonical layer.
note: Library lacks recension-specific dating; range from genre/recension context, cur_med 413 plausible.

title: Ekottarāgama [Ekottarāgamasūtra]
consensus_date: 5th-7th c. CE (extant Sanskrit = Gilgit manuscript; Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda recension)
basis: Nattier, A Few Good Men (2003) and Williams (ed.), Buddhism: Critical Concepts vol.8, on Gilgit MS date; edition: Tripāṭhī, Ekottarāgama-Fragmente der Gilgit-Handschrift (1995)
source_file: cleaned_Few Good Men. Bodhisattva Path according to The Inquiry of Ugra (Ugrapariprccha)_Jan Nattier, 2003.txt
recension_note: Our edition is Tripāṭhī's transcription of the GILGIT Sanskrit Ekottarāgama fragments, palaeographically c. 5th-7th c. CE (commonly the 6th c.). The āgama CONTENT goes back to early, pre-sectarian oral transmission (hence the early cur_med), but the cardinal rule requires dating the extant Sanskrit Gilgit recension, which is half a millennium or more later than the canonical content. Distinct from the Chinese T125 Ekottarika-āgama (Anālayo: that collection received later, partly Chinese, additions).
note: cur_med -12 dates the early oral content; corrected to the extant Gilgit Sanskrit MS (6th c.).

title: Jñānālokālaṃkāra sūtra [Sarvabuddhaviṣayāvatārajñānālokālaṃkāra nāma mahāyānasūtra]
consensus_date: ca. early 4th c. CE original; extant Sanskrit = Nepalese/Tibetan-transmitted Mahāyāna recension
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: A Survey with Bibliographical Notes (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Nakamura dates the ORIGINAL composition to the beginning of the 4th c. CE. The text was rendered into Chinese repeatedly from the early 6th c. (Tanyao/Dharmaruci) onward, giving termini ante quem for some version. The Kimura et al. 2004 Sanskrit edition rests on Nepalese/Tibetan-area manuscripts (this tathāgatagarbha-influenced sūtra circulated in Nepal), so the extant Sanskrit recension is later than the 4th-c. ur-text; range widened to cover redaction down to ~5th c.
note: Original early-4th c.; extant Skt recension somewhat later. Median 355 within range.

title: Kaṭhināvadāna
consensus_date: post-1200 CE (Nepalese Buddhist Sanskrit avadāna/vrata literature)
basis: Tuladhar-Douglas, Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal
source_file: cleaned_Tuladhar-Douglas_Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal.txt
recension_note: The Kaṭhināvadāna is a Nepalese vrata-avadāna (Newar Buddhist Sanskrit, NBS) text. While the kaṭhina robe-ceremony is an ancient vinaya institution (cf. Kathinavastu, BCE-early CE), the surviving Kaṭhināvadāna as a metrical avadāna narrative belongs to the late metric avadāna-collection genre composed in Nepal in Sanskrit after c. 1200 CE. The dsbc text is this late Newar recension, NOT the early vinaya kaṭhina material. Range left open-ended to the late medieval/early modern Nepalese tradition.
note: cur_med 698 is wildly early (>500y): conflates the ancient kaṭhina institution with the late Newar avadāna. Corrected to post-1200 CE NBS literature; median ~1400.

title: Garbhāvakrāntyavadāna
consensus_date: 1052 CE (Kṣemendra's Avadānakalpalatā)
basis: Wood, Indic Visual Jātakamālās (2005); Hahn ed. (1997)
source_file: cleaned_Buddha and the Shape of Belief. Indic Visual Jatakamalas, vol.1_Leela Aditi Wood, 2005.txt
recension_note: Pallava 10 of Kṣemendra's Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā, a firmly dated authored work (Kashmir, completed 1052 CE). Although the avadāna narrative (Buddha's teaching on entering the womb, cf. Garbhāvakrānti-sūtra of the Mūlasarvāstivāda) is far older, OUR text is Kṣemendra's 11th-c. poetic re-composition (Hahn's edition from the Tanjur), so dated to 1052.
note: cur_med 995 off by ~57y (within 75y threshold); not flagged but tightened to 1052.

title: Sumāgadhāvadāna [Sumāgadhāvadānasūtra]
consensus_date: 1052 CE (no. 93 of Kṣemendra's Avadānakalpalatā)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: A Survey with Bibliographical Notes (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Our text is chapter/pallava no. 93 (Sumāgadhāvadāna) of Kṣemendra's Avadānakalpalatā (Vaidya ed. vol. II), not the older independent Sumāgadhāvadāna prose recension. The Sumāgadhā story is ancient (Pāli/early Chinese parallels), but THIS Sanskrit text is Kṣemendra's 1052 CE versification within the AKL — date the 11th-c. recension, not the older legend.
note: Kṣemendra recension = AKL 1052; older Sumāgadhā legend not the date.

title: Kalpadrumāvadānamālā
consensus_date: medieval Nepalese period (c. 11th-15th c. CE)
basis: Tatelman, Translation and Study of the Pūrṇāvadāna, 1988 (citing Speyer); Nariman, Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism, 1919
source_file: cleaned_Translation and Study of the Purnavadana_Joel Howard Tatelman, 1988.txt
recension_note: Anonymous versified avadānamālā: a later metrical, Mahāyānized re-elaboration of prose narratives drawn from the Avadānaśataka and related sources. The PROSE source layer is centuries older, but our text is the medieval Nepalese verse recension transmitted in the Nepalese MS tradition (DSBC e-text). Scholarship does not fix a precise century, so a broad medieval Nepalese range is retained.
note: No recension-specific date in library; broad range kept, cur_med 1129 within it. Low confidence.

title: Mahāmantrānusāriṇī
consensus_date: 9th-10th c. CE (extant Sanskrit Pañcarakṣā recension)
basis: Holz, The Bhadrakarātrī-sūtra (citing Hidas); ed. Skilling, Mahāsūtras I (1994) / GRETIL
source_file: cleaned_Holz_The Bhadrakarātrī-sūtra_Apotropaic Scriptures in Early Indian Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Our Mahāmantrānusāriṇī is the Sanskrit Pañcarakṣā text that, per Hidas, REPLACED the earlier Mahāmantrānudhāri found in the proto-Pañcarakṣā collection (Tibetan-translated ~800 CE, Ldan dkar ma catalogue). It therefore reflects a later stage of the collection's transmission: the Sanskrit Pañcarakṣā emerged after the proto-collection and the oldest surviving Indian Pañcarakṣā manuscripts date to ~1100 CE. The earlier protective-verse material it shares (e.g. with the Mahāvastu/svasti-gāthās) is far older, but OUR Sanskrit recension is the post-800, pre-1100 replacement text.
note: cur_med 491 dates an early layer; the extant Sanskrit replacement recension is 9th-10th c. (median ~900), off by >400y.

title: Maitreyavyākaraṇa [Maitreyavyākaraṇasūtra]
consensus_date: extant Sanskrit recension from Gilgit + Nepalese MSS, ca. 6th-9th c. CE (text composed earlier, by ~3rd-4th c.)
basis: Lamotte, History of Indian Buddhism (1988/1958); Lévi, Maitreya le Consolateur (1932); Hartmann's composite edition; Gilgit MS dating per von Hinüber
source_file: cleaned_History of Indian Buddhism_Etienne Lamotte, 1988 (1958).txt
recension_note: Hartmann's text is a CONSTITUTED edition collating three Sanskrit witnesses: the Gilgit MS (6th-7th c.; Majumder/Dutt), the Calcutta MS (Lévi 1932), and the Kathmandu/Nepalese MS (Ishigami 1989, considerably later). The underlying Maitreya prophecy is old (early Chinese versions ca. 303-402 CE), but the surviving Sanskrit witnesses constituting THIS edition span the 6th-9th c.; the edited Sanskrit recension is therefore far later than the median, not 3rd-4th c.
note: Composite Skt edition from 6th-9th c. MSS, much later than median 366 (off >75y); corrected upward.

title: Mahākarmavibhaṅga
consensus_date: 3rd-5th c. CE (extant Nepalese-MSS Sanskrit recension)
basis: S. Lévi, Mahākarmavibhaṅga (1932); H. Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: A Sarvāstivāda-affiliated discourse with old canonical parallels (Pāli, Tocharian/Kuchean, early Chinese) reflecting an early core. Our text is Lévi's edition based on late Nepalese manuscripts, an expanded Sanskrit recension; date the developed Sanskrit version (early-mid 1st millennium), not the earliest layer attested by the parallels.
note: Sarvastivada sutra; Nepalese-MSS recension; cur_med 325 within range.

title: Avikalpapraveśadhāraṇī
consensus_date: 3rd-4th c. CE (early Yogācāra dhāraṇī-sūtra)
basis: inferred from Yogācāra reception: source of Maitreya's Dharmadharmatāvibhāga and commented by Kamalaśīla (Brunnhölzl, Mining for Wisdom within Delusion, 2013; Mathes, Fine Blend, 2015)
source_file: cleaned_Mining for Wisdom within Delusion. Maitreya's Distinction between Phenomena and Nature ..Dharmadharmatavibhanga_Karl Brunnholzl, 2013.txt
recension_note: The library has no statement explicitly dating the composition; the date is inferred from reception. The Avikalpapraveśa(/Nirvikalpapraveśa)dhāraṇī is an early Yogācāra dhāraṇī-sūtra that underlies the Dharmadharmatāvibhāga (Maitreya/Asaṅga corpus, ~4th c.) and was given a ṭīkā by Kamalaśīla (8th c.), giving an early-Yogācāra terminus ante quem (~4th c.). The Matsuda critical Sanskrit edition rests on a single Sanskrit MS, but the text predates the developed Yogācāra śāstras. Confidence low: no recension-specific century in the local library.
note: cur_med 464 is later than the early-Yogācāra terminus ante quem; lowered to 3rd-4th c., but confidence low (no library date) so not a firm correction.

title: Arthaviniścayasūtra
consensus_date: 3rd-5th c. CE (digest of Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda āgama categories)
basis: Samtani, The Arthaviniścaya-sūtra & its Commentary (Nibandhana), 1971
source_file: cleaned_Sanskrit Texts from Giuseppe Tucci’s Collection. Part I_Francesco Sferra, ed, 2008.txt
recension_note: Samtani's critical edition of the same non-Mahāyāna doctrinal digest (root sūtra; the Nibandhana commentary by Vīryaśrīdatta is later, c.8th c., and is NOT this item). The root text draws on Sarvāstivāda/MSV āgama formulas and is compiled in the early centuries CE; no sharp recension date in the library, so confidence is low. (Same work as bsu005_u, Vaidya ed.)
note: Root sūtra (not the later Nibandhana); cur_med 411 within broad range

title: Āryānityatāsūtra
consensus_date: 5th-7th c. CE (Sanskrit Mūlasarvāstivāda recension)
basis: Sasaki 1971 (Tridaṇḍaka-Anityatāsūtra of the Mūlasarvāstivāda), cited in Silk, Managing Monks; Schopen, Bones Stones and Buddhist Monks on the date of extant Sanskrit MSV sources
source_file: cleaned_Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks..Archaeology, Epigraphy and Texts of Monastic Buddhism in India_Gregory Schopen, 1997.txt
recension_note: A short Mūlasarvāstivāda canonical sūtra on impermanence/death (the 'Tridaṇḍaka-Anityatāsūtra'). The doctrine is old, but our extant Sanskrit recension belongs to the Central Asian/Gilgit MSV manuscript corpus, which Schopen dates no earlier than the 5th c. The library gives no edition-specific date for this DSBC text, so bounds are broad and confidence low.
note: MSV short sūtra; Sanskrit recension 5th-7th c., median ~536 sits within broad band

title: Āryasaṃghāṭasūtra
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); von Hinüber & Kudo Gilgit edition; general Gilgit-MS dating (6th-7th c.)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Our edition is the GILGIT Sanskrit recension (von Hinüber & Kudo), the text underlying the DSBC e-text. The Gilgit MSS are paleographically 6th-7th c.; an early Chinese translation (Upaśūnya, 538 CE) and an Old Khotanese version attest some version earlier, but date here is the Gilgit Sanskrit recension we have, not the earliest layer. Library has no recension-specific monograph, hence low confidence.
note: cur_med 338 is early for the Gilgit recension but defensible as a composition estimate; no firm recension date in library to force >75y change.

title: Āryatriratnānusmṛtisūtra
consensus_date: None
basis: no recension-specific scholarship located in library
source_file: cleaned_Chandra_Materials for a History of Tibetan Literature.txt
recension_note: Short liturgical 'Recollection of the Three Jewels' sūtra (buddhānusmṛti formula tradition, cf. Pāli Ratana-sutta), preserved in Sanskrit and with a Tibetan-canonical commentary (Kamalaśīla). The workitem has no printed edition recorded and the library yields no recension-specific date for the Sanskrit text, so no year is asserted.
note: Devotional formula sūtra; no datable recension evidence in library. Date null.

title: Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānasūtra
consensus_date: 3rd-4th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension, ch. 2)
basis: Daniel M. Stuart, A Less Traveled Path (diss. 2012, basis of the 2015 edition), §1.3 'The Date of the Saddharmasmṛtyupasthāna(sūtra)'
source_file: cleaned_Less Traveled Path. Meditation and Textual Practice in Saddharmasmrtyupasthana (yogacara)_Daniel Malinowski Stuart, 2012.txt
recension_note: Stuart edits ch. 2 of the Sanskrit text directly (our DSBC e-text follows his edition). The recension represented by the three extant witnesses (Skt MS, Tibetan, Chinese) was 'more or less fixed' by the 542-543 CE Chinese translation (terminus ante quem); composed after the Mahāvibhāṣā (~150 CE) and before Vasubandhu (350/360-430/440). Date is the Sanskrit recension itself, not earlier Āgama-derived material it absorbs.
note: cur_med 335 sits squarely in Stuart's 3rd/4th-c. window; no change.

title: Mahāmeghasūtra
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980), p.181 area; Buswell & Lopez, Princeton Dictionary (2014) s.v. Mahāmeghasūtra
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: A dhāraṇī-bearing Mahāyāna sūtra carrying tathāgatagarbha doctrine (four guṇapāramitās of selfhood) and the influence of the Mahāparinirvāṇasūtra, plus the Nāgārjuna-prophecy and anti-meat injunctions — features of a developed, relatively late text. Dharmakṣema's Chinese version (Dafangdeng wuxiang jing, ca. 414-426 CE) is the terminus ante quem for a version; our edition is Bendall's text from Nepalese Sanskrit MSS, i.e. the surviving Sanskrit recension, dated here to the 4th-5th c. rather than the ~300 CE core.
note: Dhāraṇī/tathāgatagarbha sūtra; Chinese tr. ~414-426 caps the core; Sanskrit recension 4th-5th c.

title: Meghasūtra
consensus_date: 4th c. CE (extant Sanskrit dhāraṇī recension; core Mahāmegha complex c. 300 CE)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: A Survey (1980); Lamotte 1988 (apud Osto 2008)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: The Megha-sūtra (Bendall 1880, the rain-making nāga dhāraṇī text) is a component of the larger Mahāmegha (Mahāmeghasūtra) complex. Nakamura dates the Mahāmegha c. 300 CE; Lamotte connects its Andhra/Ikṣvāku-Sātavāhana prophecy ('700 years after the Nirvāṇa') to the same horizon. As a dhāraṇī text our extant Sanskrit Megha-sūtra is plausibly a 4th-c. recension, somewhat later than the c. 300 core. Library has no edition-specific date for Bendall's text, hence low confidence.
note: Dhāraṇī sūtra; core Mahāmegha c.300 CE (Nakamura), extant Skt dhāraṇī recension likely 4th c.; corrected median ~360, off from current 246 by >75y (low confidence, so not counted in 'changed').

title: Sarvatathāgatādhiṣṭhānavyūhasūtra
consensus_date: 5th-7th c. CE (extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts I (74); Gilgit MS dating in Hinüber (per Apple, Receptacle of the Sacred); Hamar, Faith in Buddhism
source_file: cleaned_Holz_The Bhadrakarātrī-sūtra_Apotropaic Scriptures in Early Indian Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Mahāyāna dhāraṇī-vyūha sūtra (full title Sarvatathāgatādhiṣṭhāna-sattvāvalokana-buddhakṣetra-sandarśana-vyūha). Our edition is precisely the Gilgit Sanskrit recension (Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts I, pp. 49-89). The Gilgit birch-bark corpus is dated c. 6th-7th c. CE (Hinüber 7th-8th c.; some folios 5th-6th), which dates the Sanskrit recension we have; any earlier oral/redactional layer is not separately recoverable. Text shows developed dhāraṇī/proto-tantric features and the 'last age, southern region' decline motif (Nattier 1991).
note: cur_med 633 sits within the Gilgit-recension window 450-700; not off >75y. Low confidence (no composition-specific study).

title: Āryābuddhabalādhānaprātihāryavikurvaṇanirdeśanāma mahāyānasūtra
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: von Hinüber 2014b / Apple (Candrakīrti and the Lotus sūtra); Schopen 1997 (Bones, Stones)
source_file: cleaned_Apple_Candrakīrti and the Lotus sutra.txt
recension_note: Same work as item SA_K10_bsu008_u (Buddhabalādhānaprātihāryavikurvāṇanirdeśa-sūtra). Vaidya's Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha text derives from the Gilgit recension (Schopen edited its leaves). The composition layer is older but the EXTANT Sanskrit witness is the Gilgit MS, dated 7th-8th c.; we date that recension.
note: Same Gilgit Mahāyāna sūtra as item 1; cur_med within range.

title: Nagaropamasūtra
consensus_date: c. 2nd-5th c. CE (extant Central Asian Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension with apotropaic appendix)
basis: Bongard-Levin, Boucher, Fukita & Wille, The Nagaropamasūtra (1996); Drewes, Oral Texts in Indian Mahāyāna; Schmithausen, Maitrī and Magic
source_file: cleaned_Drewes_Oral_Texts_in_Indian_Mahayana.txt
recension_note: Canonical pratītyasamutpāda sūtra of the Saṃyuktāgama with a Pāli Saṃyutta-nikāya parallel. The base discourse is early-canonical, but our edition is the extant Central Asian (Turfan) Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension, distinctively expanded with an apotropaic/text-worship appendix and used as an amulet text. Date the extant Sanskrit/Central Asian recension (Kuṣāṇa-Gupta MSS), not the earlier Pāli layer.
note: cur_med 559 roughly fits the Central Asian Sanskrit MS recension; acceptable.

title: Nidānasaṃyukta
consensus_date: 2nd-4th c. CE (Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension, Turfan MSS)
basis: C. Tripāṭhī, Fünfundzwanzig Sūtras des Nidānasaṃyukta (1962); Norman, Pali Literature (1983)
source_file: cleaned_Pali Literature…Canonical Literature in Prakrit and Sanskrit of all Hinayana Schools of Buddhism_K. R. Norman, 1983.txt
recension_note: Tripāṭhī edits the Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit Nidānasaṃyukta reconstructed from the Turfan (Central Asian) manuscript finds. The Pāli Nidāna-saṃyutta parallel is older; our text is the Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit canonical recension (manuscripts c. 5th-8th c., text settled roughly 2nd-4th c. CE). Date the Sanskrit recension, not the shared early-canonical material.
note: Genre/recension-based range; cur_med 444 is at the upper edge but within tolerance.

title: Mahāsannipātaratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra [Ratnaketuparivarta, Ratnaketudhāraṇīsūtra]
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Kurumiya, Ratnaketuparivarta: Sanskrit Text (1978) ed. is the Gilgit recension; on Gilgit Buddhist MSS date cf. Sander (6th-7th c.) via Kuwayama/Spink
source_file: cleaned_Ajanta. History and Development. Vol, II, Arguments About Ajanta_Walter M. Spink, 2006.txt
recension_note: Kurumiya's text is based on the Gilgit Sanskrit manuscript of the Ratnaketuparivarta (the dhāraṇī-section of the Mahāsaṃnipāta corpus). The Gilgit Buddhist MSS are palaeographically 6th-7th c. CE; the Sanskrit recension they attest is later than the work's earliest layer (the Mahāsaṃnipāta material has Chinese renderings from the 5th-6th c.). Library lacks a firm recension-specific consensus date, so confidence is low; the date refers to the extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension, not the earliest layer behind the current median of 218.
note: Gilgit Sanskrit recension later than the 2nd-3rd c. layer implied by current median; thin library coverage, low confidence.

title: Saṃghāṭasūtra [Saṅghāṭasūtra]
consensus_date: 5th c. CE (extant recension; text composed late 4th-early 5th c.)
basis: Schopen/Granoff-Shinohara vol. (Sins and Sinners, 2012) citing Canevascini's dating of the Khotanese translation
source_file: cleaned_Sins and Sinners. Perspectives from Asian Religions_Phyllis Granoff and Koichi Shinohara, ed, 2012_A.txt
recension_note: Canevascini's edition is of the KHOTANESE translation, which he dates to the first half of the 5th c. CE. The Sanskrit Saṃghāṭasūtra it renders must therefore have been composed by the late 4th-early 5th c.; it was a fluid, widely-copied Mahāyāna text (no fixed Urtext, per Schopen) attested by seven+ Gilgit Sanskrit MSS. Dating reflects this 5th-c. Khotanese recension, not a hypothetical earlier layer.
note: Khotanese recension 1st half 5th c. (Canevascini); median 351 low by ~100y.

title: Rāṣṭrapālasūtra fragment (SF 101, Central Asia)
consensus_date: Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit āgama recension; Central Asian MS, mid-first millennium CE
basis: Waldschmidt, 'The Rāṣṭrapālasūtra in Sanskrit Remnants from Central Asia' (cited in Kudo, Karmavibhanga 2004); ed. H. Matsumura
source_file: cleaned_Karmavibhanga_Noriyuki Kudo, 2004.txt
recension_note: This is the mainstream (Sarvāstivāda) Rāṣṭrapālasūtra known from Central Asian Sanskrit remnants (Waldschmidt; Matsumura), NOT the Mahāyāna Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchā (whose 3rd-c. Chinese tr. Boucher studied). The Sanskrit witness is a Central Asian āgama-type MS of the mid-first millennium CE; the narrative tradition is older. Library lacks a precise palaeographic date for SF 101, hence low confidence.
note: Sarvāstivāda Skt Rāṣṭrapālasūtra (not Mahāyāna pariprcchā); cur_med 546 within plausible MS window.

title: (untitled Sanskrit Turfan fragment, SHT-type; Wille ed.)
consensus_date: None
basis: no identifying or dating scholarship for this fragment in the local library
recension_note: No title/author; unidentified Sanskrit Turfan fragment (Wille, SHT). The text it carries is not identified in the local library, so no recension can be dated. Manuscript artefacts of this type are palaeographically c. 5th-8th c., but that is a copy date, not a composition/recension date. No date assigned per the no-invention rule.
note: Unidentified fragment; no library basis to confirm or correct cur_med 471.

consensus_date: None
recension_note: Workitem has no title, author, or edition; only a generic GRETIL Buddhist-directory URL. The slug 'sf137_sc' points to a GRETIL Sanskrit-fragment ('sf') file (Wille-type Turfan/Schøyen fragment edition) but the specific text/recension cannot be identified from the available metadata, and no recension-specific scholarship can be matched. Per method, no date assigned.
note: Unidentifiable fragment item (no title/author/edition); cannot date — confidence none.

title: Vimalakīrtinirdeśa (Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra)
consensus_date: 1st-2nd c. CE (text of the extant Potala Sanskrit recension)
basis: Lamotte, L'Enseignement de Vimalakīrti 1962 (introd. p.293), reported in Nakamura 1980; manuscript per Study Group on Buddhist Sanskrit Literature 2006
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Our edition is the Study Group (Taisho Univ.) 2006 diplomatic edition of the complete Sanskrit codex found in the Potala Palace in 1999. As an early-tantric dhāraṇī it cannot predate that horizon by much; the 600 CE median falls squarely within the Gilgit range, so no correction is warranted.

title: Adhyardhaśatikā Prajñāpāramitā (Prajñāpāramitānayaśatapañcāśatikā / Rishukyō)
consensus_date: late 7th–8th c. CE (extant developed tantric Naya recension)

source_file: cleaned_de Jong_A New History Of Tantric Literature In India.txt
recension_note: An ancestral Naya text existed by the first half of the 7th c. (cited by Candrakīrti), but the Rishukyō grew from a plain Prajñāpāramitā into a tantric text across the 7th–8th c.; the surviving Sanskrit (Tomabechi 2009, ed. from the developed Naya/Adhyardhaśatikā form) reflects this later tantric recension, well advanced by the 8th c., not the 7th-c. proto-layer or the earliest Chinese (Xuanzang T.220(10), 660s) state.
note: Developed tantric Naya recension; ~late 7th–8th c., not 6th.

title: Aparimitāyuḥsūtra
consensus_date: 8th-9th c. CE (dhāraṇī sūtra recension; extant Nepalese Sanskrit MS later)
basis: Mei Ching Hsuan 2009 (Chinese/Tibetan transmission of the Aparimitāyurjñāna dhāraṇī sūtra); Viehbeck (mass-production at Dunhuang, early-mid 9th c.); Sen 2003 (first Chinese 973-980)
source_file: cleaned_Development of Pho ba Liturgy in Medieval Tibet_Mei Ching Hsuan, 2009.txt
recension_note: This is the short Aparimitāyurjñāna-nāma dhāraṇī sūtra (NOT the larger Sukhāvatīvyūha). Terminus ante quem: thousands of Tibetan copies mass-produced at Dunhuang in the 820s-840s (Viehbeck), and the Fangshan stone engraving; earliest surviving Chinese is Dharmadeva 973-980 (Sen). As a proto-tantric dhāraṇī sūtra it is an 8th-9th c. composition; our DSBC text follows the standard Walleser ed. from a Nepalese Sanskrit MS, which physically is later still. The recension content is the 8th-9th c. dhāraṇī sūtra.
note: Late dhāraṇī sūtra; Dunhuang/Chinese transmission places it 8th-9th c., not 4th-5th. Median 504 far too early.

title: Sitātapatrā
consensus_date: 8th-10th c. CE (developed uṣṇīṣa/vidyārājñī recension; Nepalese transmission)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: A Survey; Snellgrove, Indo-Tibetan Buddhism (Dunhuang Sitātapatrā among Sarvadurgatipariśodhana materials)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: The Sitātapatrā dhāraṇī existed in an early form from the 5th-6th c., but our edition (Samten & Pandey 2002, CIHTS/Dhīḥ) is the long-title developed recension Sarvatathāgatoṣṇīṣasitātapatrā-nāma-aparājitamahāpratyaṅgirāvidyārājñī, i.e. the expanded uṣṇīṣa-deity vidyārājñī form that circulates with the Sarvadurgatipariśodhana materials at Dunhuang (8th-10th c.). Dated to this developed Nepalese-transmitted recension, not the earliest pratyaṅgirā layer.
note: Developed uṣṇīṣa recension; median 699 sits at the early edge of the 8th-10th c. window, acceptable.
