title: Bhaiṣajyaguruvaidūryaprabharājasūtra
consensus_date: extant Sanskrit (Gilgit-derived) recension, c. 5th-7th c. CE (core 'early centuries CE')
basis: Buswell & Lopez, Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism (2014) s.v. Bhaiṣajyagurusūtra; Nakamura (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism_Buswell & Lopez 2014_.txt
recension_note: Nakamura judges 'the time of its appearance may be considered to have been fairly late'; the medicine-buddha cult emerges in India by the 3rd c. (Saddharmapuṇḍarīka), but the developed pure-land/dhāraṇī sūtra is later. The earliest secure Chinese witness ~457 CE (the 'forged' Hui-chien version) with full translations by Dharmagupta (615) and Xuanzang (650); the surviving Sanskrit is the Gilgit-deposit manuscript (6th-7th c.) on which Vaidya's edition ultimately rests. Dating the extant Sanskrit recension, c. 5th-7th c., not the 'early centuries CE' core.
note: Pure-land/dhāraṇī sūtra; Gilgit Sanskrit recension 6th-7th c.; Chinese 457/615/650 — recension far later than median 273.

title: Suvarṇaprabhāsasūtra (Suvarṇaprabhāsottamasūtrendrarāja)
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (expanded/long Nepalese Sanskrit recension)
basis: Dan Martin, Bibliography of Tib./Skt. Philology (2006); Johannes Nobel's edition; Yijing's 703 CE translation of the long recension
source_file: cleaned_Bibliography_tib_skt_philology_Dan Martin, 2006.txt
recension_note: Our edition bears the long title Suvarṇaprabhāsottamasūtrendrarāja = the EXPANDED recension (= Yijing's 'Most Victorious King' translation, 703 CE; Nobel's Sanskrit ed., basis of Bagchi). The short version precedes Dharmakṣema's early-5th-c. Chinese (~417 CE), but the expanded Nepalese Sanskrit text we edit, with its added dhāraṇī/protective chapters, is much later (terminus ante quem Yijing 703). Date is the long Sanskrit recension, not the early-5th-c. short text.
note: cur_med 343 dates the short version; the edited expanded recension (=Yijing 703) is 6th-7th c., off by >75y. Corrected.

title: Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa (Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa)
consensus_date: c. 750-1000 CE (extant Sanskrit composite recension; datable Rājavyākaraṇa layer c. 750)
basis: Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2002); Buswell, Encyclopedia of Buddhism (full Chinese tr. only early Song, c. 980-1000)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
recension_note: Same work as SA_K12_mamuka (different GRETIL upload of the Gaṇapati Śāstrī edition). Multi-layered kriyātantra: proto-core possibly 7th c., but the extant Sanskrit recension contains the c. 750 CE Rājavyākaraṇa and was rendered in full into Chinese only in the early Song (c. 980-1000), so the composite recension reaches the 10th c. Dating targets that recension, not the earliest layer.
note: Composite; core ~750, recension to ~10th c. cur_med 722 within bounds, not >75y off.

title: Svalpākṣarā Prajñāpāramitā
consensus_date: 7th-9th c. CE (extant tantric Sanskrit recension)

recension_note: Our text (Vaidya, Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha, from Nepalese MSS; classed in the Tantra section of the Tibetan canon, T 258) is the short, tantric-flavored 'Perfection of Wisdom in a Few Words', a counterpart to the Hṛdaya, dialogue Lord–Avalokiteśvara with esoteric mantra/dhāraṇī terminology. Conze places the original after the c.550 cutoff of old-style sūtras, among the tantric Prajñāpāramitā group (c.7th-9th c.); Chinese T258 by Devaśāntika gives a TAQ of late 10th c. Conze even notes our Sanskrit lacks recommendations present in the Tibetan/Chinese, i.e. recension differences.

Vaidya 1961 edits the text from the Nepalese Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha (Hodgson-tradition MSS), a much later transmission. consensus_date: mid-late 6th c. CE (Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Davidson (citing Strickmann), Some Observations on an Uṣṇīṣa Abhiṣeka rite; ed. Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts I
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Some Observations on an Uṣṇīṣa Abhiṣeka rite.txt
recension_note: Our edition is the Gilgit manuscript text (Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts I, pp. 35-40). The Eleven-headed Avalokiteśvara-dhāraṇī (Ekādaśamukha) was first translated into Chinese by Yaśogupta 564-572 CE, giving a firm terminus ante quem; Davidson/Strickmann place its emergence in the second half of the 6th c. The Gilgit Sanskrit witness (6th-7th c.) represents essentially this recension, so the date refers to the mid-late 6th-c. Sanskrit text, not a later kalparāja expansion.
note: cur_med 485 is ~65y from the 6th-c. center; within tolerance.

title: Gaṇapatihṛdaya-Dhāraṇī
consensus_date: c. 8th-9th c. CE (Buddhist dhāraṇī, broad)
basis: Wedemeyer, Making Sense of Tantric Buddhism (genre placement only); Baruah 2000
source_file: cleaned_Wedemeyer_Making Sense of Tantric Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Short Buddhist Gaṇapati dhāraṇī edited by Iwamoto 1937. The library has no recension-specific dating; Wedemeyer and Baruah discuss it only as part of the esoteric dhāraṇī genre, which crystallised in the proto-tantric/early-tantric period (roughly 7th-9th c.). Bounds left wide; no firm anchor for the extant Sanskrit text.
note: No firm scholarly date; cur_med 791 falls within plausible 8th-9th c. dhāraṇī range. Low confidence.

title: Grahamātṛkānāmadhāraṇī
consensus_date: 8th-10th c. CE (developed esoteric/dhāraṇī recension; Newar Saptavāra tradition)
basis: Boschung & Wessels-Mevissen (eds.) 2012 on navagraha cult; Grönbold, Saptavāra; Yoshizaki, Newar Buddhism; Skorupski sTog Kanjur catalogue (Grahamātṛkā nāmadhāraṇī in Tibetan canon)
source_file: cleaned_Skorupski_sTog Kanjur catalogue.txt
recension_note: Same text-type as the Āryaśrīgrahamātṛkā (SA_K12_n2990u): a planetary-deity dhāraṇī of the developed esoteric period, part of the Saptavāra collection and present in the Tibetan kriyātantra section. The navagraha framework predates it (~600 CE) but the dhāraṇī itself is a Pāla-period composition; our dsbc Sanskrit text is the Nepalese dhāraṇī recension.
note: Esoteric astral dhāraṇī; median 696 within 8th-10th c. window.

title: Mahādaṇḍadhāraṇīśītavatī
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (extant Sanskrit dhāraṇī, present by c. 800 CE)
basis: Gergely Hidas, 'Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī-Śītavatī: A Buddhist Apotropaic Scripture' (2017)
source_file: cleaned_Gergely_Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī-Śītavatī_A Buddhist Apotropaic Scripture.txt
recension_note: Hidas's edition (our text) transmits the developed Sanskrit dhāraṇī almost exclusively via Pañcarakṣā manuscripts (E. India 11th-13th c.; Nepal 11th-20th c.; main MS 16th c.). A terminus ante quem for the text is c. 800 CE: the Tibetan translation (Jinamitra/Dānaśīla/Ye shes sde) and its listing in the Lhan kar ma catalogue (~800). The MMK (8th c.) already references the Daṇḍadhāraṇī. The 'Mahāśītavatī' collection-bound form and the surviving Sanskrit witnesses are later/medieval; the Chinese rendering is as late as 984 CE (Fatian). Dated here to the recension extant by c. 800 (the form behind the Tibetan and the Nepalese MS transmission), not a hypothetical earlier layer.
note: Extant dhāraṇī present by c.800 (Tib. + Lhan kar ma); cur_med 567 ~180y too early for the developed recension.

title: Mahāpratisarāmahāvidyārājñī
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (developed dhāraṇī-sūtra; Nepalese Sanskrit recension ed. Hidas)
basis: Hidas, Mahāpratisarā-Mahāvidyārājñī: The Great Amulet (2012); Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980), on the Pañcarakṣā
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Leading member of the Pañcarakṣā amulet-spell collection. A short dhāraṇī core may be earlier, but the developed vidyārājñī sūtra (with the amulet cult) had taken shape by the 7th c.: terminus ante quem is the first Chinese translation by Baosiwei/Ratnacinta in 693 CE. Our edition (Hidas) is the later Nepalese Sanskrit recension, so dating to the 6th-7th c. developed text is correct.
note: cur_med 552 within the plausible 6th-7th c. band; current bounds 400-693 acceptable.

title: Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa [Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa]
consensus_date: c. 750-1000 CE (extant Sanskrit composite recension; datable Rājavyākaraṇa layer c. 750)
basis: Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2002); Buswell, Encyclopedia of Buddhism (full Chinese tr. only early Song, c. 980-1000)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
recension_note: The MMK is an encyclopedic, multi-layered kriyātantra. A 7th-c. proto-core may exist, but the extant Sanskrit text edited by Gaṇapati Śāstrī (S. Indian/Nepalese MSS) includes the Rājavyākaraṇa ('Prophecy of Kings'), composed c. 750 CE (Davidson); the full work was translated into Chinese only in the early Song (c. 980-1000), implying the comprehensive recension we have continued growing into the 10th c. Our date targets that composite recension, not the earliest stratum.
note: Composite text; core ~750, extant recension to ~10th c. cur_med 711 within bounds 500-1100, slightly early but not >75y off the 725-1000 window center.

title: Mahāmaṇivipulavimānaviśvasupratiṣṭhitaguhyaparamarahasyakalparājadhāraṇī
consensus_date: ca. 6th-7th c. CE (Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Orzech/Sørensen/Payne, Esoteric Buddhism and the Tantras in East Asia (2011); ed. Matsumura 1983 (Gilgit MSS)
source_file: cleaned_Esoteric Buddhism and the Tantras in East Asia_Charles D. Orzech,Henrik H. Sørensen, Richard K. Payne, 2011.txt
recension_note: The title is attested already in the Liang dynasty (502-557) and in early Chinese versions (e.g. T.1007), so an early layer existed by the 6th c. But our edition (Matsumura 1983) is the Sanskrit text of the GILGIT Buddhist MSS, whose corpus is paleographically 6th-7th c. The extant Gilgit recension is dated to that span rather than to the earliest attestation. Current median 605 fits.
note: Gilgit Sanskrit recension (our ed.) 6th-7th c.; early layer attested under the Liang; current 605 fine.

title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti
consensus_date: late 8th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension)
basis: Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (citing his 1981 study and the Vilāsavajra commentary)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
recension_note: The Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti is given a firm terminus ante quem by Vilāsavajra's commentary (Nāmamantrārthāvalokinī, c. 770-800 CE); the text is dated by Davidson to the latter 8th c. and is closely tied to the Yogatantra/STTS milieu. Pandey's 1994 Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha edition reflects the Nepalese transmission of this developed yogatantra-period text; no significantly earlier Sanskrit layer is attested.
note: cur_med 695 ~75y too early; Vilāsavajra commentary forces a later-8th-c. date. Corrected to 750-800.

title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti
consensus_date: late 8th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension)
basis: Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (citing his 1981 study and the Vilāsavajra commentary)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
recension_note: Same text as SA_K12_manjn1pu; this entry is Pandey's 1994 edition checked against Raghu Vira's Śata-piṭaka 18. Terminus ante quem from Vilāsavajra's commentary (c. 770-800 CE); Davidson dates the MNS to the latter 8th c. Nepalese stotra-collection transmission of the yogatantra-period text.
note: Duplicate MNS; same correction to 750-800 on Vilāsavajra terminus.

title: Mārīcīnāmadhāraṇī (Āryamārīcī-nāma-dhāraṇī)
consensus_date: c. 6th-8th c. CE (esoteric goddess dhāraṇī; late Nepalese Sanskrit recension)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2002), on the Mārīcī cult
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Dhāraṇī of the esoteric solar goddess Mārīcī. The library gives no precise date for this specific recension. The Mārīcī cult is firmly attested in the mature esoteric period (6th-8th c.; Chinese Mārīcī translations span from an early anonymous version to Amoghavajra in the 8th c.). Our edition is the Sarnath/CIHTS text from a late Indian/Nepalese manuscript (Dhīḥ 2006), i.e. a developed recension, not an early layer.
note: cur_med 553 sits within the loose 6th-8th c. esoteric band; low confidence, no recension-specific date in library.

title: Mekhalā-dhāraṇī
consensus_date: 5th-7th c. CE (Gilgit-recension apotropaic dhāraṇī)
basis: Tripathi, 'Gilgit-Blätter der Mekhalā-dhāraṇī' (1981); dhāraṇī-genre dating in Sanchez (Indian Buddhist Dhāraṇī)
source_file: cleaned_Sins and Sinners. Perspectives from Asian Religions_Phyllis Granoff and Koichi Shinohara, ed, 2012_A.txt
recension_note: Short apotropaic rakṣā/pratisarā-type dhāraṇī, part of the early protective (Pañcarakṣā-adjacent) cluster. The extant Sanskrit is the Gilgit recension, attested in the Gilgit birch-bark folios edited by Tripathi 1981; Gilgit MSS are dated c. 6th-7th c. CE (Hinüber 7th-8th c.), giving a terminus ante quem. The dhāraṇī itself likely originated in the 5th-6th c. apotropaic dhāraṇī wave (Sanchez: dhāraṇī-vidhis spread after 6th c.). No layer earlier than the Gilgit Sanskrit is recoverable here.
note: cur_med 631 (placeholder) falls within the 450-700 Gilgit-recension range; not off by >75y from upper part of range. Low confidence.

title: Mahāpratisarā-vidyārājñī [Mahāpratisarādhāraṇī(sūtra)]
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (Pañcarakṣā dhāraṇī; ed. Iwamoto)
basis: Iwamoto, Mahāpratisarā (Kleinere Dhāraṇī-Texte, 1937); Hidas (2012); Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Iwamoto's edition of the Mahāpratisarā dhāraṇī within the Pañcarakṣā corpus — a slightly shorter/different witness of the same text as item SA_K12_mahpratu. The dhāraṇī-sūtra is datable to the 6th-7th c. (terminus ante quem 693 CE, Baosiwei's Chinese translation); the surviving Sanskrit is the developed Nepalese-line recension, not the earliest spell-core.
note: cur_med 552 within plausible band; current bounds 400-693 acceptable.

title: Mahāpratisarāvidyādhāraṇī
consensus_date: before 693 CE; extant Sanskrit recension ca. 6th-7th c. CE
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980) on Chinese T.920 (Bodhiruci, 693 CE); ed. Hidas 2012 (Nepalese/Gilgit MSS)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Bodhiruci's Chinese versions (693 CE) give a terminus ante quem for the dhāraṇī. Our edition is Hidas 2012, a critical text based on later Nepalese and Gilgit-type Sanskrit MSS of the Great Amulet; the extant Sanskrit recension belongs to the developed 6th-7th c. dhāraṇī stratum, not to the earliest layer. Kept within the 550-693 band.
note: Pre-693 (Bodhiruci) ceiling; extant Skt recension 6th-7th c.; current 600 fits.

title: Mahāsāhasrapramardanī
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (developed Sanskrit Pañcarakṣā recension, present by c. 800 CE)
basis: Gergely Hidas 2017 (parallel Pañcarakṣā transmission); Jinah Kim, Receptacle of the Sacred (2013) on the Nepalese Pañcarakṣā manuscript tradition
source_file: cleaned_Gergely_Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī-Śītavatī_A Buddhist Apotropaic Scripture.txt
recension_note: The Mahāsāhasrapramardanī is one of the five Pañcarakṣā rakṣā-sūtras and shares the transmission history of the Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī-Śītavatī: the group was rendered into Tibetan c. 800 CE and listed among the Pañcamahādhāraṇī (~Lhan kar ma horizon). Iwamoto's edition (our text) is based on Nepalese MSS, i.e. the developed Sanskrit recension transmitted in Pañcarakṣā manuscripts (11th c. onward). Individual dhāraṇī antecedents are older, but our extant Sanskrit recension is the developed late-1st-millennium / medieval Nepalese form; dated to the recension present by c. 800.
note: Pañcarakṣā member; developed Sanskrit recension present by ~800. cur_med 578 too early for the extant recension; flagged but low confidence (no text-specific date in library).

title: Nārāyaṇaparipṛcchā āryamahāmāyāvijayavāhinī nāma dhāraṇī
consensus_date: None
basis: no recension-specific scholarship in library
recension_note: A dhāraṇī text known only from the Kanjur and the DSBC digital edition (no separate printed critical edition). Library mentions of 'Nārāyaṇaparipṛcchā' are passing doctrinal citations, not datings, and nothing addresses the Mahāmāyāvijayavāhinī dhāraṇī recension. As a dhāraṇī it likely belongs to the broad mid-1st-millennium dhāraṇī literature, but no source-grounded date is available; left undated.
note: Kanjur dhāraṇī; no recension scholarship; left undated

title: Ṣaṇmukhīdhāraṇī
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (before 645 CE terminus ante quem)
basis: Mizuno, Buddhist Sutras (1982); Abbott on the Ṣaṇmukhī-dhāraṇī-sūtra-śāstra attributed to Vasubandhu
source_file: cleaned_BUDDHIST SUTRAS. Origin, Development, Transmission_ KOGEN MIZUNO, 1982.txt
recension_note: The root dhāraṇī sūtra has a terminus ante quem of 645 CE from Xuanzang's Chinese translation (Liu-men tuoluoni jing). A *Ṣaṇmukhī-dhāraṇī-sūtra-śāstra commentary is traditionally ascribed to Vasubandhu, which—if authentic—would push the root text into the 4th-5th c., but that attribution is uncertain. The DSBC Sanskrit text is the extant Sanskrit recension; no printed critical edition or recension-specific date is stated, so confidence is low.
note: cur_med 481 sits within the pre-645 window; not off by >75y.

title: Aparimitāyunāmamahāyānasūtra
consensus_date: 8th-9th c. CE (dhāraṇī sūtra recension; extant Nepalese Sanskrit MS later)
basis: Mei Ching Hsuan 2009; Viehbeck (Dunhuang mass-production early-mid 9th c.); Sen 2003 (first Chinese, Dharmadeva 973-980)
source_file: cleaned_Development of Pho ba Liturgy in Medieval Tibet_Mei Ching Hsuan, 2009.txt
recension_note: Same short Aparimitāyurjñāna dhāraṇī sūtra as bsu003_u. Our edition is Walleser's 1916 edition explicitly 'nach einer nepalesischen Sanskrit-Handschrift' (a Nepalese MS, physically late-medieval). The text-content recension is the 8th-9th c. dhāraṇī sūtra; terminus ante quem from the 820s-840s Tibetan Dunhuang copies and the Fangshan engraving. Not the larger Sukhāvatīvyūha/Wuliangshou.
note: Late proto-tantric dharani sutra, 8th-9th c.; median 510 far too early. Edited from a Nepalese MS, recension content 8th-9th c.

title: Bhaiṣajyaguruvaidūryaprabharājasūtra
consensus_date: extant Sanskrit (Gilgit-derived) recension, c. 5th-7th c. CE (core 'early centuries CE')
basis: Buswell & Lopez, Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism (2014) s.v. Bhaiṣajyagurusūtra; Nakamura (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism_Buswell & Lopez 2014_.txt
recension_note: Same text/recension as bsu012_u (DSBC presentation of Vaidya's Mahāyāna-sūtra-saṃgraha edition, ultimately the Gilgit-deposit Sanskrit). Developed pure-land/dhāraṇī sūtra; Chinese witnesses 457/615/650 CE; the surviving Sanskrit is the Gilgit recension (6th-7th c.). Dating the extant Sanskrit recension, c. 5th-7th c., not the 'early centuries CE' core.
note: Pure-land/dhāraṇī sūtra; Gilgit Sanskrit recension 6th-7th c.; recension far later than median 274.

title: Amoghapāśakalparāja Part VI
consensus_date: late 7th-early 8th c. CE (extant long Sanskrit Kalparāja)
basis: Orzech/Sørensen/Payne, Esoteric Buddhism and the Tantras in East Asia (2011): the long version among 7th-8th c. esoteric texts
source_file: cleaned_Esoteric Buddhism and the Tantras in East Asia_Charles D. Orzech,Henrik H. Sørensen, Richard K. Payne, 2011.txt
recension_note: The short Amoghapāśahṛdaya was rendered to Chinese already in the late 6th c., but our text is the LARGE Amoghapāśakalparāja (Kimura transcription of the expanded Sanskrit MS). This long Kalparāja was first fully translated into Chinese by Bodhiruci in 707-709 CE; the extant Sanskrit of the expanded ritual compendium (incl. Part VI) belongs to the late 7th-early 8th c. esoteric-manual stratum, decades later than the early hṛdaya core. Current median 602 dates the early layer, not our recension.
note: Long Kalparāja (our ed.) = late 7th-early 8th c.; 602 mis-dates the early hṛdaya layer by >75y.

title: Amoghapāśakalparāja Part VII
consensus_date: mid-to-late 7th-early 8th c. CE (extant Sanskrit Mahā-kalparāja)
basis: Davidson, 'Some Observations on an Uṣṇīṣa Abhiṣeka Rite'
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Some Observations on an Uṣṇīṣa Abhiṣeka rite.txt
recension_note: The large Amoghapāśa-mahā-kalparāja partly survives in Sanskrit (one of the few early tantric works extant in Sanskrit). Chinese translations: 3-roll version by Manicintana 693 CE, full kalparāja by Bodhiruci 707 CE, giving a terminus ante quem. Davidson dates the extant compilation mid-late 7th c.; the full text was complete by 707. We date this recension (the long kalparāja from which Part VII comes), not a putative shorter core.
note: 7th/early-8th c. kalparāja; cur_med 673 accurate.

title: Dhāraṇīsaṃgraha
consensus_date: post-11th c. CE Nepalese compilation (heterogeneous; component dhāraṇīs 5th-10th c.)
basis: Sanchez, The Indian Buddhist Dhāraṇī (dhāraṇī-saṃgraha genre); Jinah Kim, Receptacle of the Sacred (Nepalese MS tradition)
source_file: cleaned_Sanchez_The Indian Buddhist Dhāraṇī_An Introduction to its History, Meanings and Functions.txt
recension_note: Not a single composition but a compilation/anthology of dhāraṇīs of 'wide diffusion in India, Nepal, and Tibet' (Sanchez). Our edition (Bhosekar/NMM 2017) is a modern edition of a Nepalese dhāraṇī-saṃgraha; such Sanskrit collections belong to the Nepalese palm-leaf/paper manuscript tradition and are characteristically medieval-to-late-medieval compilations (post-11th c.), even though many of their component dhāraṇīs (Uṣṇīṣavijayā, Pañcarakṣā material, etc.) are individually 5th-10th c. Date the COLLECTION (later) not the oldest constituent spell. No study of this exact collection in the library; bounds approximate.
note: cur_med 634 is the rough age of some component dhāraṇīs, not of the saṃgraha-recension, which is a later Nepalese compilation; off by >75y but corrected date is only low confidence.

title: Śrīguhyasamājatantra
consensus_date: mid-8th to early 9th c. CE (received Ārya-school recension)
basis: Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2002); Bailey/Dalton (early 9th c.)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
recension_note: Bhattacharyya's GOS edition (1931) gives the eighteen-chapter received tantra later systematised by the Ārya school. Some scholars place a late-7th/early-8th c. core (Bogin/Quintman), Davidson the composition mid-8th c., Dalton/Bailey final editing early 9th c. We date the extant edited recension (mid-8th-early-9th c.), not the hypothetical earliest core.
note: cur_med 793 within mid-8th-early-9th c. consensus for the received tantra.

title: Āryaśrīgrahamātṛkā nāma dhāraṇī
consensus_date: 8th-10th c. CE (developed esoteric/dhāraṇī recension; Newar Saptavāra tradition)
basis: Boschung & Wessels-Mevissen (eds.) 2012 on navagraha/Grahamātṛkā cult; Grönbold, Saptavāra (Dhāraṇī collection from Nepal); Yoshizaki, Newar Buddhism (Grahamātṛkā = Saptavāra dhāraṇī)
source_file: cleaned_YOSHIZAKI_Newar Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Grahamātṛkā ('Mother of the Planets/Seizers') dhāraṇī belongs to the navagraha astral cult and the Saptavāra dhāraṇī corpus of developed esoteric Buddhism. The navagraha concept was established by c. 600 CE but the Grahamātṛkā dhāraṇī as such is a Pāla-period esoteric composition, included in the Tibetan canon (kriyātantra) and transmitted in the Newar Saptavāra collection. Our dsbc Sanskrit text reflects the Nepalese dhāraṇī recension, not any early layer.
note: Esoteric astral dhāraṇī; median 693 sits inside 8th-10th c. window, acceptable.

title: Namaskāraikaviṃśatistotra (Praise in Twenty-one Homages to Tārā)
consensus_date: 8th c. CE (root praise embedded in the Tārā tantra)
basis: Willson, In Praise of Tārā, 1992 (Tārā cult/tantra; Tārā takes saviouress role 7th c., spreads 8th c. onward)
source_file: cleaned_In Praise of Tara. Songs to the Saviouress_Martin Willson  1992.txt
recension_note: The Twenty-one Homages is the famous praise preserved in Sanskrit inside the Tārā tantra ('Origin of Tārā' / Sarvatathāgatamātṛtārā cycle). The Tārā cult emerges 6th-7th c. and the tantra spreads from the 8th c.; the praise belongs to that 7th-8th-c. tantric layer (Anuttarayoga commentaries are later). Our edition (Pandey, Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha, from de Blonay 1895) gives the Sanskrit of this tantra-embedded stotra. Date refers to the praise/tantra layer, 8th c.
note: cur_med 840 (nb600/na1100) brackets an 8th-c. date; left as-is.

title: Sarvatathāgatatattvasaṅgraha [Sarvatathāgatatattvasaṃgraha]
consensus_date: late 7th-late 8th c. CE (developed/largest Sanskrit recension; Lokesh Chandra ed. from Nepalese MS)
basis: Matsunaga, History of Tantric Buddhism in India; corroborated by Germano/Eimer (Many Canons) and Brunnhölzl 2013/2015 ('late seventh to eighth century')
source_file: cleaned_Matsunaga.History-Of-Tantric-Buddhism-In-India.txt
recension_note: Matsunaga distinguishes the SHORTEST STTS (existed in S. India ~700 CE, known to Vajrabodhi; composed end of 7th c.) from the LARGEST/developed text, completed by the end of the 8th c. (commented on by Śākyamitra and Ānandagarbha, active latter half of 8th c.). Lokesh Chandra's 1987 Sanskrit edition is based on the Nepalese MS of this developed/expanded recension, so our text is later than the 723 CE Vajrabodhi Chinese version of the early form. Dated to the developed Sanskrit recension, 8th c.
note: cur_med 694 plausible for early form but our edition is the developed recension; range 700-800 brackets it; median ~750.

title: Sarvadurgatipariśodhana Tantra
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension; Skorupski ed.)
basis: R. M. Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2003); ed. T. Skorupski (1983)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
recension_note: Davidson places the Sarvadurgatipariśodhana within the esoteric (kriyā/yoga) canon already in use in the 8th c.; Buddhaguhya (mid-8th c.) wrote a commentary on it, giving a firm terminus ante quem. Skorupski's edition prints the Sanskrit + Tibetan; the underlying tantra is therefore a 7th-8th-c. composition. cur_med (650) sits at the early edge of this window.
note: 8th-c. esoteric canon, Buddhaguhya commentary = TAQ; cur_med fine.

title: Tārābhaṭṭārikānāmāṣṭottaraśatakastotra
consensus_date: late 7th-8th c. CE (Tārāmūlakalpa-associated stotra)
basis: Beyer, Cult/Magic Ritual of Tārā; Shaw, Buddhist Goddesses of India (Tārāmūlakalpa ca. 7th c.)
source_file: cleaned_Magic Ritual in Tibet. The Cult of Tara_Stephan Beyer, 1988.txt
recension_note: The 108-names-of-Tārā stotra is embedded in/associated with the Tārāmūlakalpa, dated ca. 7th c. (Shaw); listed in an 8th-c. catalogue with two Chinese translations of the prologue, giving a terminus ante quem for the stotra in the 8th c. Our edition is the Sanskrit stotra in Pāṇḍeya's Bauddha Stotra Saṃgraha (a later Nepalese-tradition collection); we date the stotra's recension to late 7th-8th c.
note: Tārā cult / Tārāmūlakalpa horizon; cur_med within range, low confidence.

title: Vajravidāraṇī-dhāraṇī
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (extant recension; Iwamoto 1937 ed.)
basis: R. M. Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2003); ed. Y. Iwamoto, Kleinere Dhāraṇī-Texte (1937)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
recension_note: Davidson lists the Vajravidāraṇa among the esoteric texts in use in the 8th c. and notes commentaries from Buddhaguhya (mid-8th c.) onward, and that it 'may have informed the Vajraśekhara' — placing this dhāraṇī in the 7th-8th c. Iwamoto's 1937 edition prints the short Sanskrit text. cur_med (650) is within the accepted window.
note: In 8th-c. esoteric canon; Buddhaguhya commentary; cur_med fine.

title: Vajravidāraṇā-dhāraṇī
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (kriyā/proto-tantric dhāraṇī; pre-mature-tantra)
basis: Lessing & Wayman, Introduction to the Buddhist Tantric Systems (mKhas grub rje), 1968; Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2002)
source_file: cleaned_Introduction to the Buddhist Tantric Systems_Lessing & Wayman, 1968.txt
recension_note: Short kriyā-class dhāraṇī (Toh. 750), of the proto-tantric dhāraṇī stratum predating the mature yoga/anuttara tantras. It is already cited and the basis of a sādhana ascribed to Buddhaguhya (fl. c. 760s; To. 2926), giving a terminus ante quem of c. 8th c.; the extant Sanskrit (Sarnath/Dhīḥ edition from Nepalese-transmitted MSS) reflects this 6th-7th c. dhāraṇī, not any later expanded *vaipulyatantra version.
note: Buddhaguhya TAQ ~8th c.; dhāraṇī itself 6th-7th c. cur_med 650 fits.

title: Ḍākārṇavatantra (ch. 15) with Jayasena's Ratnapadmarāganidhi
consensus_date: 10th-11th c. CE (late Cakrasaṃvara-cycle yoginī tantra)
basis: Williams/Tribe/Wynne 2000 (Ḍākārṇava placed in the Yoginī/Cakrasaṃvara cycle); Gray 2007 & Arnold (Cakrasaṃvara base text late 8th/early 9th c., Ḍākārṇava a later expansion); Sugiki 2022 ed.
source_file: cleaned_Buddhist Thought. A Complete Introduction to the Indian Tradition_Paul Williams, Anthony J. Tribe, Alexander Wynne, 2000.txt
recension_note: The Ḍākārṇava-mahāyoginītantra is one of the latest and most expanded members of the Cakrasaṃvara/yoginī-tantra corpus, building on the Laghusaṃvara base (late 8th/early 9th c., Gray/Arnold). It survives in a vernacular-tinged Sanskrit (Snellgrove). Our edition is Sugiki's 2022 critical edition of ch. 15 plus Jayasena's later meditation manual (Ratnapadmarāganidhi), so the manual layer is later still. Conservatively a 10th-11th c. tantra; the Padmavajra commentary cited by Davidson is sometimes dated 9th c. but more recent scholarship places this material later.
note: Late yogini tantra, 10th-11th c. Workitem has no real median/bounds (510 is an index placeholder, nb/na blank), so flag_off not meaningfully computable; date given is for the extant Sanskrit recension.

title: Ratnāvalī (Kamalanātha's commentary on the Hevajratantra)
consensus_date: 11th-12th c. CE (Vikramaśīla-milieu Hevajra commentary)
basis: Szántó, Catuṣpīṭha studies / Before a Critical Edition of the Sampuṭa (Kamalanātha listed among Vikramaśīla-corpus Hevajra exegetes alongside Ratnākaraśānti, Abhayākaragupta)
source_file: cleaned_Szanto_Selected  Chapters  from the Catuṣpīṭhatantra_1.txt
recension_note: This item is the COMMENTARY (Ratnāvalī) by Kamalanātha, NOT the root Hevajratantra (which is late 8th-early 10th c.). Kamalanātha is a late, undated Hevajra exegete, preserved only in Nepalese MS (Kaiser Library), never translated into Tibetan, grouped with the Vikramaśīla scholastic Hevajra commentators (Ratnākaraśānti, Abhayākaragupta) of the 11th-12th c. Conlon 2023 is the editio princeps. Date is the commentary, c. 11th-12th c., considerably later than the root tantra.
note: cur_med 820 dates the root tantra, not Kamalanātha's late commentary; ~250-350y too early.

title: Mahāmāyātantra (Toh 425)
consensus_date: late 8th-9th c. CE (yoginītantra)
basis: Szántó, Catuṣpīṭha studies (Mahāmāyā corpus, Guṇavatī of Ratnākaraśānti); general yoginītantra chronology (Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism)
source_file: cleaned_Szanto_Selected  Chapters  from the Catuṣpīṭhatantra_1.txt
recension_note: The original Sanskrit Mahāmāyātantra is lost; the 84000 translation (Damron 2014) reconstructs the text from the Tibetan Kangyur and from Sanskrit MSS of Ratnākaraśānti's Guṇavatī commentary (Ratnākaraśānti fl. c. 1000). The tantra itself is a yoginītantra of the same milieu as the Hevajra, conventionally placed late 8th-9th c. CE; the surviving Sanskrit is embedded only in the later (c. 1000) commentary. Library lacks a recension-specific date, hence low confidence.
note: 8th-9th c. yoginītantra; cur_med 816 within plausible range.

title: Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa
consensus_date: 8th c. CE (latest strata of the extant Śāstrī recension)
basis: Glenn Wallis, Mediating the Power of Buddhas (2002); Ronald Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2002); M. Delhey, 'Textual Sources of the Mañjuśriyamūlakalpa'
source_file: cleaned_Mediating the Power of Buddhas. Ritual in the Manjusrimulakalpa_Glenn Wallis, 2002.txt
recension_note: The MMK is a composite text that grew in layers. Our text is Gaṇapati Śāstrī's editio princeps (Trivandrum, 1920-25), based on the bulky Nepalese palm-leaf MS that contains far more chapters than the partial early-8th-c. Chinese translations or the 11th-c. Tibetan version. Date OUR full recension by its latest datable strata: Wallis's working estimate is the 8th c. (Pāla east India); Davidson dates the historical 'Prophecy of Kings' (rājavyākaraṇa) chapter to c. 750 CE, since it names the Pāla kings Gopāla and Dharmapāla (mid/late 8th c.). The 8th-c. Chinese renderings cover only some chapters and bound only an earlier/partial version, not the full Śāstrī Sanskrit text.
note: Full extant (Śāstrī/Nepalese) recension reaches 8th c. (Pāla king-list); cur_med 586 ~200y too early.

title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti (Davidson 1981 ed.)
consensus_date: late 8th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension)
basis: Davidson, 'The Litany of Names of Mañjuśrī' (1981) and Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2002); R. Tribe, thesis on Vilāsavajra's NAS commentary (Oxford 1994)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
recension_note: The Nāmasaṃgīti was commented on by Vilāsavajra (Nāmamantrārthāvalokinī), an eighth/early-ninth-century master, giving a terminus ante quem of c. 800; it is generally placed in the late 8th c. and entered Tibet in the early-transmission period. Davidson's 1981 Sanskrit edition is this late-8th-c. recension. There is no demonstrable earlier layer for this developed tantric litany, so root = recension here.
note: NAS is late 8th c. (Vilāsavajra TAQ ~800), not mid-7th. cur_med 658 off by ~110y -> corrected to 750-800.

title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti (Minaev 1887 ed.)
consensus_date: late 8th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension)
basis: Davidson, 'The Litany of Names of Mañjuśrī' (1981) and Indian Esoteric Buddhism (2002); R. Tribe, thesis on Vilāsavajra's NAS commentary (Oxford 1994)
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Indian Esoteric Buddhism_ A Social History of the Tantric Movement.txt
recension_note: Same text as the Davidson item; Minaev (1887) is the older Sanskrit editio princeps of the identical late-8th-c. tantric Nāmasaṃgīti. Dated by Vilāsavajra's commentary (TAQ c. 800). The edition is 19th-c. but the recension it represents is the late-8th-c. Indian one.
note: Same NAS recension, late 8th c.; cur_med 667 off by ~100y -> corrected to 750-800.

title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti (Āryamañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti)
consensus_date: late 8th c. CE (composition of the tantra; transmitted by Nepalese Sanskrit MSS)
basis: Davidson 1981 ('The Litany of Names of Mañjuśrī'), cited in Davidson, Tibetan Renaissance; Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980) on its Vajrayāna/tantra setting
source_file: cleaned_Davidson_Tibetan Renaissance.txt
recension_note: The Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti is a Yoga/anuttarayoga-class tantra embedded in the Māyājāla cycle; Mañjuśrī here is an esoteric deity (vāgīśvara), placing composition in the mature tantric period (late 8th c. CE), well after the early-medieval cut implied by the current median of 523. Our edition (Mukherji 1963) prints the Nepalese Sanskrit recension together with the Tibetan; no earlier Sanskrit layer is at issue.
note: Tantric Mañjuśrī litany; late 8th c. consensus, current median ~250y too early

title: Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti (Mañjuśrījñānasattvasya paramārthā nāmasaṃgīti)
consensus_date: c. 700 CE (extant Sanskrit recension; text current by late 8th c.)
basis: Anthony Tribe, 'Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti' (Brill's Encyclopedia of Buddhism, entry); Tribe, Names of Wisdom (1994)
source_file: cleaned_Tribe_Mañjuśrīnāmasaṃgīti.txt
recension_note: Dated to the tantric/proto-mahāyoga Nāmasaṃgīti as transmitted in Sanskrit: composed ~700 CE and demonstrably current by the late 8th c., when Vilāsavajra's Sanskrit commentary (latter part of the 8th c.) was written on it. The Vira & Chandra 1966 edition prints this developed Sanskrit recension, not any hypothetical earlier core; the 591 CE median predates the text's attested existence.
note: Tantric Nāmasaṃgīti, ~700 CE; commentaries by late 8th c. (Vilāsavajra).

title: Siddhayogeśvarīmata(tantra)
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (early Vidyāpīṭha Śaiva tantra; Törzsök critical ed.)
basis: A. Sanderson, Śaivism and the Tantric Traditions; cf. J. Törzsök, ed. (Oxford D.Phil. 1999); A. McKay (ed.), History of Tibet vol. 2 (2003)
source_file: cleaned_History of Tibet.Vol2_ Alex McKay, 2003_OCR.txt
recension_note: This is a Śaiva Vidyāpīṭha (Bhairava/Yoginī) tantra, not a Buddhist text, edited by Törzsök (1999) from the Nepalese MSS as one of the EARLIEST Vidyāpīṭha scriptures. Sanderson places the Vidyāpīṭha from the 8th c. (possibly earlier); the Siddhayogeśvarīmata clearly predates the Buddhist Cakrasaṃvara cycle, which borrows from it 'virtually unchanged word-for-word', so it sits at the early (7th-8th c.) end of the Vidyāpīṭha. cur_med (643) already falls in this window.
note: Early Vidyāpīṭha Śaiva tantra, 7th-8th c.; cur_med within range.

title: Tantrasadbhāva (Śaiva Trika tantra)
consensus_date: 9th-11th c. CE (developed Trika Vidyāpīṭha text; 11th-c. Nepalese Śaiva MS recension edited)
basis: Sanderson (cited in Gray, Cakrasamvara Tantra 2007; English, Vajrayoginī); Bang 2018 edition statement
source_file: cleaned_Buddhism. Critical Concepts in Religious - Volume 6_Paul Williams.txt
recension_note: NOT a Buddhist text: this is a Śaiva Trika Vidyāpīṭha scripture; Buddhist Cakrasamvara/yoginītantras borrowed from it (Sanderson). Bang's edition is explicitly 'based on the tradition of 11th-century Śaiva Sanskrit manuscripts in Nepal'. We date the developed Trika scripture (Sanderson's Vidyāpīṭha corpus, ca. 9th-10th c.) as transmitted in the 11th-c. Nepalese recension, not any hypothetical earlier core.
note: Śaiva Trika tantra; recension centuries later than cur_med 667; corrected to ~950.

title: Vajramahābhairava-tantra (Vajrabhairava Tantra)
consensus_date: 10th c. CE (revealed to Lalitavajra, 10th-c. Nālandā scholar)
basis: Kapstein (ed.), Buddhism between Tibet and China (tradition of Lalitavajra); Davidson, Indian Esoteric Buddhism (Yoginī/Bhairava-tantra milieu); Wenta 2020 ed.
source_file: cleaned_Buddhism between Tibet and China_Matthew Kapstein, ed. 2009.txt
recension_note: Highest-yoga/Father tantra of the Yamāntaka-Vajrabhairava cycle, first critically edited from Sanskrit by Wenta 2020. Tradition attributes its revelation to Lalitavajra, placed in the 10th c., and the cycle draws heavily on Śaiva Bhairava (Vidyāpīṭha) sources (Davidson), consistent with a 10th-c. emergence; the surviving Sanskrit recension Wenta edits is no earlier. Distinct from the much shorter (7-chapter) Tibetan canonical version vs. the 'three hundred chapter' legendary original. Dating the extant Sanskrit recension, not the legendary Ur-text.
note: cur_med 637 (7th c.) far too early for a Yoginī/Bhairava-class tantra revealed to a 10th-c. figure; corrected to 10th c. CE (medium confidence).

title: Ahorātravratakathā 1
consensus_date: later-medieval Nepalese recension (12th-17th c. CE)
basis: Bendall, Catalogue of Buddhist Skt MSS, Cambridge (1883); Tuladhar-Douglas, Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal (2006); Handurukande ed. 2000
source_file: cleaned_Bendall_Catalogue of the Buddhist Sanskrit manuscripts in the University Library, Cambridge.txt
recension_note: A Newar caitya-worship vratakathā transmitted within/alongside the Nepalese Bhadrakalpāvadāna avadānamālā cycle; Handurukande edited it from Nepalese MSS as one of 'Three Sanskrit Texts on Caitya Worship in Relation to the Ahorātravrata'. This is medieval Newar Sanskrit vrata literature, not an early-8th-c. canonical text; the library gives no firm composition date, only the medieval-Nepalese genre context.
note: Newar vratakathā; cur_med 733 anachronistically early but no recension-specific date in library, low confidence so not counted as a corrected date

title: Ahorātravratakathā 2
consensus_date: post-1200 CE (Nepalese Buddhist Sanskrit vrata/avadāna literature)
basis: Tuladhar-Douglas, Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal
source_file: cleaned_Tuladhar-Douglas_Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal.txt
recension_note: The Ahorātravratakathā ('story of the day-and-night vow') is a Newar Buddhist vrata-kathā/vrata-avadāna text, a genre composed in Nepal in Sanskrit after c. 1200 CE (with later congeners shifting into Newari). The dsbc Sanskrit text is a late medieval/early modern Nepalese composition; there is no early Indic recension. Range extended toward the early modern Newar manuscript tradition.
note: cur_med 707 is centuries too early (>500y): this is late NBS vrata literature, not an early sūtra. Corrected to post-1200 CE; median ~1450.

title: Ajitasenavyākaraṇa
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: von Hinüber (1986/7) on the Gilgit colophons, via Neelis 2011; Schopen 1997; Nakamura 1980
source_file: cleaned_Neelis_Long-distance Trade and the Transmission of Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Identical text to bsu006_u; GRETIL input by Klaus Wille from the same Dutt/Gilgit edition. The dated Sanskrit witness is the Gilgit manuscript (6th-7th c.); the earlier Kashmiri composition layer is not what our edition reproduces.
note: Same Gilgit recension as item above; median 501 too early for the Gilgit codex our edition reproduces.

title: Āmoghapāśanāmahṛdayasūtra [Āryāmoghapāśanāmahṛdayasūtra]
consensus_date: 6th c. CE (before 587 CE; extant Sanskrit recension)
basis: Buswell & Lopez, Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism (2014); Meisezahl 1962 (Reiunji Sanskrit MS)
source_file: cleaned_Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism_Buswell & Lopez 2014_.txt
recension_note: This is the short Amoghapāśahṛdaya(sūtra), the hṛdaya/dhāraṇī text (= first chapter of the larger Amoghapāśakalparāja), not the kalparāja itself. Amoghapāśa worship developed in India in the 6th c.; the 587 CE Chinese translation by Jñānagupta gives a firm terminus ante quem. The extant Sanskrit survives in the Gilgit manuscript and in later Nepalese recensions; this item is the Nepalese recension edited in Dhīḥ 38 (2004). The hṛdaya text is textually conservative, so the consensus 6th-c. composition date applies, with the Nepalese MS being a later copy of the same short scripture.
note: cur_med 496 is within ~75y of the late-6th-c. center; not flagged.

title: Dharmasamuccaya [Saddharmasmṛtyupasthānakārikā]
consensus_date: 5th-7th c. CE (Avalokitasiṃha's verse compilation; Nepalese MSS)
basis: Daniel M. Stuart, A Less Traveled Path (2012), §1.2-1.3 on the Dharmasamuccaya as a later extract; Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Less Traveled Path. Meditation and Textual Practice in Saddharmasmrtyupasthana (yogacara)_Daniel Malinowski Stuart, 2012.txt
recension_note: NOT the Saddharmasmṛtyupasthāna itself but Avalokitasiṃha's LATER re-compilation of its verses (saddharmasmṛtyupasthānasūtra...gāthāḥ samuddhariṣyāmi), surviving only in late Nepalese MSS (Caube ed.). It postdates the 3rd/4th-c. Saddhsu and was 'well known throughout the Buddhist world for many centuries after.' Date refers to Avalokitasiṃha's compilation, later than the source sūtra; library gives no firm date for him, hence low confidence.
note: cur_med 343 reflects the source sūtra, not Avalokitasiṃha's later extract; range nudged later, but no firm date to justify >75y flag.

title: Āmoghapāśanāmahṛdaya sūtra [Ārya amoghapāśahṛdaya nāma mahāyānasūtra]
consensus_date: 6th c. CE (before 587 CE; extant Nepalese Sanskrit recension)
basis: Buswell & Lopez, Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism (2014); Meisezahl 1962; ed. Shakya, NIEM Nepal 1988
source_file: cleaned_Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism_Buswell & Lopez 2014_.txt
recension_note: Same short Amoghapāśahṛdaya scripture as the preceding item, here in the Nepalese recension edited by M. B. Shakya (NIEM 1988, via DSBC). The hṛdaya text was composed in the 6th c. (terminus ante quem 587 CE, Jñānagupta's Chinese translation); the Shakya edition rests on later Nepalese manuscripts of this same conservative dhāraṇī text, not on the larger Amoghapāśakalparāja. Date assigned is the 6th-c. composition of the hṛdaya, with the Nepalese MS being a later witness.
note: cur_med 497 is within ~75y of the late-6th-c. center; not flagged.

title: Ekādaśamukha
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Ekādaśamukha in N. Dutt, Gilgit Manuscripts vol. I (1939); Gilgit MS context per Nakamura (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Eleven-faced Avalokiteśvara dhāraṇī sūtra; our text is Dutt's edition of the Gilgit Sanskrit manuscript. The first Chinese translation (Yaśogupta, later 6th c.) gives a terminus ante quem; the Gilgit Sanskrit recension we edit dates to the 5th-6th c. dhāraṇī-sūtra horizon. Library coverage is thin (passing mentions only).
note: Sparse evidence; date keyed to dhāraṇī-sūtra genre and Gilgit recension. cur_med 444 just below window but within tolerance.

title: Patna Dharmapada
consensus_date: early centuries CE (BHS Dharmapada anthology; sectarian affiliation debated, prob. Sāmmitīya-related)
basis: von Hinüber, Das ältere Mittelindisch im Überblick (2001); Cone, Patna Dharmapada Part I (1989)
source_file: cleaned_Altere Mittelindisch im Uberblick_Oskar von Hinüber, 2001.txt
recension_note: A Dharmapada verse-anthology in Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit / mixed Middle Indic (von Hinüber). The verse material is drawn from the common early-canonical stock (parallels in Pali Dhp and the Udānavarga), but the Patna collection as a redacted BHS anthology is a sectarian recension of the early centuries CE; the library offers no firm manuscript or composition date, so confidence is low. Cone's edition is of the single (relatively late) manuscript.
note: BHS Dharmapada anthology; current range plausible, library has no recension-specific date. Low confidence.

title: Mahāvadānasūtra
consensus_date: ca. 2nd-4th c. CE (Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit Dīrghāgama recension; Northern Turkestan/Turfan MSS)
basis: Fukita 2003 (ed., Turkestan MSS); Dhammadinna (ed.), Research on the Dīrgha-āgama (2014); Waldschmidt
source_file: cleaned_Research on the Dirgha-agama_Dhammadinna, ed., 2014.txt
recension_note: The legend of the seven past Buddhas is one of the oldest strata (Pali Mahāpadāna parallel), but our edition (Fukita) is the Sanskrit Sarvāstivāda Dīrghāgama recension reconstructed from Northern Turkestan/Turfan MSS (script 5th-8th c.). The Sanskritized Sarvāstivāda āgama recension is an early-centuries-CE redaction, considerably later than the underlying Middle-Indic discourse; date the Sanskrit recension, not the Pali-parallel layer.
note: Median 82 (range -100 to 200) dates the early discourse layer; the extant Sanskrit Sarvāstivāda recension is 2nd-4th c. CE. Off by >75y.

title: Mahāvastu-avadāna
consensus_date: compiled work; latest layer 4th c. CE or later (nucleus 2nd c. BCE)
basis: Winternitz, History of Indian Literature II (1933); cf. Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_History of Indian Literature II_MAURICE WINTERNITZ, 1933.txt
recension_note: The Mahāvastu (Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda Vinaya prologue) is a multi-layered compilation: nucleus ca. 2nd c. BCE, but the work as transmitted (Senart's edition, our text) contains demonstrably late material — references to the Huns and to Chinese language/script — bringing the latest redaction down to the 4th c. CE 'and perhaps still later.' Per the cardinal rule we date the compiled recension's latest layer (4th c.+), not the early nucleus; the current median of 226 (range -200 to 400) brackets nucleus-to-final-layer and is within tolerance of the 4th-c. final redaction.
note: Senart compiled recension; final layer 4th c. CE; current median's range already spans to 400, so not off by >75y from the redaction.

title: Mahāparinirvāṇasūtra
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Mūlasarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension, Turfan MSS)
basis: Lamotte (MSV 4th-5th c., via Shaw 2006); Waldschmidt 1950-51 ed. of the MSV Turfan recension
source_file: cleaned_Perspectives on Satipatthana_Analayo, 2014.txt
recension_note: This is the mainstream (Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda) Mahāparinirvāṇasūtra, NOT the Mahāyāna MPNS. Waldschmidt's Sanskrit text is reconstructed from Turfan fragments of the Mūlasarvāstivādin recension. The Pāli Mahāparinibbāna-sutta and early Chinese parallels preserve a far older sūtra core, but our extant Sanskrit MSV recension belongs to the MSV corpus that Lamotte dates to the 4th-5th c. CE (Turfan MSS ~5th-6th c.). Date the MSV Sanskrit recension, not the early āgama layer.
note: cur_med 269 too early; MSV Sanskrit recension 4th-5th c. CE (~400). Corrected to 350-450.

title: Mahāsudarśanāvadāna
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: Revire 2016 (citing Matsumura 1988) on its source MS; Schopen 1997 and Neelis on the dating of the Gilgit manuscripts (mid-7th c. palaeographic anchor)
source_file: cleaned_Enthroned Buddha in Majesty. Iconological Study_Nicolas Revire, 2016.txt
recension_note: Matsumura's 1988 edition is based on the Gilgit Sanskrit manuscript (a Mūlasarvāstivāda recasting of the Mahāsudarśana legend, ultimately paralleling the Pali Mahāsuddassana-sutta of the Dīgha-nikāya). DATE OUR RECENSION, not the early Pali layer: the Gilgit codices are dated 6th-7th c. CE (Neelis: late-Brāhmī→Proto-Śārada transition mid-7th c.; Williams: 6th c.; Schopen: 'still later' than 5th-c. Central Asian MSS). Current median 537 sits just inside the band.
note: Gilgit MSV recension 6th-7th c.; Pali Mahāsuddassana parallel far older but not our text

title: Kaṭhināvadāna
consensus_date: None
basis: Tatelman, The Legend of the Buddha's Wife in the Bhadrakalpāvadāna (on the Nepalese avadāna/avadānamālā corpus); Hunter, Life of B.H. Hodgson (Nepalese MS list)
source_file: cleaned_Hunter_Life of Brian Houghton Hodgson_British resident at the court of Nepal.txt
recension_note: An avadāna transmitted only in the Nepalese (Hodgson) Sanskrit MS collection and edited by Degener (1990, not in library). The Newar avadāna/avadānamālā narrative literature was largely composed/recompiled medievally (Tatelman: extensive Sanskrit narrative production 14th-17th c.), retelling earlier Indian (incl. MSV Kaṭhinavastu) material. Library has no recension-specific date for Degener's edited text, so no defensible year is set; current_median 654 is not corroborated.
note: No recension-specific scholarship in library; Nepalese avadāna, likely medieval. Date left null.

title: Mahāsattvamaṇicūḍamahārājabodhisattvāvadāna
consensus_date: c. 7th-8th c. CE (prose Maṇicūḍāvadāna, Nepalese MSS)
basis: Ohnuma, Head, Eyes, Flesh and Blood (2007), citing Hahn 1992 on Gopadatta; Handurukande ed. 1967
source_file: cleaned_Head, Eyes, Flesh and Blood. Giving Away the Body in Indian Buddhist Literature_Reiko Ohnuma,2007.txt
recension_note: Handurukande's edited prose Maṇicūḍāvadāna survives only in Nepalese Sanskrit manuscripts and shares material with Gopadatta's Jātakamālā / Avadānasārasamuccaya (Gopadatta = 7th-8th c., per Khoroche/Buswell). The story was later versified by Kṣemendra (Avadānakalpalatā, c. 1052 CE), which is a still-later recasting, not our text. Library has no tighter date for our prose recension; the Gopadatta horizon gives a soft anchor.
note: No recension-specific date in library; Gopadatta horizon (7th-8th c.) consistent with cur_med 711.

title: Catuṣpariṣatsūtra
consensus_date: c. 2nd-4th c. CE (extant Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension, reconstructed from Turfan MSS)
basis: Waldschmidt, Das Catuṣpariṣatsūtra (1952-62); Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Schopen on the (Mūla)sarvāstivāda-vinaya redaction, Bones, Stones and Buddhist Monks (1997)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Canonical sūtra on the founding of the saṅgha, paralleling the Pāli and the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya (Saṅghabhedavastu). Although the narrative/material is very early (pre-Common-Era oral tradition), our edition is Waldschmidt's reconstruction of the extant SANSKRIT (Mūla-)sarvāstivāda recension from Turfan manuscripts. Date that Sanskrit codification (Kuṣāṇa-Gupta, c. 2nd-4th c.; the surviving Turfan MS copies are later, c. 5th-7th c.), NOT the earliest Pāli/oral layer.
note: cur_med 551 fits the MS copies but is too late for the Sanskrit recension; set to Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit codification.

title: Vajrasattvaniṣpādanasūtra [Vajrasattvasādhana]
consensus_date: early 9th c. CE (tantric Candrakīrti / Ārya Guhyasamāja school)
basis: David B. Gray & R. Overbey (eds.), Tantric Traditions in Transmission and Translation, 2016
source_file: cleaned_Tantric traditions in transmission and translation_David B. Gray, Ryan Richard Overbey, 2016_a.txt
recension_note: This sādhana is attributed to the TANTRIC Candrakīrti, author of the Pradīpodyotana and a figure of the Ārya (Ārya) exegetical school of the Guhyasamāja — NOT the 7th-c. Madhyamaka Candrakīrti. Gray dates Ārya-school Guhyasamāja writings to the early 9th c.; the surviving Sanskrit sādhana belongs to this esoteric/sādhana corpus, hence early 9th c., not the 7th c. implied by the cur_med (which conflates the two Candrakīrtis).
note: cur_med (639) confuses tantric Candrakīrti with Madhyamaka Candrakīrti; Ārya Guhyasamāja school is early 9th c.

title: (unidentified GRETIL e-text n3013u)
consensus_date: None
basis: no identifying metadata (no title/author/edition/URL) and no GRETIL or library match for slug 'n3013u'
recension_note: Work item carries no title, author, edition, or URL; slug 'n3013u' resolves to no GRETIL file (404) and no library reference. Cannot identify the text or its recension, so no date can be sourced.
note: Unidentifiable; left null/none per instructions (do not invent).

title: Mahāvadānasūtra
consensus_date: ca. 2nd-4th c. CE (Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit Dīrghāgama recension; Northern Turkestan/Turfan MSS)
basis: Fukita 2003 (ed., Turkestan MSS); Dhammadinna (ed.), Research on the Dīrgha-āgama (2014); Waldschmidt
source_file: cleaned_Research on the Dirgha-agama_Dhammadinna, ed., 2014.txt
recension_note: Duplicate of the Mahāvadānasūtra (same Fukita 2003 edition). The seven-Buddhas legend is an old stratum (Pali Mahāpadāna parallel), but the extant Sanskrit text is the Sarvāstivāda Dīrghāgama recension from Turfan/Northern Turkestan MSS, an early-centuries-CE Sanskritized redaction, not the Middle-Indic original layer.
note: Median 95 (range -100 to 200) dates the early discourse; extant Sanskrit Sarvāstivāda recension is 2nd-4th c. CE. Off by >75y.

title: Pāṃśupradāna-avadāna
consensus_date: 2nd c. CE (present Sanskrit form of the Aśokāvadāna; terminus ante quem 300 CE)
basis: Strong, The Legend of King Aśoka: Study and Translation of the Aśokāvadāna (1989)
source_file: cleaned_Legend of King Asoka. Study and translation of the Asokavadana_John Strong,  1989.txt
recension_note: The Pāṃśupradāna ('gift of dirt') is the opening avadāna of the Aśokāvadāna cycle, embedded in the Divyāvadāna and drawn from the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya milieu. The underlying legends are several centuries older (depicted at Sāñchī, 2nd-1st c. BCE), but the PRESENT Sanskrit form is c. 2nd c. CE (dīnāra terminus post quem after 1st c.; Chinese translation 300 CE as upper bound). Our edition draws on the Gilgit Buddhist MSS / Cowell-Neil Divyāvadāna — this extant Sanskrit recension, not the older oral layer.
note: Strong dates present Sanskrit form to 2nd c. CE; current median 239 within range though somewhat late; <75y from corrected midpoint, no flag.

title: Ratnamālāvadāna
consensus_date: mid-15th c. CE (Newar verse avadānamālā 'Garland' recension; Takahata ed.)
basis: Will Tuladhar-Douglas, Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal (2006); cf. R. Buswell, Encyclopedia of Buddhism
source_file: cleaned_Tuladhar-Douglas_Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal.txt
recension_note: The Ratnamālāvadāna is one of the anonymous verse avadānamālās ('Garland of avadānas'), which Buswell calls 'a later subgenre' retelling earlier stories 'in a distinctively Nepalese idiom'. Tuladhar-Douglas places the Garland-text genre in the mid-15th-c. Newar Buddhist renaissance. Takahata's edition is built from two MS families (a shorter and a much longer recension). Individual avadāna plots are far older, but OUR extant Sanskrit metrical collection is a late-medieval Nepalese composition, not a 7th-c. work.
note: cur_med (640) ~800y too early; Garland avadānamālā = mid-15th c. Newar Nepal.

consensus_date: None
basis: no data
recension_note: No title, author, or edition supplied (catalogue stub 'sf259_sc', K14). Could not identify the text or any recension; no scholarship located. No date assigned.
note: Unidentifiable fragment; insufficient metadata to date — cannot judge whether cur_med 420 is wrong.

title: Catuṣpariṣatsūtra fragment (SHT/Turfan SF 273)
consensus_date: Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit Dīrghāgama recension; Turfan MS, mid-first millennium CE
basis: Frauwallner, Earliest Vinaya (1956) on the Catuṣpariṣatsūtra; Eltschinger on the Sarvāstivāda Dīrghāgama as a 'relatively late' recension
source_file: cleaned_Earliest Vinaya and the Beginnings of Buddhist Literature_Erich Frauwallner_1956.txt
recension_note: SF 273 is a Turfan Sanskrit fragment of the Catuṣpariṣatsūtra (Waldschmidt's edition from Turfan-Handschriften). It belongs to the Sarvāstivāda Dīrghāgama Sanskrit recension (originally Skandhaka/Vinaya material). The narrative is very old and has Pāli parallels, but our witness is the comparatively late Sarvāstivāda Sanskrit recension, transmitted in Central Asian MSS copied roughly 5th-7th c. CE. Library lacks a precise MS date, hence low confidence.
note: Sarvāstivāda Skt Dīrghāgama recension/Turfan MS; cur_med 546 within plausible MS-copying window.

title: Sumāgadhāvadāna
consensus_date: 1052 CE (Kṣemendra's Avadānakalpalatā recension)
basis: Wood, Buddha and the Shape of Belief (2005); Speyer; ed. Vaidya, Avadāna-kalpalatā vol. II (1959), no. 93
source_file: cleaned_Buddha and the Shape of Belief. Indic Visual Jatakamalas, vol.1_Leela Aditi Wood, 2005.txt
recension_note: Our edition is no. 93 of Kṣemendra's Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā (Vaidya 1959), the Kashmiri poet Kṣemendra's verse kāvya completed ca. 1052 CE. The Sumāgadhā narrative itself is an ancient Mainstream/Sarvāstivāda story (with much older Chinese and Pāli/Sanskrit avadāna parallels), but OUR Sanskrit text is Kṣemendra's 11th-c. poetic recasting—the date to assign is 1052 CE, not the early narrative layer.
note: cur_med 493 dates the old story; our recension is Kṣemendra's 1052 CE kāvya, off by ~559y.

title: Svayambhū Purāṇa
consensus_date: 15th–16th c. CE (developed Sanskrit recension; older nucleus may be earlier)
basis: Brinkhaus 1993 stemma (versions IIB/IIIA) as summarized in Tuladhar-Douglas 2006; corroborated by Kivelä 2005
source_file: cleaned_Svayambhu. The sacred Hilltop (Kathmandu)_ Sami Kivelä_2005.txt
recension_note: The SvP exists in many versions in Brinkhaus' stemma (I–IV); the developed Sanskrit verse versions (IIB/IIIA) that carry the framing narrative belong to the same mid-15th-c. Nepalese 'Garland'-text horizon as the GKV (c. 1400–1480), while a prose nucleus (version IIA) is somewhat earlier, possibly spurred by the 1349 CE Muslim raid, and the final IIIB form shifts into Newari. The Shakya & Bajracharya 2001 (Nagarjuna Institute) printed edition gives the standard developed Sanskrit recension, hence a 15th–16th c. date; it is not the earliest layer.
note: Current median 1469 sits squarely within the consensus 15th-16th c. window for the developed Sanskrit recension; no correction needed.

title: Viśvantarāvadāna
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (extant Gilgit Sanskrit recension)
basis: von Hinüber on the Gilgit manuscripts (2014), as cited in Apple, Candrakīrti and the Lotus Sūtra; Matsumura, Four Avadānas from the Gilgit Manuscripts, 1980
source_file: cleaned_Apple_Candrakīrti and the Lotus sutra.txt
recension_note: The Vessantara/Viśvantara narrative is a very old jātaka (Pāli and earlier), but OUR text is the Sanskrit Viśvantarāvadāna edited by Kabita Das Gupta (Berlin 1978) from the Gilgit manuscript — a Mūlasarvāstivāda avadāna recension. The date here is for that physical Gilgit Sanskrit recension (Gilgit corpus conventionally 6th-7th c., some folios into the 7th-8th c.), NOT the much older Vessantara legend it transmits.
note: dated to extant Gilgit MS recension, not the old jataka layer

title: Abhisamayālaṅkāranāmaprajñāpāramitopadeśaśāstra
consensus_date: c. 350 CE (4th c. CE)
basis: Apple, Abhisamayālaṃkāra (entry/study)
source_file: cleaned_Apple_Abhisamayālaṃkāra.txt
recension_note: Root kārikā text (upadeśaśāstra) attributed to Maitreyanātha, dated c. 350 CE. Stcherbatsky-Obermiller's Bibliotheca Buddhica 23 edition prints this kārikā recension; it is the AA root verses, not later commentaries (Ārya-Vimuktisena 6th c., Haribhadra 8th c.). The text has no Chinese translation, so dating rests on the Maitreya/Asaṅga attribution and pre-Vimuktisena terminus.
note: c. 350 CE per Apple; median 351 correct.

title: Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha
consensus_date: c. 480-540 CE (Dignāga)
basis: standard consensus on Dignāga's floruit; Tucci 1947 ed. attributes to Dignāga
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: A verse summary (piṇḍārtha) of the Aṣṭasāhasrikā-prajñāpāramitā by Dignāga; the underlying sūtra is far older but our text is Dignāga's own śāstra, dated to his floruit c. 480-540 (Tucci's 1947 edition). Date is the composition of Dignāga's summary, not the sūtra.
note: Dignāga's own piṇḍārtha; cur_med 510 = midpoint. Correct.

title: Abhisamayālaṅkāra
consensus_date: c. 350 CE (4th c. CE)
basis: Apple, Abhisamayālaṃkāra (entry/study)
source_file: cleaned_Apple_Abhisamayālaṃkāra.txt
recension_note: Tripathi's edition prints the AA kārikā together with Haribhadra's Sphuṭārthā commentary. The dated object is the AA root verse text (c. 350 CE, Maitreyanātha). The accompanying Sphuṭārthā is Haribhadra's (8th c.) but is the commentary, not the root text being dated here.
note: AA root text c. 350 CE; median 352 correct (commentary is later).

title: Abhisamayālaṅkāravṛttiḥ sphuṭārthā
consensus_date: late 8th c. CE (Haribhadra)
basis: Almogi (Rong zom pa); Hopkins 2005; Apple
source_file: cleaned_Almogi_Rong zom pa's Discourses on Buddhology.txt
recension_note: The Sphuṭārthā is Haribhadra's own shorter vṛtti on the Abhisamayālaṅkāra (distinct from his longer Āloka). A single-author 8th c. composition; the Tripathi 1977 edition reproduces it directly. Dated to Haribhadra's floruit (late 8th c., active under Dharmapāla).
note: cur_med 784 fits Haribhadra fl. late 8th c.

title: Abhisamayālaṅkārāntaḥpātināṃ (saṃketānāṃ nāmāni)
consensus_date: post-350 CE; AA exegetical tradition (date uncertain)
basis: no recension-specific scholarship; derivative of the Abhisamayālaṅkāra tradition (AA dated c. 350 CE, Apple)
source_file: cleaned_Apple_Abhisamayālaṃkāra.txt
recension_note: Anonymous scholastic glossary of the technical terms/abbreviations (saṃketa) occurring WITHIN the Abhisamayālaṅkāra. By definition it cannot predate the AA root text (c. 350 CE) and belongs to its exegetical apparatus; such index/glossary works are typically considerably later (Indo-Tibetan scholastic period). The library has no dating for this specific text, so bounds are kept wide and confidence low.
note: Derivative AA glossary; no specific scholarship, wide low-confidence bounds bracket current median.

title: Pāramitāsamāsa
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Āryaśūra)
basis: Āryaśūra dated 4th c. (Joshi; Richardson); Ohnuma/Meadows on authorship
source_file: cleaned_Head, Eyes, Flesh and Blood. Giving Away the Body in Indian Buddhist Literature_Reiko Ohnuma,2007.txt
recension_note: Verse compendium ascribed to Āryaśūra (Jātakamālā author conventionally 4th c.). Ohnuma/Meadows argue the Pāramitāsamāsa author may be distinct from and later than that Āryaśūra, which would push it toward the 5th-6th c.; conventional dating keeps it ~4th-5th c. An authored śāstra (Ferrari ed. 1946), so no layered recension issue. Confidence low because of the authorship uncertainty.
note: Āryaśūra 4th c. (author possibly later per Ohnuma); cur_med 409 acceptable

title: Sāratamā [Aṣṭasāhasrikā-prajñāpāramitā pañjikā]
consensus_date: early-mid 11th c. CE (Ratnākaraśānti, ca. 970-1045)
basis: Seton, Defining Wisdom: Ratnākaraśānti's Sāratamā (2015)
source_file: cleaned_Defining Wisdom. Ratnakarasanti’s Saratama_Gregory Max Seton, 2015.txt
recension_note: This is Ratnākaraśānti's COMMENTARY (pañjikā) on the Aṣṭasāhasrikā-prajñāpāramitā, not the root sūtra (which is far older). The root Aṣṭasāhasrikā goes back to the early centuries CE, but our text is the 11th-c. commentary by Ratnākaraśānti (ed. Jaini 1979); date the commentary's author, ca. 970-1045.
note: Commentary, not root sūtra; Ratnākaraśānti's floruit.

title: Prajñāpāramitāvajracchedikāṭīkā
consensus_date: later 8th c. CE (c. 740-795)
basis: Krasser (1991); Nakamura (1980, Kamalaśīla 8th c.)
source_file: cleaned_Dharmottaras kurze Untersuchung der Gültigkeit einer Erkenntnis Laghupramanyapariksa_ HELMUT KRASSER, 1991.txt
recension_note: Kamalaśīla's ṭīkā on the Vajracchedikā Prajñāpāramitā is his own later-8th-c. commentary (c.740-795, fixed by the 793-94 Council of Tibet). It postdates the Vajracchedikā root sūtra (c.4th-5th c.) by centuries; we date the commentary layer, not the root sūtra.
note: Kamalaśīla commentary c.740-795; cur_med 767 within range

title: Navaślokī
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (Kambalapāda, contemporary of Vinītadeva)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism, 1980 (Kambalapāda's nine-verse Prajñāpāramitā treatise; contemporary of Vinītadeva, c. 690-750)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: The Navaślokī is Kambalapāda's nine-verse treatise on the Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā. Nakamura makes Kambalapāda a contemporary of Vinītadeva (c. 690-750 CE), placing him late 7th-8th c. (this Kambalapāda is to be distinguished from any earlier Ālokamālā author dated 5th-6th c. by Brunnhölzl; Nakamura ties the nine-verse work to the Vinītadeva generation). Edition is Wille's GRETIL e-text from Göttingen MS, first ed. Lindtner WZKS 26 (1982); authored work, no later recension layer.
note: 7th-8th c.; cur_med 848 (nb700/na1010) ~within 75y of an 8th-c. midpoint; not flagged.

title: Abhisamayālaṃkārāloka [Abhisamayālaṅkārāloka]
consensus_date: late 8th c. CE (Haribhadra, under Pāla king Dharmapāla)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Haribhadra (disciple of Śāntarakṣita/Buddhaśrījñāna, working at Nālandā/Vikramaśīla under Dharmapāla, r. c. 775-812) composed this large Prajñāpāramitā exegesis in the late 8th c. This is an original Sanskrit śāstra, not a translation or layered sūtra, so there is no earlier layer to confuse it with; Wogihara's edition prints this same authorial Sanskrit text. cur_med 750 is at the very early edge but within tolerance; best placement is c. 775-800.
note: Late 8th c. under Dharmapāla; cur_med 750 within ~25-50y, not flagged

title: Catuḥstavasamāsārtha [Catuḥstavasamārtha]
consensus_date: c. 8th-9th c. CE (Amṛtākara's commentary)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980), on Amṛtākara; Tucci, Minor Buddhist Texts I (1956, the edition)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: This is Amṛtākara's commentary (samāsārtha) summarizing the Catuḥstava root hymns attributed to Nāgārjuna — date the COMMENTATOR, not the much older root Stavas. Nakamura associates Amṛtākara with the late period of Indian-pandit migration to Tibet (post-imperial decline), and his exegesis fits the hymns into the bhūmi framework, pointing to a late date c. 8th-9th c.; library gives no firmer anchor, hence low confidence.
note: Commentary by Amṛtākara; soft 8th-9th c. date; cur_med 722 within a generation of lower bound, not flagged.

title: Bodhicaryāvatāra [Bodhisattvacaryāvatāra]
consensus_date: early 8th c. CE (Śāntideva); canonical/expanded recension (Vaidya ed.)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Saito (2000) on the recensions
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Vaidya's BST 12 edition prints the longer, canonical/Nepalese recension of the Bodhi(sattva)caryāvatāra, NOT the shorter Dūn-huáng recension (only c. 701 verses) studied by Saito 2000 that is generally taken as closer to Śāntideva's original. Both go back to Śāntideva (c. 650-750, so the work itself is early 8th c.); the expanded canonical recension that our edition represents is the standard transmitted text, conventionally dated to Śāntideva's lifetime / shortly after. cur_med 749 is correct.
note: cur_med 749 accurate; Vaidya = longer canonical recension, not the shorter Dūnhuáng version

title: Bodhipathapradīpa
consensus_date: 1042-1054 CE (composed by Atiśa in Tibet)
basis: Apple, Jewels of the Middle Way, 2019; Eimer, Bodhipathapradīpa, 1978
source_file: cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way  Madhyamaka Legacy of Atisa and His Early Tibetan Followers_James B  Apple, 2019.txt
recension_note: Atiśa's signature lam-rim verse text, traditionally composed at Tholing in western Tibet shortly after his arrival (1042), hence late in his life (d. 1054). Sanskrit lost; preserved/edited from Tibetan (Eimer 1978). Single-author work, no earlier recension; date narrowed to his Tibetan period.
note: Author-dated; composed in Tibet 1042-54; cur_med 1026 within lifespan, slightly early but <75y.

title: Garbhasaṅgraha
consensus_date: early-to-mid 11th c. CE (recension attributed to Atiśa)
basis: R. C. Negi (ed.), Atīśaviracitā Ekādaśagranthaḥ: Eleven Treatises by Atiśa (1992); J. B. Apple, Atiśa Dīpaṃkara (2019)
source_file: cleaned_Atisa Dipamkara  Illuminator of the Awakened Mind_James B  Apple, 2019.txt
recension_note: Our edition is Negi's 'Eleven Treatises by Atiśa', where the Garbhasaṅgraha is one of the works attributed to Dīpaṅkaraśrījñāna/Atiśa (982-1054), NOT the traditional Nāgārjuna attribution implied by cur_med 741. Date OUR edition's recension = the Atiśa corpus, hence 11th c. (Note: distinct from the tantric *Garbhasaṃgraha ascribed to Kāṇha in Davidson.)
note: Edition attributes text to Atiśa (982-1054); 11th-c. recension, ~277y later than cur_med 741 -> corrected

title: Pratītyasamutpādahṛdayavyākhyāna
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (traditional Nāgārjuna attribution; Sanskrit a modern restoration)
basis: Wayman, Buddhist Insight (1984); attribution discussed by Dragonetti 1979
source_file: cleaned_Buddhist Insight. Essay_Alex Wayman, 1984.txt
recension_note: Auto-commentary on the Pratītyasamutpādahṛdayakārikā, traditionally Nāgārjuna's own expansion (so 2nd-3rd c.); attribution questioned by Dragonetti (1979), who also weighed Śuddhamati. Crucially, NO Sanskrit MS survives: the Namdol/CIHTS (1997) 'Sanskrit text' is a modern restoration back-translated from the Tibetan Tanjur. Date refers to the original composition era, not the modern reconstruction.
note: cur_med 256 fits Nāgārjuna's era; Sanskrit edition is a 20th-c. retroversion.

title: Śikṣāsamuccayakārikā
consensus_date: late 7th-8th c. CE (Śāntideva)
basis: Wallace & Wallace, Guide to the Bodhisattva Way of Life (1997); standard dating of Śāntideva (Bu ston, Tāranātha traditions)
source_file: cleaned_Guide to the Bodhisattva way of life_Santideva_Vesna A. Wallace & B. Alan Wallace, tr , 1997.txt
recension_note: The Śikṣāsamuccayakārikā are the 27 root verses (kārikā) that structure Śāntideva's own prose Śikṣāsamuccaya (Vaidya/Bendall ed.); root verses and prose are by the same author, the Nālandā monk Śāntideva, conventionally placed late 7th-8th c. CE. No earlier layer to distinguish; the kārikās are his original frame.
note: Firmly Śāntideva, 7th-8th c.; cur_med 724 and bounds 685-763 correct.

title: Suhṛllekha
consensus_date: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (composition by Nāgārjuna; Sanskrit is a modern CIHTS restoration)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); standard dating of Nāgārjuna
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Suhrllekha survives anciently only in Tibetan and three Chinese versions (earliest Gunavarman, 431 CE) — no ancient Sanskrit recension exists. Pema Tenzin's 2002 CIHTS 'Sanskrit' edition (with Mahamati's Vyaktapada tika) is a modern restoration into Sanskrit, so the meaningful date is the COMPOSITION by Nagarjuna (c. 150-250 CE), not an extant Sanskrit recension. Terminus ante quem for the work: 431 CE Chinese translation.
note: cur_med 183 within Nāgārjuna's floruit; Sanskrit is a reconstruction, dated by composition.

title: Śūnyatāsaptati
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (Nāgārjuna; one of the authentic core works)
basis: Lindtner, Nagarjuniana (1982), authenticating 13 works incl. Śūnyatāsaptati; reported in de Jong (1987); Nāgārjuna ca. 150-250 CE
source_file: cleaned_Brief History of Buddhist Studies in Europe and America_J. M. de Jong, 1987.txt
recension_note: The Śūnyatāsaptati(kārikā) is among Nāgārjuna's authentic works (Lindtner), composed ca. 2nd-3rd c. CE. The original Sanskrit kārikās are essentially lost (transmitted in Tibetan, with the Śūnyatāsaptativṛtti); the Sempa Dorje 1985 / DSBC text is a modern Sanskrit reconstruction. We date the underlying composition, not a later Sanskrit recension (none exists). Current median 220 is appropriate.
note: Authentic Nāgārjuna kārikā; modern Sanskrit reconstruction of a 2nd-3rd c. work; median accurate.

title: Vigrahavyāvartanī
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (Nāgārjuna; authentic Madhyamaka work)
basis: Schoening (1991) on Nāgārjuna's dates; Vaidya ed. (1960) reprints the standard Sanskrit text
source_file: cleaned_Salistamba-sutra and its Indian Commentaries_Jeffrey Davis Schoening, 1991.txt
recension_note: Vaidya's 1960 edition reproduces the same single Sanskrit recension of the Vigrahavyāvartanī (essentially the Johnston-Kunst/Bhattacharya text). Authentic Nāgārjuna; no distinct later recension layer. Late Sanskrit MS transmission does not push the composition date forward.
note: Duplicate text, Vaidya recension; Nāgārjuna 2nd-3rd c.

title: Satyadvayāvatāranāma
consensus_date: early-mid 11th c. CE (Atiśa's lifetime, 982-1054)
basis: Apple, Jewels of the Middle Way, 2019; Lindtner, 'Atiśa's Introduction to the Two Truths', JIP 9 (1981)
source_file: cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way  Madhyamaka Legacy of Atisa and His Early Tibetan Followers_James B  Apple, 2019.txt
recension_note: Madhyamaka two-truths treatise by Atiśa (982-1054). Sanskrit original largely lost (preserved in Tibetan); GRETIL/DSBC is a romanized canon text. Single-author composition, no layered recension; dated to the author's own (likely later/Tibetan-period) activity.
note: Author-dated; cur_med 1026 within lifespan.

title: Madhyamakaśāstra
basis: Ruegg (via de Jong, Brief History 1987); Gethin, Foundations (1998)
source_file: cleaned_Brief History of Buddhist Studies in Europe and America_J. M. de Jong, 1987.txt
recension_note: 'Madhyamakaśāstra' here denotes the same root verses as the Mūlamadhyamakakārikā (the Vaidya 1960 edition, Madhyamakaśāstra of Nāgārjuna). It is the genuine 2nd-c. authored kārikā-text of Nāgārjuna (ca. 150–250 CE); the surrounding Prasannapadā prose is Candrakīrti's later commentary (7th c.) and is NOT what is being dated — the root śāstra is dated to its author.
note: Root text = MMK verses; 2nd c. CE. Distinguished from Candrakīrti's commentary.

title: Catuḥśatikā
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (Āryadeva)
basis: Karen C. Lang, Āryadeva on the Bodhisattva's Cultivation of Merit and Knowledge (Catuḥśataka) (1983), diss.
source_file: cleaned_Aryadeva on the Bodhisattva's Cultivation of Merit and Knowledge (India)(Catuhsataka)_Karen Christina Lang, 1983.txt
recension_note: The Catuḥśataka was composed by Āryadeva, Nāgārjuna's pupil, in the 2nd-3rd c. CE. The Sanskrit survives only in fragments (Haraprasād Shāstrī's palm-leaf MS = less than a third of the 400 verses, plus a few verses in Candrakīrti's Prasannapadā); the full text is transmitted via Tibetan and Chinese. The Bhagchandra Jain 1971 / DSBC text rests on these fragments plus reconstruction. We date the original composition; no later Sanskrit recension exists to distinguish. Current median 222 is appropriate.
note: Āryadeva, 2nd-3rd c.; Sanskrit only fragmentary + reconstruction; median accurate.

title: Bodhisattvādikarmikamārgāvatāradeśanā
consensus_date: first half 11th c. CE (Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna, 982-1054)
basis: Apple, Jewels of the Middle Way (2019); standard dates Atiśa 982-1054
source_file: cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way  Madhyamaka Legacy of Atisa and His Early Tibetan Followers_James B  Apple, 2019.txt
recension_note: Authored teaching-manual (deśanā) for the beginner bodhisattva by Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (982-1054). The DSBC e-text reproduces this single-author work; no earlier layer. Composed in Atiśa's mature career (India/Tibet), so bounds set to the latter part of his life, c. 1015-1054.
note: Atiśa 982-1054; median fine.

title: Śaraṇagamanadeśanā
consensus_date: early-mid 11th c. CE (Atiśa's lifetime, 982-1054)
basis: Apple, Jewels of the Middle Way: Madhyamaka Legacy of Atisa, 2019
source_file: cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way  Madhyamaka Legacy of Atisa and His Early Tibetan Followers_James B  Apple, 2019.txt
recension_note: Short instructional śāstra by Atiśa (982-1054); no earlier recension layer. Sanskrit original largely lost (preserved in Tibetan); GRETIL/DSBC is a romanized canon text. Dated to the author's own composition.
note: Author-dated; cur_med 1017 within lifespan.

title: Sūtrārthasamuccayopadeśa
consensus_date: first half 11th c. CE (authored text)
basis: Apple, Jewels of the Middle Way (2019); Lopez, Religions of Tibet in Practice (1997)
source_file: cleaned_Religions of Tibet in Practice_Donald S. Lopez, ed, 1997.txt
recension_note: Single-author upadeśa by Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (982-1054), a sūtra-collection/instruction work (cf. his Sūtrasamuccaya-related corpus discussed in Apple 2019). Composed in the first half of the 11th c., during Atiśa's active career (largely after his arrival in Tibet 1042). No earlier layer; the date is the author's own lifetime.
note: Atisa 982-1054; cur_med 619 wrong by >400y -> corrected to early 11th c.

title: Daśākuśalakarmapathadeśanā
consensus_date: early-mid 11th c. CE (Atiśa's lifetime, 982-1054)
basis: Apple, Jewels of the Middle Way: Madhyamaka Legacy of Atisa, 2019
source_file: cleaned_Jewels of the Middle Way  Madhyamaka Legacy of Atisa and His Early Tibetan Followers_James B  Apple, 2019.txt
recension_note: Single-authored short śāstra by Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (b. 982, d. 1054); no multi-layer recension problem. Sanskrit largely lost/preserved via Tibetan; DSBC/GRETIL e-text is a reconstruction/romanized canon text. Date is the author's own composition, almost certainly composed in his mature/Tibetan period (post-1025 return to India, Tibet 1042-54), hence late within his life.
note: Author-dated; cur_med 1016 within 982-1054 lifespan.
