title: Cittotpādasaṃvaravidhikrama
consensus_date: early-mid 11th c. CE (Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna, 982-1054)
basis: Standard dating of Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (982-1054), widely attested (e.g. Almogi; Andrews et al.)
source_file: cleaned_Andrews, Jinhua Chen, Cuilan Liu_Rules of Engagement.txt
recension_note: An original ritual manual (vidhikrama) for taking the bodhisattva-vow/cittotpāda, authored by Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna himself. No earlier layer is involved: it is an 11th-c. composition. Atiśa's productive Indian/Tibetan career was c. 1010s-1054 (he reached Tibet in 1042), so the work falls in the first half of the 11th c.
note: Atiśa 982-1054; an 11th-c. work. cur_med 546 ~480y too early.

title: Gurukriyākrama
consensus_date: first half 11th c. CE (Atiśa, c. 1010s-1054)
basis: Buswell & Lopez, Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism (2014), s.v. Atiśa (982-1054); reached Tibet 1042
source_file: cleaned_Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism_Buswell & Lopez 2014_.txt
recension_note: An authored minor sādhana/ritual work (kriyā-krama) by Atiśa Dīpaṃkaraśrījñāna (982-1054 CE), edited in Pandey's Bauddhalaghugranthasaṃgraha. As a single-author composition, root = recension: it dates to Atiśa's active scholarly period in the first half of the 11th c. (no earlier layer to distinguish).
note: Authored by Atiśa (982-1054); cur_med 583 off by ~460y.

title: Bhāvanākrama
consensus_date: later 8th c. CE (c. 740-795)
basis: Krasser, Dharmottaras Laghuprāmāṇyaparīkṣā (1991)
source_file: cleaned_Dharmottaras kurze Untersuchung der Gültigkeit einer Erkenntnis Laghupramanyapariksa_ HELMUT KRASSER, 1991.txt
recension_note: Kamalaśīla (c.740-795) is firmly anchored by his role in the bSam-yas / Council of Tibet debate (793-794). The three Bhāvanākramas are his own original compositions of the later 8th c.; the Sanskrit of the first/third largely survives (Namdol's Sarnath ed.), the second mainly in Tibetan. We date Kamalaśīla's authorial floruit.
note: Kamalaśīla c.740-795; cur_med 766 within range

title: Madhyamakāvatāra
consensus_date: late 6th–mid 7th c. CE (Candrakīrti's floruit; autograph)
basis: MacDonald, In Clear Words: Prasannapadā ch.1 (2015), dating Candrakīrti
source_file: cleaned_In Clear Words. Prasannapada Ch. One. Vol. I (Candrakirti)_Anne MacDonald, 2015.txt
recension_note: Candrakīrti's own verse treatise (with his auto-commentary). La Vallée Poussin's 1907–12 Bibliotheca Buddhica edition is based largely on the Tibetan with Sanskrit fragments; the work itself is Candrakīrti's autograph dated to his floruit, MacDonald c. 570–640 (synchronism with Dharmapāla). No earlier recension layer.
note: Candrakīrti floruit per MacDonald 570–640; cur_med 610 within range.

title: Bodhicaryāvatārapañjikā
consensus_date: late 10th-early 11th c. CE
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980): Prajñākaramati 10th c.
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Authored commentary (pañjikā) of Prajñākaramati on Śāntideva's Bodhicaryāvatāra. Date = the commentator's floruit (late 10th-early 11th c., Vikramaśīla), NOT the date of the root Bodhicaryāvatāra (Śāntideva, 8th c.). The de La Vallée Poussin edition is Prajñākaramati's own text.
note: cur_med 988 correct for the commentator.

title: Bhavasaṅkrāntiṭīkā
consensus_date: 5th-6th c. CE (commentary, post-Yogācāra)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism. A Survey (1980) on the Bhavasaṅkrānti corpus; N. Aiyaswami Sastri ed. (1938)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Our text is the ṭīkā (commentary) ascribed to Maitreyanātha on Nāgārjuna's root Bhavasaṅkrānti, NOT the root verses themselves. As a commentary it necessarily postdates the Madhyamaka root text; its ālaya-vijñāna-aware doctrinal horizon places the commentary in the Yogācāra-influenced 5th-6th c. Distinguish root (2nd-3rd c. Nāgārjuna tradition) from this later commentary, which is what we date.
note: Attribution uncertain; commentary later than root. cur_med 452 within plausible window.

title: Karmavibhaṅgopadeśa
consensus_date: 5th-7th c. CE (commentary; Nepalese-transmitted Sanskrit text)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980) on Lévi's edition; Kudo's Nepalese-MS transliterations (cited in Anālayo, Dawn of Abhidharma)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: This is the UPADEŚA (commentary) on the root Mahākarmavibhaṅga, not the root text; it cites and quotes numerous other works and so postdates them. Edited by Sylvain Lévi (1932) from Nepalese Sanskrit MSS (cf. Kudo's transliterations). The root Mahākarmavibhaṅga is earlier (Chinese versions exist), but our text is the later commentary. No recension-specific date in the library; range is an estimate.
note: Commentary is later than the root, but library gives no firm recension date; keep low confidence, no forced >75y flag.

title: Prasannapadā [Madhyamakaśāstravṛtti]
consensus_date: early-mid 7th c. CE (authored text, Vaidya ed.)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Warrick, Wise One in the Prasannapadā (1983)
source_file: cleaned_Wise One in the Prasannapada_M Warrick - 1983_OCR.txt
recension_note: Single-author commentary of Candrakīrti on Nāgārjuna's 2nd-c. Mūlamadhyamakakārikā; though it embeds the much older root verses, the Prasannapadā itself is a 7th-c. composition. Vaidya 1960 reproduces La Vallée Poussin's edition of the Sanskrit text (Nepalese MS transmission); no significantly later recensional layer.
note: Candrakīrti c. 600-650; cur_med 610 within range

title: Madhyamakāloka
consensus_date: later 8th c. CE (c. 740-795)
basis: Krasser (1991); Nakamura (1980, Kamalaśīla 8th c.)
source_file: cleaned_Dharmottaras kurze Untersuchung der Gültigkeit einer Erkenntnis Laghupramanyapariksa_ HELMUT KRASSER, 1991.txt
recension_note: The Madhyamakāloka is Kamalaśīla's own original Yogācāra-Madhyamaka (Svātantrika) treatise of the later 8th c. (c.740-795). It survives chiefly in Tibetan; the DSBC/GRETIL Sanskrit is a partial/reconstructed e-text. We date Kamalaśīla's authorial floruit, anchored by the 793-94 Council of Tibet.
note: Kamalaśīla c.740-795; cur_med 767 within range

title: Hastavālaprakaraṇavṛtti
consensus_date: late 5th-6th c. CE (Dignāga-era śāstra; Sanskrit not extant, Tucci reconstruction)
basis: Nakamura 1980 (lists it among Dignāga's works; rejects the Āryadeva attribution); Tucci 1956 (reconstruction from Tibetan/Chinese)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: No Sanskrit original survives; our 'edition' is Tucci's Sanskrit reconstruction from the Tibetan and the two Chinese versions (Paramārtha, mid-6th c.; Yijing/I-tsing). Authorship disputed between Āryadeva and Dignāga, but its Vijñaptimātra doctrine places it in the Dignāga horizon (c. 480-540). The Paramārtha translation gives a 6th-c. terminus ante quem for the underlying work.
note: Dignaga-era Yogacara verse-treatise; current bounds 470-540 consistent. Sanskrit is a reconstruction, not an extant recension.

title: Madhyamakahṛdaya
consensus_date: mid-6th c. CE (composition by Bhāviveka)
basis: Eckel, Jñānagarbha on the Two Truths (1987/92), 'the sixth-century philosopher Bhāvaviveka'; Nakamura, Indian Buddhism; Lindtner ed. 2001 (recovered Sanskrit verses)
source_file: cleaned_Jnanagarbha on the Two Truths. An Eight Century Handbook of Madhyamaka Philososphy (Svatantrika)_Malcolm David Eckel, 1987, 1992.txt
recension_note: The Madhyamakahṛdaya is Bhāviveka's own verse work (his auto-commentary is the Tarkajvālā). Lindtner's 2001 edition prints the Sanskrit kārikā text recovered from a manuscript found in Tibet; this transmits Bhāviveka's authorial composition, so dating the author (c. 550 CE) dates our recension. No earlier layer applies. Current median 537 is correct.
note: Bhāviveka's own verses, c. 550 CE; median accurate

title: Svabhāvavāda (collection of svabhāvavāda verses)
consensus_date: None
basis: R. Bhattacharya, 'Verses Relating to Svabhāvavāda: A Collection' (modern anthology); doctrine traced to pre-Buddhist Materialists (Kalupahana, Wayman)
source_file: cleaned_Causality.The Central Philosophy of Buddhism_David_J. Kalupahana 1986.txt
recension_note: This is NOT a single Buddhist work with one recension. It is a modern scholarly collection (Bhattacharya) of svabhāvavāda verses gathered from heterogeneous Sanskrit/Prakrit sources spanning centuries, the doctrine itself being pre-Buddhist Lokāyata/Materialist. There is no unitary recension to date; no recension-specific date can be assigned.
note: Modern anthology of scattered verses; no single recension date. Assign none rather than invent.

title: Madhyamakāvatārakārikā
consensus_date: early-mid 7th c. CE (authored text)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Single-author root verses (kārikā) of Candrakīrti, 7th c. La Vallée Poussin's 1907-12 Bibliotheca Buddhica edition reconstructs the Sanskrit kārikās (the autocommentary survives mainly in Tibetan); this is Candrakīrti's own composition, not a layered sūtra. Same author/date as the Prasannapadā.
note: Candrakīrti c.600-650; cur_med 611 within range

title: Madhyamakaśāstrastuti
consensus_date: late 6th–mid 7th c. CE (Candrakīrti's floruit; attributed stotra)
basis: MacDonald, In Clear Words: Prasannapadā ch.1 (2015), dating Candrakīrti; de Jong 1962 ed.
source_file: cleaned_In Clear Words. Prasannapada Ch. One. Vol. I (Candrakirti)_Anne MacDonald, 2015.txt
recension_note: A short eulogy of Nāgārjuna attributed to Candrakīrti and transmitted between the end of MMK ch.27 and the colophon; edited by de Jong 1962 as 'La Madhyamakaśāstrastuti de Candrakīrti'. Pandey's 1994 Bauddhastotrasaṃgraha reprints the stotra. If genuinely by Candrakīrti it dates to his floruit (MacDonald: 570–640); no earlier layer involved.
note: Candrakīrti floruit per MacDonald 570–640; cur_med 609 within range.

title: Niraupamyastava
basis: Lindtner, Nagarjuniana (1982, the cited edition; via de Jong, Brief History 1987)
source_file: cleaned_Brief History of Buddhist Studies in Europe and America_J. M. de Jong, 1987.txt
recension_note: The Niraupamyastava is one of the four hymns (Catuḥstava) attributed to Nāgārjuna and accepted as genuine by Lindtner (whose 1982 Nagarjuniana, building on Tucci's 1932 ed., is the cited edition). Authenticity has been debated (e.g. Mitrikeski 2010), but the consensus dating follows the historical Nāgārjuna (ca. 150–250 CE). It is a short authored stotra with extant Sanskrit; no earlier/later recension layering applies.
note: Catuḥstava hymn attributed to Nāgārjuna; 2nd c. CE. Some authenticity doubt, hence medium.

title: Mūlamadhyamakakārikā
basis: Ruegg (via de Jong, Brief History 1987); Gethin, Foundations (1998); Kapstein, Buddhism between Tibet and China (2009)
source_file: cleaned_Foundations of Buddhism_Rupert Gethin, 1998.txt
recension_note: The MMK is the unitary authored root-text (kārikās) of Nāgārjuna (fl. ca. 150–250 CE), universally accepted as his most important and certainly genuine work. The Vaidya edition presents the verses as embedded in Candrakīrti's Prasannapadā, but the kārikās themselves are the 2nd-c. authored śāstra; no later-layer/earlier-layer problem applies (the date is that of the author, not of the surviving manuscript witnesses).
note: Authored śāstra; cur_med 200 within consensus 150–250.

title: Mahāyānaviṃśikā [Mahāyānaviṃśati]
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE if by Nāgārjuna, but attribution disputed; no recension-specific date in library
basis: Attribution to Nāgārjuna recorded but flagged as disputed in the workitem; library has only bibliographic references (Jamieson 2000; Tucci 1956 ed.)
source_file: cleaned_Companion to Asian Art and Architecture_Rebecca M. Brown, Deborah S. Hutton, eds., 2011.txt
recension_note: Short Madhyamaka verse-text edited by Tucci (1956) from a Sanskrit MS; traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna (so ~2nd-3rd c.) but the ascription is widely doubted and many such 'Nāgārjuna' minor works are later. Library holds no scholarship dating the extant Sanskrit recension specifically; band kept wide (mid-2nd to mid-5th c.) around the disputed Nāgārjuna attribution.
note: Disputed Nāgārjuna attribution; no firm recension date in library; cur_med within wide band

title: Vigrahavyāvartanī
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (Nāgārjuna; authentic Madhyamaka work)
basis: Schoening, The Śālistamba-sūtra and its Indian Commentaries (1991), placing Nāgārjuna; Bhattacharya, The Dialectical Method of Nāgārjuna (1978/2002), critical ed. of the VV
source_file: cleaned_Salistamba-sutra and its Indian Commentaries_Jeffrey Davis Schoening, 1991.txt
recension_note: The Vigrahavyāvartanī (kārikās + Nāgārjuna's own vṛtti) is one of the few works of undisputed authenticity in the Nāgārjuna corpus; there is no significant later-recension problem. The sole Sanskrit witness (Bhattacharya's MS R, copied by a Tibetan ~12th c.) is a late, corrupt copy, but it transmits the original 2nd-3rd c. text, not an expanded recension. Date is of the composition, not the medieval MS. The Yonezawa transliteration edits this same tradition.
note: Standard Nāgārjuna date (c. 150-250 CE); current median fine.

title: Vigrahavyāvartanīkārikā
basis: Ruegg & Lindtner (via de Jong, Brief History 1987); Westerhoff, The Dispeller of Disputes (2010)
source_file: cleaned_Brief History of Buddhist Studies in Europe and America_J. M. de Jong, 1987.txt
recension_note: The Vigrahavyāvartanī (kārikās + autocommentary) is among the small set of works whose authenticity by the Nāgārjuna of the MMK is accepted by both Ruegg and Lindtner (whose 1982 Nagarjuniana is the cited edition). It is a unitary 2nd-c. authored text; the date is the author's (ca. 150–250 CE), with no earlier/later layer issue.
note: Genuine Nāgārjuna work; cur_med 201 within 150–250.

title: Yuktiṣaṣṭikākārikā
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (Nāgārjuna; one of the authentic core works)
basis: Lindtner, Nagarjuniana (1982), which authenticates 13 works incl. Yuktiṣaṣṭikā; reported in de Jong (1987); Nāgārjuna ca. 150-250 CE (standard)
source_file: cleaned_Brief History of Buddhist Studies in Europe and America_J. M. de Jong, 1987.txt
recension_note: The Yuktiṣaṣṭikākārikā is one of Nāgārjuna's authentic works (Lindtner's canon), composed ca. 2nd-3rd c. CE. The complete Sanskrit kārikā-text is largely lost (surviving via Tibetan and Chinese and quotations); the GRETIL text is a modern Sanskrit reconstruction (B. Kumar 1993). We date the composition of the work itself, which the modern reconstruction represents; there is no later Sanskrit recension to distinguish. Current median 219 is appropriate.
note: Authentic Nāgārjuna kārikā; Sanskrit is a modern reconstruction of a 2nd-3rd c. work; median accurate.

title: Śikṣāsamuccaya
consensus_date: first half / mid 8th c. CE (Śāntideva fl. 685-763)
basis: B. R. Clayton, Ethics in the Śikṣāsamuccaya (2001), reporting de Jong
source_file: cleaned_Ethics in the Siksasamuccaya. A Study in Mahayana Morality_Barbra R. Clayton, 2001.txt
recension_note: Śāntideva's compendium of Mahāyāna sūtra citations; Bendall's Bibliotheca Buddhica edition is from Nepalese MSS but represents the author's 8th-c. composition. The embedded sūtra quotations are older, but the compilation (our text) is Śāntideva's own, fl. 685-763.
note: Śāntideva fl. 685-763; cur_med 747 within range

title: Bodhicaryāvatāra [Bodhisattvacaryāvatāra]
consensus_date: late 7th-mid 8th c. CE (longer canonical/Nepalese recension)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: A Survey (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Vaidya's edition (with Prajñākaramati's Pañjikā) is the longer canonical/Nepalese vulgate recension, not the shorter Dūn-huang/early-Tibetan recension. We date the vulgate text as fixed by Śāntideva's floruit (c. 650-750) and stabilised by Prajñākaramati's c.1000 commentary; the recension itself belongs to the early-mid 8th c.
note: Śāntideva c.650-750; cur_med 750 within range

title: Tattvasaṃgraha
consensus_date: mid-to-late 8th c. CE
basis: J. Blumenthal, 'Śāntarakṣita', Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy
source_file: cleaned_Blumenthal_Śāntarakṣita_SEP.txt
recension_note: Original composition of Śāntarakṣita (725-788); Krishnamacharya's edition prints it with Kamalaśīla's Pañjikā. No earlier layer at issue — author-dated work, placed in the latter part of his life, mid-late 8th c.
note: Author 725-788; cur_med 744 within range

title: Tattvasaṃgrahapañcikā [Tattvasaṃgrahapañjikā]
consensus_date: later 8th c. CE (Kamalaśīla's autograph commentary)
basis: Kajiyama, Antarvyāptisamarthana of Ratnākaraśānti (1999); cf. Nakamura 1980, van Schaik 2004
source_file: cleaned_Antarvyaptisamarthana of Ratnakarasanti_Yuichi KAJIYAMA, 1999.txt
recension_note: This is Kamalaśīla's own pañjikā on Śāntarakṣita's Tattvasaṃgraha (Śāntarakṣita 725-788). Both author and text are firmly 8th c.; the Krishnamacharya GOS edition (1926) reproduces this single-author work with no earlier layer to disentangle. Dated to the commentator's floruit.
note: cur_med 780 within range; author Kamalaśīla c.740-795.

title: Bodhicittotpādasūtraśāstra
consensus_date: before c.410 CE (composition); extant 'Sanskrit' is a 1949 retranslation from Chinese
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980): Sanskrit lost, translated into Chinese by Kumārajīva (d. c.413)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: CRITICAL: the Sanskrit original is LOST. Our 'edition' (Shantibhiksu Shastri, Visva-Bharati Annals 1949) is a modern Sanskrit RECONSTRUCTION made by back-translating the Chinese (T.1659, by Kumārajīva, c.402-413). So no ancient Sanskrit recension exists; the date band is for the original composition, whose terminus ante quem is Kumārajīva's translation (~410). Attribution to Vasubandhu is doubtful ('ascribed'); Nakamura notes its terminology is 'rather Hīnayānist'. Confidence medium because the text we read is a 20th-c. retranslation, not a transmitted Sanskrit witness.
note: Lost Skt original; extant text 1949 retranslation from Kumārajīva's Chinese. TAQ ~410.

title: Śālistambakakārikā [Āryaśālistambakakārikā]
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (attributed to Nāgārjuna; text survives only in Tibetan, our Sanskrit is a modern reconstruction)
basis: Schoening, The Śālistamba-sūtra and its Indian Commentaries, 1991 (diss.)
source_file: cleaned_Salistamba-sutra and its Indian Commentaries_Jeffrey Davis Schoening, 1991.txt
recension_note: The Śālistambakakārikā (ST-K) is traditionally ascribed to Nāgārjuna (2nd-3rd c. CE) but the attribution is disputed and Schoening notes the kārikā carries Yogācāra terminology ('grasped'/'grasper', trisvabhāva), implying it may post-date the 4th-c. Yogācāra school. Crucially it is NOT extant in Sanskrit at all: it survives in Tibetan (translated c. early 9th c.) and Mongolian. Our DSBC 'Sanskrit' edition (Sonam Rabten, CIHTS 2004) is a modern back-translation from Tibetan, so it is a 20th-c. reconstruction of a verse text whose Indian original is 2nd-5th c. CE. Date given for the work, not the reconstruction; confidence low.
note: No surviving Sanskrit; Tibetan-only kārikā, modern reconstruction; ascribed Nāgārjuna 2nd-3rd c.; cur_med 141 within range.

title: Pratītyasamutpādādivibhaṅganirdeśasūtra
consensus_date: before c. 350-430 CE (Sarvāstivāda canonical sūtra, terminus from Vasubandhu's commentary); extant Sanskrit recension undated in library
basis: Mejor, Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośa and the Commentaries Preserved in the Tanjur (1991): Vasubandhu authored the Pratītyasamutpādavyākhyā on this sūtra
source_file: cleaned_Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakosa and the Commentaries Preserved in the Tanjur_Marek Mejor, 1991_OCR.txt
recension_note: This is the root sūtra (analysis of the 12-fold pratītyasamutpāda), distinct from Nāgārjuna's Pratītyasamutpādahṛdaya kārikā. Vasubandhu (fl. ~350-430 CE) wrote a vyākhyā on it, so the sūtra layer predates him (Sarvāstivāda canonical milieu, likely 2nd-4th c.). The extant Sanskrit text Vaidya prints comes from the Nepalese Mahāyānasūtrasaṃgraha transmission; no scholarship in the library dates this specific Sanskrit recension, so date is bracketed by the pre-Vasubandhu terminus.
note: cur_nb/cur_na empty (no current median basis); root sūtra pre-Vasubandhu; recension undated in library

title: Bodhyāpattideśanāvṛtti
consensus_date: early text of the early-Madhyamaka 'circle' (c. 3rd-5th c. CE); Nāgārjuna attribution doubtful
basis: Lindtner, Nagarjuniana (1987)
source_file: cleaned_Nagarjuniana. Studies in the Writings and Philosophy of Nagarjuna_Chr Lindtner, 1987.txt
recension_note: Lindtner rejects firm Nāgārjuna authorship but places the work in the early-Madhyamaka 'circle', linked to Mātṛceṭa's hymns and early bodhisattva-confession (Triskandhaka) ritual texts — i.e. an early composition, far older than the cur_med of 748. Caveat: Lindtner knows it chiefly through the Tibetan Tanjur (TP no. 5506); our DSBC Sanskrit is a (re)constituted/transmitted Sanskrit text whose own redaction date is not established in the library, so confidence is low and bounds are broad.
note: cur_med 748 too late; early-Madhyamaka ritual text per Lindtner, but Sanskrit-recension date unverified so only low confidence

title: Śālistambakamahāyānasūtraṭīkā [Āryaśālistambakamahāyānasūtraṭīkā]
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE (Yogācāra-influenced commentary; pseudo-Nāgārjuna)
basis: Schoening, The Śālistamba-sūtra and its Indian Commentaries (1991), pp. (intro, ch. 3) on authorship and earliest attestation
source_file: cleaned_Salistamba-sutra and its Indian Commentaries_Jeffrey Davis Schoening, 1991.txt
recension_note: NOT the 2nd-3rd c. Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna: the ṭīkā (T) teaches ālayavijñāna and the three-natures, i.e. Yogācāra doctrine, and the attribution to Nāgārjuna is a 'late Indian tradition' co-optation. The earliest reference (Bhāviveka's Madhyamaka-ratnapradīpa, which cites Dharmakīrti and Candrakīrti, both early 7th c.) cannot predate the 7th c. and may be later. The work survives only in Tibetan/Mongolian translation (never translated into Chinese); the GRETIL/Sonam Rabten (2004) 'Sanskrit' is a modern reconstruction. Date is of the original composition (7th-8th c.), set conservatively before the ~9th c. Tibetan translation.
note: Mis-dated as Nāgārjuna 2nd-3rd c.; actually a 7th-8th c. Yogācāra commentary, off by >400y.

title: Yogācārabhūmi, Kleśa section
consensus_date: 4th c. CE (Yogācārabhūmi as compiled/redacted)
basis: U. T. Kragh (ed.), The Foundation for Yoga Practitioners: The Yogācārabhūmi..., 2013; cf. Deleanu 2006
source_file: cleaned_Foundation for Yoga Practitioners. Yogacarabhumi and Its Adaptation in India, East Asia, and Tibet_Ulrich Timme Kragh, ed., 2013.txt
recension_note: The Kleśa material belongs to the maulī-bhūmi core of the Yogācārabhūmi. The YBh is a layered compilation; recent consensus (Kragh ed. 2013; Deleanu 2006) places its redaction in the 4th c. CE (associated with Asaṅga's milieu). The Sanskrit base is Bhattacharya's 1957 edition. Date is for the redacted YBh, not any single earlier stratum.
note: YBh redacted 4th c. CE (Kragh, Deleanu); cur_med 543 ~190y too late.

title: Abhidharmasamuccayabhāṣya
consensus_date: 6th-7th c. CE (Jinaputra)
basis: library attribution to Jinaputra (post-Asaṅga/Sthiramati commentator); cf. Tatia ed. 1976
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Bhāṣya on Asaṅga's Abhidharmasamuccaya. The library consistently attributes the bhāṣya to Jinaputra (not Sthiramati), a later Yogācāra commentator working after the mid-6th-c. masters; commentary is distinct from Asaṅga's earlier root text. Dated c. 6th-7th c. The work-item's tentative Sthiramati attribution (510-570) is less well supported.
note: Jinaputra commentary; ~6th-7th c.; cur_med 521 within 75y of corrected range midpoint.

title: Abhidharmasamuccaya
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Asaṅga's floruit; extant Sanskrit recension)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); cf. Frauwallner, Philosophie des Buddhismus (1956)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: A genuine work of Asaṅga, composed in his lifetime (c. 310-390 CE; Frauwallner places his birth early 4th c.). Pradhan's edition reconstructs/edits a recovered Sanskrit MS of the abhidharma compendium; no earlier non-Sanskrit layer is at issue here since this is an authored śāstra, so the date is Asaṅga's own period.
note: cur_med 368 sits squarely within Asaṅga's 4th-c. floruit; no change.

title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṅkāra
consensus_date: 4th c. CE (Yogācāra śāstra, Asaṅga/Vasubandhu generation)
basis: H. Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980), on Maitreya-Asaṅga-Vasubandhu authorship and Vasubandhu's date (c. 400)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Yogācāra śāstra: kārikās ascribed to Maitreya(nātha)/transmitted via Asaṅga with Vasubandhu's bhāṣya. Single-author composition (no long pre-history), placed in the Asaṅga-Vasubandhu floruit, 4th c. CE. Lévi's 1907 edition rests on a Nepalese manuscript of this same recension. Distinct from Aśvaghoṣa's homonymous Sūtrālaṅkāra.
note: 4th-c. Yogacara sastra; existing nb270/na380 fine; cur_med 327 within range.

title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Maitreya/Asaṅga kārikās + Vasubandhu bhāṣya)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Frauwallner, Philosophie des Buddhismus (1956)
source_file: cleaned_Philosophie des Buddhismus_Erich Frauwallner, 1956.txt
recension_note: Kārikās ascribed to Maitreya(nātha)/Asaṅga (early-mid 4th c.); the prose bhāṣya is attributed to Vasubandhu (mid 4th-early 5th c.). Bagchi's edition gives the verses-with-bhāṣya Sanskrit text. The whole compound work belongs to the single Yogācāra generation of Asaṅga-Vasubandhu, so no significantly earlier layer applies.
note: Within Asaṅga-Vasubandhu period; no change.

title: Śarīrārthagāthā
consensus_date: 4th c. CE (Yogācārabhūmi redaction; verses older)
basis: Hsu-feng Lee, A Study of the Śarīrārthagāthā in the Yogācārabhūmi (2017), citing Schmithausen (2007)
source_file: cleaned_Study Of The Sarirarthagatha In The Yogacarabhumi_Hsu-feng Lee, 2017.txt
recension_note: A collection of canonical (Āgama) verses with embedded commentary, located in the Cintāmayībhūmi of the Yogācārabhūmi; Enomoto's edition. The individual verses derive from older Āgama/Mūlasarvāstivāda sources, but the collection-cum-commentary is part of the YBh Basic Section, redacted ~4th c. CE (Xuanzang's 7th-c. Chinese = terminus ante quem). Date refers to the YBh redaction, not the older verse sources.
note: cur_med 350 matches the 4th-c. YBh redaction; no change.

title: Madhyāntavibhāgakārikā
consensus_date: c. 350 CE (4th c. CE; Maitreya/Asaṅga period)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); cf. standard dating of Asaṅga (c. 310-390) to whom the Maitreya works are transmitted
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Kārikā text attributed to Maitreya(nātha), transmitted via Asaṅga (c. 310-390 CE), thus c. 4th c. CE. The Pandeya edition prints the kārikās with Vasubandhu's bhāṣya and Sthiramati's ṭīkā; the root kārikā layer (our text) is the earliest stratum, 4th c. The commentaries are later (Vasubandhu mid-4th c.; Sthiramati 6th c.) but are not the dated object here.
note: 4th c. Maitreya/Asaṅga; current median 350 correct.

title: Tattvaratnāvaloka [Tattvaratnāloka]
consensus_date: c. 1000 CE (Vāgīśvarakīrti, late 10th-early 11th c.)
basis: Gray, The Visualization of the Secret; Campbell, Vajra Hermeneutics
source_file: cleaned_Gray_The Visualization of the Secret_Atiśa’s Contribution to the Internalization of Tantric Sexual Practices.txt
recension_note: Original verse treatise by Vāgīśvarakīrti, the southern gatekeeper-paṇḍita of Vikramaśīla and a teacher associated with Nāropa/Atiśa, datable to the late 10th-early 11th c. (Campbell: 'early eleventh century'). Janardan Pandey's 1997 edition reproduces this single-author work; no earlier layer.
note: Vikramaśīla gatekeeper, contemporary of Nāropa; c. 1000.

title: Triṃśikāvijñaptikārikā [Triṃśikākārikā]
consensus_date: late 4th-early 5th c. CE (Vasubandhu)
basis: Gethin (1998); Brunnhölzl, Luminous Heart (2009), names Triṃśikā a landmark of Vasubandhu's Yogācāra writing
source_file: cleaned_Luminous Heart. Third Karmapa on Consciousness, Wisdom, and Buddha Nature_Karl Brunnholzl, 2009.txt
recension_note: The 30 root verses (kārikā) by Vasubandhu, his last Yogācāra work. Lévi's Sanskrit (1925) edits the verses from the Nepalese MS he discovered alongside the Triṃśikābhāṣya of Sthiramati; the verses are Vasubandhu's genuine Sanskrit. Date is the composition (c.350-430); not to be confused with Sthiramati's later 6th-c. commentary.
note: Vasubandhu root verses; median 391 within range.

title: Trisvabhāvanirdeśa
consensus_date: late 4th-early 5th c. CE (attrib. Vasubandhu)
basis: Gethin (1998) for Vasubandhu's floruit; Brunnhölzl, Luminous Heart (2009) treats it as Vasubandhu's
source_file: cleaned_Luminous Heart. Third Karmapa on Consciousness, Wisdom, and Buddha Nature_Karl Brunnholzl, 2009.txt
recension_note: Short verse treatise traditionally and predominantly ascribed to Vasubandhu (so dated to his floruit c.350-430); some scholars question the attribution, hence medium confidence. Anacker's Sanskrit text (1986) rests on the Nepalese MS tradition / Tibetan; no Chinese translation survives, so the extant Sanskrit is the only recension and there is no earlier non-Sanskrit layer to distinguish.
note: Attributed Vasubandhu verse work; median 391 within range.

title: Viṃśatikā vijñaptimātratāsiddhi [Viṃśikāvṛtti] kārikā
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Yogācāra Vasubandhu)
basis: Mejor, Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośa and the Commentaries Preserved in the Tanjur (1991); Frauwallner
source_file: cleaned_Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakosa and the Commentaries Preserved in the Tanjur_Marek Mejor, 1991_OCR.txt
recension_note: The Viṃśatikā is by the Yogācāra Vasubandhu (same author as the Abhidharmakośa and Triṃśikā). Scholarship debates between a 4th-c. and a 5th-c. Vasubandhu (Frauwallner's two-Vasubandhu hypothesis; Mejor cites 400-480 for the Kośa/Vijñaptimātratā author); range 350-450 covers the spread. Lévi's 1925 edition is from Nepalese MSS of this short kārikā+vṛtti text; no earlier-layer problem since it is a self-contained authored śāstra.
note: Authored śāstra; Vasubandhu 4th-5th c. Median 360 within range.

title: Vijñaptimātratāsiddhi (prakaraṇadvayam: Viṃśatikā + Triṃśikā)
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Vasubandhu)
basis: Lévi (Deux traités de Vasubandhu); Hirakawa 1973; standard Yogācāra chronology
source_file: cleaned_Abhidharmasamuccaya by Asanga_Walpola Rahula_Sara Boin-Webb, English, 2001.txt
recension_note: The Tripathi 1992 edition is Vasubandhu's two short vijñaptimātratā treatises (Viṃśatikā and Triṃśikā), the same texts edited by S. Lévi. Vasubandhu (brother of Asaṅga) is dated 4th-5th c. CE. The Sanskrit is single-author and well established; dated to Vasubandhu.
note: cur_med 803 is grossly wrong: this is Vasubandhu's Viṃśatikā/Triṃśikā, 4th-5th c. CE, off by ~400y.

title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra (kārikā only)
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Maitreya/Asaṅga kārikās)
basis: Frauwallner (1956); Nakamura (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Philosophie des Buddhismus_Erich Frauwallner, 1956.txt
recension_note: The kārikā-only redaction of the MSA, attributed to Maitreya(nātha)/Asaṅga; the accompanying bhāṣya is Vasubandhu's. Same Yogācāra generation as the full text (item asmahsuu); Bagchi 1970 prints the Sanskrit kārikās. No earlier layer beyond the verses themselves.
note: Same as asmahsuu; no change.

title: Bodhisattvabhūmi
consensus_date: 4th c. CE (Asaṅga, c. 310-390)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Wallis 2002 (Asaṅga ca. 290-360)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Bodhisattva section of Asaṅga's Yogācārabhūmi; a single-author śāstra. Extant Sanskrit (Dutt 1966, after Wogihara) is the original Sanskrit composition, not a translation, so the composition date = Asaṅga's floruit, c. 310-390 (4th c. CE). No earlier-layer issue.
note: cur_med 267 within ~75y of mid-4th-c. Asaṅga; the cur range start (230) is too early but median is acceptable.

title: Pañcaskandhaprakaraṇa
consensus_date: late 4th-early 5th c. CE (Vasubandhu)
basis: Gethin (1998) for Vasubandhu's floruit; Mejor (1991) treats Pañcaskandhaprakaraṇa as Vasubandhu's
source_file: cleaned_Foundations of Buddhism_Rupert Gethin, 1998.txt
recension_note: Vasubandhu's prose abhidharma manual on the five skandhas. Shastri's Sanskrit edition (1955) is a reconstruction aided by Tibetan/Chinese in places, but the work is securely Vasubandhu's composition; date refers to that 4th-5th c. composition. No earlier non-Sanskrit layer; root attribution stable.
note: Vasubandhu abhidharma prakaraṇa; median 391 within range.

title: Ratnagotravibhāga [Mahāyānottaratantraśāstra / Uttaratantra]
consensus_date: early-mid 5th c. CE (extant Sanskrit recension, Johnston ed.)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism. A Survey (1980); E. H. Johnston ed. (1950)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Johnston's edition is the single extant Sanskrit recension (verses + prose commentary). Attribution split between Maitreya/Asaṅga (Tibetan) and Sāramati (Chinese), dated c. 350-450; the Chinese translation by Ratnamati (511 CE) is a firm terminus ante quem. No materially earlier/later Sanskrit recension is involved, so root-text date and our-edition date coincide.
note: cur_med 429 squarely within consensus 5th-c. window.

title: Bāhyārthasiddhikārikā
consensus_date: mid-late 8th c. CE (Śubhagupta, teacher of Dharmottara)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); library notices placing Śubhagupta as teacher of Dharmottara
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Śubhagupta post-dates Dharmakīrti and is named as a teacher of Dharmottara (fl. c. 740-800), so a mid-to-late-8th-c. floruit (c. 720-780) is preferred over Nakamura's looser 650-750. The Bāhyārthasiddhikārikā survives mainly in Tibetan, with only Sanskrit fragments/reconstruction (Sastri/Hattori); the date is for Śubhagupta's authorial composition, not a later Sanskrit recension. cur_med 750 is accurate.
note: cur_med 750 fits Śubhagupta (teacher of Dharmottara); survives chiefly in Tibetan

title: Cittatattvasaṅgraha
consensus_date: None
recension_note: DSBC lists the Cittatattvasaṅgraha in its Yogācāra section with no editor or printed-edition metadata and no named author. No recension-specific (or even text-specific) scholarship for this title could be located in the library; the only 'cittatattva' hits are unrelated compounds. Per the no-invention rule, left undated.
note: No recension-specific scholarship found; cannot confirm or correct cur_med.

title: Yogācārabhūmi
consensus_date: ca. 3rd-4th c. CE (layered compilation, completed 4th c. CE)
basis: Schmithausen (2007) and Deleanu (2006), reported in Lee, Study of the Śarīrārthagāthā in the Yogācārabhūmi (2017)
source_file: cleaned_Study Of The Sarirarthagatha In The Yogacarabhumi_Hsu-feng Lee, 2017.txt
recension_note: The Yogācārabhūmi is a stratified compilation (Schmithausen, Deleanu, Aramaki): early bhūmis (incl. Śrāvakabhūmi) go back to the 3rd c., with the whole assembled in several steps through the 4th c. CE; traditional ascription to Asaṅga/Maitreyanātha (c. 270-350). Bhattacharya's 1957 edition prints the surviving Sanskrit of the early bhūmi sections, i.e. the core compilation, not a later redaction. Date set to the compilation horizon.
note: 4th-c. compilation horizon; median 365 within range.

title: Mahāyānasaṃgraha
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Asaṅga; no extant Skt original)
basis: Lamotte, La Somme du Grand Véhicule (1938-39, the reference edition); Nakamura (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: An authored Yogācāra śāstra by Asaṅga (c. 310-390). NO Sanskrit original survives; the work is preserved in Tibetan and in Chinese (Paramārtha and Xuanzang versions) and reconstructed by Lamotte. The DSBC 'Sanskrit' text is a modern back-translation/restoration, not a transmitted recension; the historical date remains Asaṅga's own composition period in the 4th-early 5th c.
note: Asaṅga; restored Skt only. cur_med 369 fine; no change.

title: Pañcaskandhakavibhāṣā
consensus_date: c. 510-570 CE (Sthiramati)
basis: Frauwallner's dating of Sthiramati 510-570 CE, as reported across the library; ed. Kramer 2013 (Sanskrit MS from TAR)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Sthiramati's commentary on Vasubandhu's Pañcaskandhaka. Kramer's 2013 critical edition rests on a recovered Sanskrit manuscript; the text is Sthiramati's own work, dated to his floruit c. 510-570. No earlier layer; commentary distinct from Vasubandhu's root Pañcaskandhaka.
note: Sthiramati 510-570; cur_med 511 correct.

title: Yogācārabhūmi: Samāhitā Bhūmi
consensus_date: 4th c. CE (Yogācārabhūmi Basic Section)
basis: Hsu-feng Lee (2017) citing Schmithausen on the YBh; Schmithausen, Ālayavijñāna (1987) treats the Samāhitā Bhūmi as part of the Basic Section
source_file: cleaned_Study Of The Sarirarthagatha In The Yogacarabhumi_Hsu-feng Lee, 2017.txt
recension_note: Samāhitā Bhūmi is the 6th bhūmi of the Maulī bhūmi (Basic Section) of the Yogācārabhūmi; Delhey's 2009 critical edition uses the Sanskrit MS plus Tibetan/Chinese. The Basic Section is dated ~4th c. CE (Schmithausen), with Xuanzang's first-half-7th-c. Chinese as terminus ante quem. Date is the YBh Basic-Section Sanskrit recension.
note: cur_med 350 matches the 4th-c. YBh Basic Section; no change.

title: Yogācārabhūmi: Śrāvakabhūmi
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE text-formation (one of the earliest Yogācārabhūmi layers; final YBh redaction mid-late 4th c.)
basis: Deleanu, Chapter on the Mundane Path (Laukikamārga) in the Śrāvakabhūmi, vol. 1 (2006)
source_file: cleaned_Chapter on the Mundane Path (Laukikamarga) in the Sravakabhumi.Vol.1_Florin Deleanu, 2006.txt
recension_note: Deleanu treats the Śrāvakabhūmi as among the EARLIEST textual units of the Yogācārabhūmi (proto-Yogācāra phase, traceable to Sarvāstivādin yogācāras of the 1st-2nd c.), with the whole YBh reaching its final form mid-late 4th c. and continuing to receive redactional additions/cross-references thereafter. Our edition (Śrāvakabhūmi Study Group, Tokyo 1998/2007) is based essentially on the Gilgit Sanskrit MS — the extant recension of the ŚrBh text, formed c. 2nd-3rd c. but embedded in the larger 4th-c. YBh redaction. Date here = formation of the ŚrBh text itself.
note: Tokyo Study Group ed. of Gilgit-based ŚrBh; Deleanu dates ŚrBh among earliest YBh layers (2nd-3rd c.); current median 236 consistent.

title: Yogācārabhūmi: Śrāvakabhūmi
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE text-formation (one of the earliest Yogācārabhūmi layers; final YBh redaction mid-late 4th c.)
basis: Deleanu, Chapter on the Mundane Path (Laukikamārga) in the Śrāvakabhūmi, vol. 1 (2006)
source_file: cleaned_Chapter on the Mundane Path (Laukikamarga) in the Sravakabhumi.Vol.1_Florin Deleanu, 2006.txt
recension_note: Same Śrāvakabhūmi text as srabhu_u, here via Karunesha Shukla's 1973 edition (TSWS 14), also based on the Gilgit Sanskrit MS. Deleanu places the ŚrBh among the earliest layers of the Yogācārabhūmi (proto-Yogācāra, ~2nd-3rd c.), embedded in a YBh whose final redaction is mid-late 4th c. Date refers to the formation of the ŚrBh text/recension, not the much later Gilgit physical MS (~5th-7th c.).
note: Shukla 1973 ed. of same Gilgit-based ŚrBh; Deleanu dating; current median 236 consistent.

title: Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā
consensus_date: early-mid 6th c. CE (Sthiramati, author of the extant Sanskrit ṭīkā)
basis: Hirakawa, Index to the Abhidharmakośabhāṣya (1973); Makransky 1997 (ca. 510-70 C.E.)
source_file: cleaned_ABHIDHARMAKOSABHASYA, INDEX TO THE..  _skt-tib-chi_AKlRA HIRAKAWA,1973.txt
recension_note: Same work as the Yamaguchi-edited ṭīkā (here also cf. Pandeya 1971). Sthiramati's own commentary, c. 510-570 CE; not the Maitreya/Asaṅga kārikā or Vasubandhu's bhāṣya layer.
note: Sthiramati 510-570; cur_med 541 correct.

title: Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā, Chapter 1
consensus_date: first half 6th c. CE (composition by Sthiramati)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980): Sthiramati c. 470-550; 'three natures' study (Sthiramati in the 6th c. CE); Yamaguchi 1934 ed. of the Sanskrit
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Sthiramati's sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on the Madhyāntavibhāga(-bhāṣya). Yamaguchi's 1934 edition prints the Sanskrit recovered from a Nepalese manuscript; it transmits Sthiramati's authorial work, so the author's floruit (c. 510-540 CE) dates our recension. Distinct from the root Madhyāntavibhāga kārikās (Maitreya/Asaṅga) and Vasubandhu's bhāṣya, both earlier. Current median 540 is correct.
note: Sthiramati c. 470-550; ṭīkā composed first half 6th c.; median accurate

title: Madhyāntavibhāgaṭīkā (Yamaguchi)
consensus_date: early-mid 6th c. CE (Sthiramati, author of the extant Sanskrit ṭīkā)
basis: Hirakawa, Index to the Abhidharmakośabhāṣya (1973); corroborated by Makransky 1997, Ruegg 2010
source_file: cleaned_ABHIDHARMAKOSABHASYA, INDEX TO THE..  _skt-tib-chi_AKlRA HIRAKAWA,1973.txt
recension_note: Sthiramati's ṭīkā on the Madhyāntavibhāga(-bhāṣya), edited by Yamaguchi (1934) from Nepalese MSS. This is the commentary by Sthiramati himself (c. 510-570), not the root kārikā (attributed to Maitreya/Asaṅga, 4th c.) nor Vasubandhu's bhāṣya. The date is that of the author's composition; the surviving Sanskrit is essentially his text.
note: Sthiramati 510-570; cur_med 541 correct.

title: Triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣya [Triṃśikābhāṣya]
consensus_date: late 5th-mid 6th c. CE (Sthiramati, fl. 470-550)
basis: Nakamura 1980 and Ganguly (both give Sthiramati 470-550); Buescher 2007 critical ed. of the extant Sanskrit
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Authored work, not a layered sūtra. Our edition is Buescher's 2007 critical edition of the actually-extant Sanskrit (Nepalese MS tradition). Date is Sthiramati's lifetime (470-550 CE); root verses are Vasubandhu's, but this is the bhāṣya, which is Sthiramati's own composition.
note: Sthiramati 470-550; current bounds 470-550 exactly right.

title: Triṃśikāṭīkā
consensus_date: c. 690-750 CE (late 7th-8th c.)
basis: Vose, Birth of Prāsaṅgika, 2005 (Vinītadeva 630-700); standard floruit late 7th-early 8th c.
source_file: cleaned_Birth of Prasangika. A Buddhist movement in India and Tibet_Kevin Alan Vose, 2005.txt
recension_note: Vinītadeva is a successor of Dharmakīrti (d. c. 660) and a contemporary/near-successor of Dharmottara, dated by scholarship to the late 7th-8th c. (Vose '630-700'; elsewhere 'eighth century' / 'um 700'). His Triṃśikāṭīkā (sub-commentary on Vasubandhu's Triṃśikā) survives complete only in Tibetan, with Sanskrit only in fragments; the date is the author's composition c. 700 CE, not the much earlier root Triṃśikā of Vasubandhu (4th-5th c.).
note: Vinītadeva c. 700; prior median 625 ~95y too early

title: Viṃśatikā vijñaptimātratāsiddhi [Viṃśikāvṛtti]
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Vasubandhu)
basis: Multiple (Brunnhölzl 2009; Apple); Lévi 1925 edition of the Sanskrit
source_file: cleaned_Luminous Heart. Third Karmapa on Consciousness, Wisdom, and Buddha Nature_Karl Brunnholzl, 2009.txt
recension_note: Authored work of Vasubandhu, dated 4th c. (some 4th-5th c.). Lévi's edition (1925) prints the Sanskrit Viṃśikā (kārikās + vṛtti) recovered from Nepalese MSS; this is the original-authored śāstra, not a later reworking. cur_med 409 (range 350-430) is correct.
note: Vasubandhu 4th-5th c.; authored śāstra; cur_med 409 correct

title: Madhyāntavibhāgabhāṣya [Madhyāntavibhāgakārikābhāṣya]
consensus_date: late 4th-early 5th c. CE (Vasubandhu's bhāṣya)
basis: Gethin, Foundations of Buddhism (1998); Brunnhölzl, Luminous Heart (2009, lists it among Vasubandhu's main Yogācāra works)
source_file: cleaned_Luminous Heart. Third Karmapa on Consciousness, Wisdom, and Buddha Nature_Karl Brunnholzl, 2009.txt
recension_note: This is the BHĀṢYA (Vasubandhu's commentary), distinct from the root Madhyāntavibhāga-kārikā ascribed to Maitreya(nātha)/Asaṅga, which is earlier (Asaṅga, early/mid 4th c.). Our edition (Nagao 1964) is the editio princeps from a single Nepalese Sanskrit MS, so it transmits Vasubandhu's genuine Sanskrit. Date applies to Vasubandhu's bhāṣya layer, not the root verses.
note: Vasubandhu bhāṣya on Maitreya/Asaṅga kārikās; median 391 within range.

title: Yogācārabhūmi: Laukikamārga
consensus_date: 3rd–4th c. CE (Śrāvakabhūmi core ca. 200–270; final redaction within Yogācārabhūmi by ca. 380)
basis: Florin Deleanu, The Chapter on the Mundane Path (Laukikamārga) in the Śrāvakabhūmi, vol. 1, 2006
source_file: cleaned_Chapter on the Mundane Path (Laukikamarga) in the Sravakabhumi.Vol.1_Florin Deleanu, 2006.txt
recension_note: The Laukikamārga is part of the Śrāvakabhūmi, which Deleanu identifies as the oldest layer of the Yogācārabhūmi (compiled ca. 200–270, before the rest of the Maulyo bhūmayaḥ at ca. 270–340). Shukla's edition gives the extant Sanskrit Śrāvakabhūmi as fixed within the Yogācārabhūmi whose final redaction Deleanu dates to ca. 350–380 (Asaṅga's milieu). So this is a śāstra root layer, not a later commentary; the extant Sanskrit text reflects the 3rd-c. core stabilised by the late-4th-c. redaction.
note: cur_med 279 matches Deleanu's 3rd–4th-c. Śrāvakabhūmi dating.

title: Dharmaskandha
consensus_date: 2nd-1st c. BCE (composition: one of the two earliest Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma texts); extant Sanskrit = Gilgit fragments (c. 6th c. CE)
basis: Potter (ed.), Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies: Abhidharma; Frauwallner (1956); Dietz, Fragmente des Dharmaskandha (1984)
source_file: cleaned_Potter_Encylopedia of Indian Philosophies_Abhidharma.txt
recension_note: As an early-canonical Abhidharma śāstra, the standard scholarly date is its composition among the earliest Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma (c. 2nd c. BCE; Frauwallner notes it is quoted at the start by the Sarvāstivāda Vinaya council account and the Mahāprajñāpāramitopadeśa). Our EDITION, however, is Dietz's transcription of the GILGIT Sanskrit fragments (palaeographically c. 6th c. CE), so the physical witness is far later than the composition; the date given is for the composed text the Gilgit fragments transmit.
note: Earliest Sarvastivada Abhidharma; cur_med within early-Abhidharma range. Extant witness Gilgit 6th c.

title: Satyasiddhiśāstra [Tattvasiddhiśāstra] of Harivarman
consensus_date: 3rd-4th c. CE (Harivarman; the Sanskrit edition is a modern reconstruction)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980): 'Harivarman about 250 wrote his Satyasiddhi'; also 'cir. fourth century A.D.'
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Harivarman's Satyasiddhi is variously placed c. 250 to c. 4th c. CE. The Sanskrit ORIGINAL IS LOST; Aiyaswami Sastri's 1975 'Sanskrit text' is a modern back-translation/reconstruction from Kumārajīva's Chinese (T1646, 411-412 CE). The dated object here is the composition by Harivarman, not a surviving Sanskrit recension.
note: cur_med 303 within Harivarman's 3rd-4th-c. window; not flagged. Editorial caveat: extant 'Sanskrit' is a reconstruction.

title: Abhidharmāmṛta
consensus_date: 2nd c. CE (original composition; extant only in Chinese, Sanskrit is a modern reconstruction)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980); Cox, Disputed Dharmas (1995); Dessein, Saṃyuktābhidharmahṛdaya II (1999)
source_file: cleaned_Disputed Dharmas. Early Buddhist Theories on Existence…Sanghabhadra's Nyayanusara_Collett Cox, 1995.txt
recension_note: The *Abhidharmāmṛta(rasa)śāstra of the Sarvāstivāda master Ghoṣaka, who is cited as an authority in the Mahāvibhāṣā (~2nd c. CE), survives as an integral text only in the Chinese translation T1553 (trans. 5th c.). The GRETIL edition is Shanti Bhikshu Sastri's 1953 SANSKRIT RECONSTRUCTION made backward from the Chinese; no ancient Sanskrit recension is extant, so the date given is that of the original composition (~2nd c. CE), not of any Sanskrit witness.
note: cur_med 190 matches ~2nd c. CE composition; Sanskrit text is a modern retroversion, not an ancient recension.

title: Subodhālaṅkāra
consensus_date: 12th c. CE
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes, 1980
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Single-author Pali/Sanskritized poetics manual by the Sinhalese thera Saṅgharakkhita, 12th c. CE. No earlier layer; the PTS (Jaini) edition / DSBC e-text transmit this 12th-c. text. (A Pali alaṅkāra-śāstra modelled on Daṇḍin's Kāvyādarśa, hence catalogued among Buddhist works.)
note: cur_med 1204 within ~25y of 12th-c. close; consistent with Nakamura's '12th century'.

title: Dharmadhātugarbhavivaraṇa
consensus_date: 8th-10th c. CE (pseudonymous 'Deutero-Nāgārjuna' work)
basis: D. Seyfort Ruegg, in Études tibétaines dédiées à la mémoire de Marcelle Lalou (on the works attributed to Nāgārjuna / Deutéro-Nāgārjuna)
source_file: cleaned_Études tibétaines dédiées à la mémoire de Marcelle Lalou.txt
recension_note: The attribution to the 2nd-c. Madhyamaka Nāgārjuna is spurious. Ruegg groups this short commentary on the ye dharmā formula (invoking Mañjuśrī Kumārabhūta, abhidharma/prajñā genre) among the devotional-tantric works of a later 'Deutéro-Nāgārjuna' influenced by developed Mahāyāna and Vajrayāna guru-devotion. The Chinese rendering (Taishō 1675) is late (10th c.), reinforcing a late composition. Namdol's CIHTS (1997) edition presents this pseudonymous later work, not a Nāgārjuna-era text. Range reflects the later-1st-millennium pseudo-Nāgārjuna horizon; library gives no firm Sanskrit composition date.
note: Pseudo-Nāgārjuna ('Deutero-Nāgārjuna') devotional commentary, late Chinese tr.; later than cur_med 583, but low confidence (no precise date).

title: Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti
consensus_date: mid-5th to mid-6th c. CE (extant Sanskrit Abhidharmadīpa+Vṛtti)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980), summarizing Jaini's ed.; Cox, Disputed Dharmas (1995); Mejor, Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośa (1991)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: The Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti is the auto-commentary on the Abhidharmadīpa kārikās (Jaini's single recension from one Tibetan-region MS). It is a post-Vasubandhu Vaibhāṣika polemic that reproduces and rebuts the Abhidharmakośabhāṣya, so it must postdate Vasubandhu (4th-5th c.); there is no earlier layer — the work is an original composition by the anonymous 'Dīpakāra' (a self-styled follower of Saṅghabhadra), placed c. 450-550 CE.
note: cur_med 707 too late; reacts to Vasubandhu, c. 450-550 CE per Jaini/Nakamura.

title: Abhidharmadīpakārikā
consensus_date: c. 450-550 CE (Sarvāstivāda śāstra by the Dīpakāra)
basis: Nakamura 1980 (composition 450-550 A.D.); Jaini 1959 critical ed. of the Sanskrit
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Authored śāstra by the anonymous Dīpakāra, a follower of Saṅghabhadra and critic of Vasubandhu's Kośa. Our edition is Jaini's 1959 critical edition of the (incomplete) extant Sanskrit found in Tibet. Post-dates Vasubandhu and Saṅghabhadra; placed 450-550 CE. The 'Vimalamitra' attribution in the project hint is unverified and rejected by the scholarship, which uses 'Dīpakāra'.
note: Post-Kosa Sarvastivada polemic, 450-550 CE; current bounds 450-600 / median 509 consistent.

title: Abhidharmadīpa(kārikā) with Vibhāṣāprabhāvṛtti
consensus_date: 6th c. CE
basis: Williams, Tribe & Wynne, Buddhist Thought (2000); cf. Jaini ed. 1959 (Dīpakāra writes against Vasubandhu's Kośa)
source_file: cleaned_Buddhist Thought. A Complete Introduction to the Indian Tradition_Paul Williams, Anthony J. Tribe, Alexander Wynne, 2000.txt
recension_note: Vaibhāṣika polemic by the anonymous 'Dīpakāra' responding to Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośabhāṣya; therefore post-Kośa, placed possibly in the 6th c. Jaini's 1959 edition is the single recension (no earlier layer); date is the date of composition.
note: Post-Vasubandhu Vaibhāṣika reply; 6th c.; cur_med 510 near lower edge but within 75y.

title: Abhidharmakośabhāṣya Chapter 9
consensus_date: late 4th-early 5th c. CE (Vasubandhu the Kośakāra)
basis: Gethin, Foundations of Buddhism (1998); Mejor, Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośa and the Commentaries (1991)
source_file: cleaned_Foundations of Buddhism_Rupert Gethin, 1998.txt
recension_note: Chapter 9 (Pudgalaviniścaya / refutation of the self) is the appended ninth section of Vasubandhu's own auto-commentary (bhāṣya) on his Abhidharmakośa-kārikā. Our Sanskrit is the Pradhan critical edition (1975) rebuilt on the Rāhula Sāṅkṛtyāyana Gilgit/Tibet MS rediscovered at Ngor; this is the genuine 4th-5th c. Sanskrit, not a retranslation. Frauwallner's older 'two-Vasubandhu' split assigned the Kośakāra to c.400-480 (so Mejor's conventional 400-480); the now-prevailing single-Vasubandhu view places him c.350-430. Date is the composition, of which the Sanskrit MS tradition is a faithful witness.
note: Vasubandhu, end 4th/early 5th c.; current median 385 within range.

title: Abhidharmakośabhāṣya
consensus_date: late 4th-early 5th c. CE (Vasubandhu the Kośakāra)
basis: Gethin, Foundations of Buddhism (1998); Mejor (1991)
source_file: cleaned_Foundations of Buddhism_Rupert Gethin, 1998.txt
recension_note: Vasubandhu's own prose bhāṣya on his Abhidharmakośa-kārikā. Pradhan ed. (1975) is built on the Gilgit-region Sanskrit MS recovered by Sāṅkṛtyāyana; it is the original Sanskrit work, not reconstructed from Chinese/Tibetan. Older Frauwallner two-Vasubandhu hypothesis gave the Kośakāra c.400-480 (Mejor's working date); modern single-Vasubandhu consensus = c.350-430. Root kārikās and bhāṣya are by the same author and contemporaneous.
note: Same work, full text; median 387 within range.

title: Abhidharmakośakārikā
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Vasubandhu)
basis: Mejor, Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakośa and the Commentaries Preserved in the Tanjur (1991); Frauwallner (1951)
source_file: cleaned_Vasubandhu's Abhidharmakosa and the Commentaries Preserved in the Tanjur_Marek Mejor, 1991_OCR.txt
recension_note: Self-contained authored verse-text (kārikā) by Vasubandhu; the Pradhan/Gokhale Sanskrit (recovered 1930s-40s from the Bhāṣya MS) reflects the original kārikā, not a later expansion. Date follows Vasubandhu: Mejor cites the 'generally accepted' 400-480 CE, while the rival 4th-c. dating (single Vasubandhu, brother of Asaṅga) pulls earlier; range 350-450 spans the debate.
note: Vasubandhu 4th-5th c.; median 365 at lower edge but within 75y.

title: Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā
consensus_date: 6th c. CE (Jayarakṣita's ṭīkā; the commentary, not the root manual)
basis: Derrett & Jayarakṣita (1983), as reported by Tuladhar-Douglas; cf. Williams (Critical Concepts vol. 7)
source_file: cleaned_Tuladhar-Douglas_Remaking Buddhism in Medieval Nepal.txt
recension_note: Sanghasena Singh's TSWS 24 edition is Jayarakṣita's commentary (Sphuṭārthā ṭīkā), a Mahāsāṃghika(-Lokottaravāda) hybrid-Sanskrit gloss for novices. The ROOT text, the Śrīghanācāra(saṃgraha), is a much earlier Mahāsāṃghika novice manual 'of perhaps the 3rd century, now lost' (Tuladhar-Douglas) — do NOT date our text to that root layer. The extant commentary that our edition prints is dated by Derrett (1983) to the 6th c.; Williams loosely calls it a '5th century' source. Hence 6th c. CE for the ṭīkā, far earlier than cur_med 749.
note: cur_med 749 off by ~200y; our text is the 6th-c. ṭīkā (Derrett), not an 8th-c. work and not the 3rd-c. root manual

title: Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE (Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda ācāra; reconstructed from Jayarakṣita's 5th-c. commentary)
basis: Williams (ed.), Buddhism: Critical Concepts, vol.7 — vinaya-legalism study dating Jayarakṣita's hybrid-Sanskrit Mahāsāṃghika commentary to the 5th c. CE
source_file: cleaned_Buddhism. Critical Concepts in Religious - Volume 7_Paul Williams.txt
recension_note: Our 'text' is Sanghasena Singh's reconstruction of the Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha root verses extracted from Jayarakṣita's Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā. Jayarakṣita's Mahāsāṃghika(-Lokottaravāda) commentary is dated to the 5th c. CE (alongside Buddhaghosa's Samantapāsādikā); the root ācāra verses it comments on are no later than that and likely somewhat earlier (4th-5th c.). The reconstructed Sanskrit recension thus belongs to the 4th-5th c., NOT the 9th c.
note: cur_med 878 ~400y too late; Jayarakṣita and the root ācāra text are 5th c. or earlier.

title: Suvarṇavarṇāvadāna
consensus_date: 4th-5th c. CE
basis: Tatelman, Translation and Study of the Pūrṇāvadāna (1988)
source_file: cleaned_Translation and Study of the Purnavadana_Joel Howard Tatelman, 1988.txt
recension_note: An avadāna 'dedicated by a Mahāyāna bhikṣu' but free of distinctively Mahāyānist doctrine (Tatelman), dated 4th-5th c. CE. Roy's edition (1971) rests on Nepalese MSS but the text is treated as a single composition without a markedly later layer; cur_med 409 matches.
note: Tatelman 4th-5th c.; cur_med 409 correct

title: Jātakamālā
consensus_date: 4th c. CE
basis: Nariman, Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism (1919); confirmed by Buswell, Encyclopedia of Buddhism (Āryaśūra early 5th c.)
source_file: cleaned_Literary History of Sanskrit Buddhism_Nariman G K_1919.txt
recension_note: Single-author kāvya by Āryaśūra; no multi-recension problem. Hanisch's edition is the standard critical text of the 34-story Jātakamālā. Terminus ante quem: a work of Āryaśūra was translated into Chinese in 434 CE and the Ajanta inscriptions of his verses are 6th-c. palaeographically, placing the poet in the 4th c. CE.
note: Āryaśūra 4th c. (Chinese trans. 434); median 353 correct.

title: Jātakamālā
consensus_date: 4th c. CE (single-author Sanskrit kāvya; Vaidya ed. = standard Nepalese recension)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: A Survey with Bibliographical Notes (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Āryaśūra's Jātakamālā is a single-author kāvya, so the recension date = composition date (4th c. CE). The Vaidya 1959 edition (BST 21) prints the standard Sanskrit text transmitted in Nepalese MSS; this is the same work, not a later expansion. The Chinese P'u-sa-pen-sheng-man purporting to translate it is in fact a pseudo-translation/anthology, not a witness to an earlier layer.
note: 4th c. CE consensus; median 354 fits.

title: Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā
consensus_date: completed 1052 CE; final avadāna added c. 1066 (single-author Kashmiri composition)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: A Survey with Bibliographical Notes (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Not a layered sūtra: this is an original kāvya-avadāna anthology composed by the Kashmiri poet Kṣemendra, completed 1052 CE; the 108th (final) avadāna was supplied by his son Somendra c. 1066. The Vaidya edition (Mithila 1959) reproduces this 11th-c. authored text — there is no earlier 'layer' to date; though it retells older avadāna material, the work itself is mid-11th-c.
note: Author's completion date 1052; well anchored.

title: Jātakamālā [Ajātaśatrvavadāna]
consensus_date: 7th-8th c. CE
basis: Buswell, Encyclopedia of Buddhism; Khoroche (2006); Holt, Buddha in the Crown
source_file: cleaned_Buswell_Encyclopedia of Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Gopadatta's jātakamālā/avadāna collection (incl. the Ajātaśatrvāvadāna) is markedly later than Āryaśūra's: he follows the ornate prosimetric style of Kumāralāta and is named after Āryaśūra and Haribhaṭṭa as a successor in the genre. Buswell, Khoroche and Holt place him in the 7th-8th c. CE. Hahn's edition reflects this late-1st-millennium author; no earlier layer applies.
note: Gopadatta 7th-8th c.; current median 353 wrong by ~350y.

title: Jātakamālā (Haribhaṭṭa)
consensus_date: early 5th c. CE (Hahn/Wille ed. of Haribhaṭṭa's text)
basis: Khoroche, Once a Peacock, Once an Actress (Univ. Chicago Press, 2017), drawing on Hahn (1981, 2002)
source_file: cleaned_Khoroche_Once a peacock, once an actress twenty-four lives of the Bodhisattva from Haribhaṭṭa's Jātakamālā.txt
recension_note: This is a single-author kāvya jātaka collection by the named poet Haribhaṭṭa, written shortly after Āryaśūra's Jātakamālā (early 4th c.). No earlier layer issue: the work is the composition of one author in the early 5th c.; the Hahn/Wille edition reconstructs that text from later Nepalese MSS and Tibetan, but the composition date is the early Gupta period.
note: cur_med 453 sits right in Khoroche's early-5th-c. window; correct.

title: Jātakastava
consensus_date: 6th c. CE (extant Central-Asian Sanskrit recension; uncertain)
basis: Ohnuma, Head, Eyes, Flesh and Blood (2007); ed. Shackleton Bailey 1954 (Central Asian/Khotan MS)
source_file: cleaned_Head, Eyes, Flesh and Blood. Giving Away the Body in Indian Buddhist Literature_Reiko Ohnuma,2007.txt
recension_note: Our edition (Shackleton Bailey 1954) rests on a Sanskrit stotra MS recovered from Central Asia (Khotan region). The library offers no recension-specific date for Jñānayaśas's Jātakastava; it is cited only as a source for bodily-gift jātaka lists. Treated as a ca. 5th-6th c. kāvya stotra surviving in a Central Asian Sanskrit copy; no earlier-layer problem since it is an original composition, not a sūtra grown in strata.
note: No recension date in library; current 500-699 band plausible, kept low confidence.

title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā]
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (Nāgārjuna)
basis: Schoening (1991) for Nāgārjuna's dates; Vaidya ed. (1961) reprinting Tucci's Sanskrit
source_file: cleaned_Salistamba-sutra and its Indian Commentaries_Jeffrey Davis Schoening, 1991.txt
recension_note: Vaidya's 1961 edition reprints essentially Tucci's Sanskrit recension of the Ratnāvalī. Authentic Nāgārjuna work; the only Sanskrit witness is a partial Nepalese MS, but composition is 2nd-3rd c. No later-recension shift.
note: Vaidya reprint of Tucci recension; Nāgārjuna 2nd-3rd c.

title: Bhavasaṃkrāntiparikathā
consensus_date: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (verses attributed to Nāgārjuna); Sanskrit is a modern reconstruction from Chinese/Tibetan
basis: Aiyaswami Sastri, Bhavasaṅkrānti Sūtra and Nāgārjuna's Bhavasaṅkrānti Śāstra (1938); Potter, Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies Vol. 8 (1999); Ramanan, Nāgārjuna's Philosophy (1966)
source_file: cleaned_Nagarjuna's Philosophy. As Presented in the Maha-Prajnaparamita-Sastra_K. Venkata Ramanan, 1966, 1987.txt
recension_note: CRITICAL: the Sanskrit original is LOST. Aiyaswami Sastri's edition (our text) is a 20th-c. retranslation INTO Sanskrit from the Chinese and Tibetan. Traditionally the verses are ascribed to Nāgārjuna (c. 150-250 CE; Potter places him in the 100-350 CE volume) while the prose commentary is held to be later (Chi-tsang). We date the Nāgārjuna verse-text layer, but note the extant 'Sanskrit' is a reconstruction, not a transmitted recension.
note: cur_med 555 off by ~350y; verse text is Nāgārjuna-era (2nd-3rd c.), though our Sanskrit is reconstructed.

title: Śiṣyalekha (alternate)
consensus_date: 5th c. CE (Candragomin's composition)
basis: Buswell & Lopez, Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism (2014), s.v. Candragomin
source_file: cleaned_Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism_Buswell & Lopez 2014_.txt
recension_note: The Śiṣyalekha is a single-author verse epistle (lekha), not a layered sūtra, so root = recension. Standard reference scholarship (Princeton Dictionary) dates its author Candragomin to the 5th c. CE (junior contemporary of Kālidāsa), and explicitly assigns the Śiṣyalekha to him. Some older works place Candragomin as late as the 7th c., but the prevailing recent reference date is 5th c. The Minaev edition transmits this same authored poem.
note: Candragomin = 5th c.; cur_med 565 (mid-6th c.) ~115y too late.

title: Śiṣyalekha

Tatz (1978), applying I-tsing's testimony and modern method, places Candragomin in the later 7th c., superseding the older Wassilief/Kern 4th-5th c. dating; the Sanskrit kāvya epistle (Minaev ed.) is the author's own composition, so root text = author's date.

title: Daṇḍikathā

title: Vimalaratnalekha
consensus_date: c. 1040 CE (composed by Atiśa in Nepal en route to Tibet)

The Sanskrit original is LOST and survives only in Tibetan; our edition (n3009) is therefore a reconstruction/witness via the Tibetan, but the composition date is firmly mid-11th c.

title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 2

The Tucci edition (and Hahn's later edition) present the extant Sanskrit verses; this is the 2nd-c. authored text (ca. 150–250 CE). 'Part 2' is a chapter division of the single work, not a separate later recension; Ajitamitra's commentary is a separate later layer not dated here.
note: Genuine Nāgārjuna work; cur_med 202 within 150–250.

title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 3
consensus_date: 2nd-3rd c. CE (Nāgārjuna)
basis: Schoening (1991) for Nāgārjuna's dates; Tucci ed. JRAS 1934-1936 (the printed recension)

recension_note: Ratnāvalī (Ratnāvalī-rājaparikathā) is a core authentic Nāgārjuna work. Tucci's edition rests on a single incomplete Nepalese Sanskrit MS (only parts survive in Sanskrit; the rest via Tibetan/Chinese; later re-edited by Hahn). The fragmentary, medieval Sanskrit MS still transmits the 2nd-3rd c. composition, not a recast recension. This GRETIL portion is chapter 3 of the Tucci text.
note: Nāgārjuna; Tucci recension; current median correct.

title: Subhāṣitaratnakaraṇḍakathā
consensus_date: None
basis: Hahn, Die Subhāṣitaratnakaraṇḍakathā (NAWG 1983) — title characterises it as 'ein spätbuddhistischer Text'

recension_note: The traditional attribution to the early Āryaśūra (3rd-4th c.) is rejected; Hahn's edition titles it explicitly a 'late Buddhist text' (spätbuddhistischer Text) on the merit doctrine, i.e. a work of a later (probably medieval) author writing under the name Āryaśūra/Śūra. The local library has only an index mention and no sourced century, so no firm date can be assigned without inventing one. Substantively later than the classical Āryaśūra layer.
note: No recension-specific date in library; cannot confirm or correct. cur_med 470 unverifiable here.

title: Vajrasūcī
consensus_date: core perhaps 2nd c. CE (Aśvaghoṣa attribution), but extant Sanskrit text enlarged later; c. 2nd-4th c. CE
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism. A Survey (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Authorship is disputed: the Sanskrit text ascribes the Vajrasūcī to Aśvaghoṣa (early 2nd c.), the Chinese version (tr. Tianxizai, late 10th c.) to Dharmakīrti. Nakamura holds the core may be Aśvaghoṣa's but was 'enlarged gradually in later days'; its contents parallel chuan 61 of the Chinese Yogācārabhūmi. The extant Dvivedī/DSBC Sanskrit text therefore likely postdates the 2nd-c. core. Range covers core-through-enlargement.
note: cur_med ~117 matches the Aśvaghoṣa-core attribution; defensible, kept (low confidence due to enlargement/attribution dispute).

title: Apohasiddhi
consensus_date: first half of 11th c. CE (Ratnakīrti, fl. c. 1000-1050)

title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi

Frauwallner's old 'c. 950' dating is superseded; modern consensus is the first half of the 11th c. Edition = Thakur, Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī (TSWS 3, 1957/1975); this is the extant Sanskrit, the author's own work, not a layered text.
note: Author-dated; cur_med 1024 within floruit.

note: Author-dated; cur_med 1024 within floruit.

title: Avayavinirākaraṇa
consensus_date: 9th c. CE (Paṇḍita Aśoka; often placed c. 950 by later scholarship)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: A Survey with Bibliographical Notes (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Single-author Buddhist Nyāya tract (refutation of the whole/avayavin) by Paṇḍita Aśoka, edited in Six Buddhist Nyāya Tracts (Shastri 1910). No textual layering. Nakamura dates the author to the 9th c.; since Jñānaśrīmitra (active 980-1030) postdates and engages this Dignāga-school logic, a late-9th-to-10th-c. floruit is the range. Bounds kept to Nakamura's 9th c.
note: Median 998 is ~150y too late vs Nakamura's 9th-c. author; flagged.

title: Sāmānyadūṣaṇadikprasāritā
consensus_date: c. 1000 CE (late 10th-early 11th c.)

source_file: cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
recension_note: Authored work of Paṇḍita Aśoka, a Pāla-period Nyāya logician sharing apoha verses with Jñānaśrīmitra (fl. c.980-1030), hence c.1000 CE. The edited Sanskrit (Haraprasad Shastri, Six Buddhist Nyaya Tracts, 1910) IS this author's text — no earlier layer; dating = author's floruit.
note: cur_med 969 essentially correct (within 75y of c.1000).

title: Antarvyāptisamarthana
consensus_date: early-mid 11th c. CE (Ratnākaraśānti, ca. 970-1045)

note: Author floruit ca. 970-1045; median fine.

title: Nyāyabindu Prakaraṇakārikā
consensus_date: early-mid 7th c. CE (Dharmakīrti's own kārikā)

The kārikā text is the root verse-form of the Nyāyabindu and is contemporaneous with its author; no later recension issue. Our edition (Dwarikadas Shastri / Stcherbatsky BB VII) transmits the 7th-c. work.

title: Santānāntarasiddhi
consensus_date: c. 600-660 CE (Dharmakīrti)
basis: Franco (ed.), Yogic Perception, 2009 (Dharmakīrti c. 600-660); Frauwallner chronology
source_file: cleaned_Yogic Perception, Meditation and Altered States of Consciouness_Eli Franco (ed) 2009 (A).txt

The Sanskrit of the Santānāntarasiddhi is a reconstruction (Negi 1997) from Tibetan, but the date is the original composition by Dharmakīrti, not the modern reconstruction. Current bounds already match.

title: Santānāntarasiddhiṭīkā
consensus_date: early-to-mid 8th c. CE (Vinītadeva's commentary)

recension_note: This is a ṭīkā (sub-commentary) by Vinītadeva on Dharmakīrti's Santānāntarasiddhi-kārikā — date the commentary, not the root kārikā. Vinītadeva is consensually placed c. 690-750 (Brunnhölzl, Makeham) to c. 710-770 (Watson), 'eighth century' (Princeton Dictionary, Buswell-Lopez). His ṭīkā therefore belongs to the early-to-mid 8th c.
note: Vinītadeva 8th c.; cur_med 690 at early edge but within ±25 of the floruit core.

title: Sambandhaparīkṣā

The root text = author's floruit, c. 600-660 CE; no earlier sūtra layer. Distinguish the root verses (Dharmakīrti) from the later commentary. Current median 605 is slightly early but within band.

title: Vādanyāya

note: Dharmakīrti work; current 600-660 correct

title: Nyāyapraveśapravṛtti
consensus_date: 8th c. CE (Jain Haribhadra Sūri's commentary)
basis: Ganeri, Philosophy in Classical India (2001); Nakamura (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Philosophy in Classical India. The Proper Work of Reason_Jonardon Ganeri, 2001.txt
recension_note: OUR text is the pravṛtti (commentary) of the Jain Haribhadra Sūri (8th c., c.750-800), NOT Śaṅkarasvāmin's root Nyāyapraveśa(ka) (a c.6th-c. introduction to Dignāga's logic) and NOT the contemporaneous Buddhist Haribhadra (Seng-ge-bzang-po). Dhruva's GOS edition prints this Jain commentary; we date the commentary, which postdates the Dignāgan root by ~two centuries.
note: Jain Haribhadra Sūri 8th c.; cur_med 751 within range

title: Tarkaśāstra
consensus_date: pre-Dignāga (ca. 4th-5th c. CE) original; 'Sanskrit' is Tucci's 1929 reconstruction from Chinese
basis: Tucci, Pre-Diṅnāga Buddhist Texts on Logic from Chinese Sources (1929); cf. Aston (2004)
source_file: cleaned_Early Indian Logic and the Question of Greek Influence (Upiiyahrdaya)_G.V. Aston, 2004.txt
recension_note: CRITICAL: there is no transmitted Sanskrit Tarkaśāstra. The text survives only as the Chinese *Ru shi lun (如實論, T1633), translated by Paramārtha ca. 550 CE, attributed to Vasubandhu's school; it is a pre-Dignāga logic treatise. Our 'edition' is Tucci's 1929 MODERN Sanskrit back-translation (same volume as his Upāyahṛdaya reconstruction). The dated object here is the original work (pre-Dignāga, ~4th-5th c. CE, Vasubandhu's milieu), NOT a medieval Sanskrit recension. Confidence low because the Sanskrit text is a 20th-c. scholarly construct.
note: Original pre-Dignaga ~4th-5th c.; Skt is Tucci reconstruction. No cur_nb/na given.

title: Upāyahṛdaya
consensus_date: ca. 2nd–3rd c. CE original (Tucci's text is a modern Sanskrit reconstruction from the 472 CE Chinese)
basis: Y. Kajiyama in Steinkellner (ed.), Studies in the Buddhist Epistemological Tradition; on the Chinese translation date
source_file: cleaned_Steinkellner_Buddhist Epistemological Tradition.txt
recension_note: There is NO extant Sanskrit recension: Tucci's 'edition' is a modern Sanskrit retroversion of the Chinese Fāngbiàn xīnlùn (方便心論, T 1632), translated in 472 CE by Kiṃkara (an earlier translation is lost). 472 CE is therefore a firm terminus ante quem for the original. The original early logic (hetuvidyā) text is dated by Kajiyama to the time Nāgārjuna began his activities (c. 150–250 CE), after the Carakasaṃhitā and before the Nyāyasūtra. The date refers to this 2nd–3rd-c. original underlying the 472 Chinese, since the Sanskrit form we have is reconstructed, not transmitted.
note: Original is ca. 150–250 CE (median ~200), terminus ante quem 472 CE; cur_med 282 ~82y too late.

title: Hetubindu
consensus_date: 1st half 7th c. CE (Dharmakīrti, c. 600-660; latest research 'active 550-650')

source_file: cleaned_Eltschinger_Why did the Buddhists adopt Sanskrit?.txt
recension_note: The Hetubindu is an authored śāstra by Dharmakīrti; there is no earlier 'layer' to disentangle. Sanskrit text recovered/edited by Gokhale 1997 (Sanskrit lost for long, known via Tibetan/Chinese and a Sanskrit fragment); date is the author's lifetime. Traditional date c. 600-660 CE; Krasser's revised chronology (accepted by Eltschinger) pushes Dharmakīrti's activity to c. 550-650, but 600-660 remains the most widely cited bracket and contains the work's composition.
note: Authored work; cur_med 631 within Dharmakīrti's lifetime. Correct.

title: Tarkabhāṣā

note: cur_med 1179 vs scholarly c. 1100 (Kajiyama, Ganeri 'eleventh-century'); corrected to late-11th/early-12th c. Flagged: off by ~80y.

Edition = Thakur (TSWS 3, 1957/1975), the extant Sanskrit collection of the author's own works. Frauwallner's 'c. 950' superseded; cur_na 1099 is slightly too late.

No multi-layer recension issue: the text date equals the author's floruit, c. 600-660 CE (Nakamura c. 650). The Matsuda-Steinkellner folio fragment merely supplies one MS witness. consensus_date: late 6th–mid 7th c. CE (Dharmakīrti's floruit; autograph kārikā text)

title: Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti [Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti]
