recension_note: This is the Svavṛtti — Dharmakīrti's OWN prose autocommentary on chapter 1 (svārthānumāna) of the Pramāṇavārttika, edited by Gnoli (1960) from the unique Sanskrit MS. It is contemporaneous with the kārikās, i.e. a genuine 7th-c. authorial text (not a later commentator's gloss), so the date of the root text and of our edited recension coincide: ca. 600-660 CE. cur_med (650) is correct.
note: Dharmakīrti's authorial autocommentary, ca. 600-660; cur_med correct.

title: Sambandhaparīkṣā
consensus_date: late 6th–mid 7th c. CE (Dharmakīrti's floruit; autograph kārikā text)
basis: Tillemans, Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika ch.4 (2000), intro on Dharmakīrti's date
source_file: cleaned_DHARMAKIRTI'S PRAMANAVARTTIKA. Translation of the 4. chapter  (pararthanumana)_TOM J. F. TILLEMANS, 2000.txt
recension_note: Root kārikā work composed by Dharmakīrti himself; no Ur-text/recension gap. The Dvārikādāsa Śāstrī edition prints the kārikās with Prabhācandra's later commentary, but the verses are Dharmakīrti's own (c. 550–660). Tillemans corrects Frauwallner's older firm c.600–660 to a 6th–7th c. range.
note: Dharmakīrti floruit; cur_med 606 within range.

title: Vādanyāya
consensus_date: 1st half 7th c. CE (Dharmakīrti, c. 600-660; one of his later works)
basis: Eltschinger (citing Krasser 2011); Steinkellner; Much 1991 ed.; Dharmakīrti's chronology of works (Hetubindu/Vādanyāya late)
source_file: cleaned_Eltschinger_Why did the Buddhists adopt Sanskrit?.txt
recension_note: Authored śāstra by Dharmakīrti, edited from Sanskrit by Much 1991; counted among Dharmakīrti's later works (with the Hetubindu) in the relative chronology (Steinkellner_Buddhist Epistemological Tradition). No earlier textual layer; date = author's lifetime, 1st half 7th c. CE (Krasser revision: c. 550-650).
note: Authored; cur_med 632 within Dharmakīrti's lifetime. Correct.

title: Nyāyabinduprakaraṇa
consensus_date: late 6th–mid 7th c. CE (Dharmakīrti's floruit; autograph treatise)
basis: Tillemans, Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika ch.4 (2000), intro on Dharmakīrti's date
source_file: cleaned_DHARMAKIRTI'S PRAMANAVARTTIKA. Translation of the 4. chapter  (pararthanumana)_TOM J. F. TILLEMANS, 2000.txt
recension_note: Same work as the Nyāyabindu; the Dvārikādāsa Śāstrī (1994) edition prints it with Vinītadeva's and Dharmottara's later commentaries, but the root prakaraṇa is Dharmakīrti's autograph, dated to his floruit c. 550–660.
note: Dharmakīrti floruit; cur_med 608 within range.

title: Hetutattvopadeśa
consensus_date: 10th-early 11th c. CE
basis: Brunnhölzl, Luminous Heart (2009); Tucci, Minor Buddhist Texts I (1956)
source_file: cleaned_Luminous Heart. Third Karmapa on Consciousness, Wisdom, and Buddha Nature_Karl Brunnholzl, 2009.txt
recension_note: Same Jitāri Hetutattvopadeśa, here in Tucci's edition (Minor Buddhist Texts I, 1956). Authored c.940-1010 by Jitāri, teacher of Atiśa; the edited Sanskrit is the author's text, no distinct earlier layer.
note: Duplicate of jithetuu (different edition); same author-date.

title: Hetutattvopadeśa
consensus_date: 10th-early 11th c. CE
basis: Brunnhölzl, Luminous Heart (2009); Frauwallner/Steinkellner reporting Jitāri as teacher of Atiśa
source_file: cleaned_Luminous Heart. Third Karmapa on Consciousness, Wisdom, and Buddha Nature_Karl Brunnholzl, 2009.txt
recension_note: Authored pramāṇa primer by Jitāri (Jetāri), a Pāla logician and teacher of Atiśa (982-1054), so fl. c.940-1010. This is the Chattopadhyaya 1939 edition (Sanskrit partly reconstructed from the Tibetan); the Sanskrit recension reflects Jitāri's own composition — dating = author's floruit, no earlier layer.
note: cur_med 970 correct; reconstructed Skt but same author-date.

title: Nibandhāvalī
consensus_date: late 10th-early 11th c. CE (Jñānaśrīmitra, active 980-1030)
basis: Seton, Defining Wisdom: Ratnākaraśānti's Sāratamā (2015), citing Kajiyama
source_file: cleaned_Defining Wisdom. Ratnakarasanti’s Saratama_Gregory Max Seton, 2015.txt
recension_note: The Nibandhāvalī is the collection of Jñānaśrīmitra's own philosophical works (ed. Thakur 1959). It is an authored corpus, not a layered scripture; date = the author's documented activity 980-1030 CE at Vikramaśīla.
note: Author active 980-1030; median fine.

title: Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti Commentary [Pramāṇavārttikavṛttiṭīkā]
consensus_date: c. 800 CE (Karṇakagomin, 9th c.)
basis: Vose, Birth of Prāsaṅgika (2005); Ishida 2011 (9th c.)
source_file: cleaned_Birth of Prasangika. A Buddhist movement in India and Tibet_Kevin Alan Vose, 2005.txt
recension_note: Karṇakagomin's ṭīkā glosses Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti (svārthānumāna chapter), itself partly copying Śākyabuddhi. The ṭīkā is a single-author work of c. 800 CE (Sāṅkṛtyāyana's 1943 edition from the same MS). Dated to Karṇakagomin's floruit, distinct from the much earlier root text (Dharmakīrti, c. 600-660).
note: cur_med 800 = Karṇakagomin fl. 800; exact.

title: Vādanyāyaṭīkā
consensus_date: latter half 8th c. CE
basis: Joshi, Studies in the Buddhistic Culture of India; Nakamura (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Studies in the Buddhistic Culture of India_Lal Mani Josh.txt
recension_note: Vinītadeva's ṭīkā is a sub-commentary on Dharmakīrti's Vādanyāya; the ṭīkā postdates Dharmakīrti (7th c.) and belongs to Vinītadeva's own floruit in the latter half of the 8th c. We date the commentary layer, not Dharmakīrti's root prakaraṇa.
note: Vinītadeva late 8th c.; cur_med 757 within range

title: Jātinirākṛti
consensus_date: 9th-10th c. CE
basis: Warder, Indian Buddhism (2000); Nakamura on Dharmakīrti-school pramāṇa tracts
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism_Anthony Kennedy Warder, 2000 (1970).txt
recension_note: Short anonymous (later attributed to Dharmakīrti or his school) pramāṇa tract refuting the universal (jāti), of the same late post-Dharmakīrti Nyāya-tract genre as Aśoka's and Jñānaśrīmitra's apoha works; library has no recension-specific study, so dated by genre to the 9th-10th c. Pāla logicians. The Dharmakīrti attribution is spurious; the surviving Sanskrit (DSBC e-text) is a late tract, not a 7th-c. work.
note: cur_med 969 within genre window; low confidence, attribution uncertain.

title: Nyāyabinduṭīkāṭippaṇī
consensus_date: 9th-11th c. CE
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980) on Dharmottara c.730-800
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Anonymous ṭippaṇī (sub-gloss) on Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā. As a gloss it necessarily postdates Dharmottara (c.730-800), so 9th c. at the earliest; such pramāṇa sub-commentaries circulated through the Pāla period (to c.11th c.). This is a derivative annotation, not the root Nyāyabindu (Dharmakīrti, 7th c.) nor Dharmottara's ṭīkā itself.
note: cur_med 886 fits a post-Dharmottara gloss; bounds widened to 11th c.

title: Bauddha manīṣā - Bauddheṣu Pramāṇamīmāṃsā
consensus_date: None
basis: no library reference; DSBC homepage URL only, no author, no printed edition
recension_note: Not a classical Sanskrit Buddhist work but a modern secondary/expository compilation on Buddhist pramāṇa-mīmāṃsā (no named author, edition field null, only the DSBC homepage as URL). No recension to date; left null per the no-invention rule.
note: Modern compilation, not a datable classical recension; no date assigned

title: Viśālāmalavatī Pramāṇasamuccayaṭīkā
consensus_date: first half of the 8th c. CE
basis: O. H. Pind, Dignāga's Philosophy of Language (2009)
source_file: cleaned_Dignaga’s Philosophy of Language. Dignaga on anyapoha. Pramanasamuccaya V_Ole Holten Pind, 2009.txt
recension_note: Jinendrabuddhi's commentary on Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya; the Steinkellner/Krasser/Lasic critical edition is based on the recovered Sanskrit MS from the Tibetan Autonomous Region (STTAR 1, 2005). Post-Dharmakīrti author, securely 8th c.; no earlier layer at issue (this is the original composition, not a translation-derived recension).
note: Author-dated 8th c.; cur_med 735 correct

title: Nyāyapraveśakasūtra
consensus_date: mid-6th c. CE (Śaṅkarasvāmin, pupil of Dignāga)
basis: Conference Proceedings (Śaṅkarasvāmin, Dignāga's disciple, ca. 6th c.); Dignāga c. 480-540 attested across library
source_file: cleaned_BUDDHISM INTO THE YEAR 2000. International Conference Proceedings.txt
recension_note: Brief introduction to Dignāga's logic, traditionally by Śaṅkarasvāmin (pupil of Dignāga, c. 480-540), occasionally ascribed to Dignāga himself; either way mid-6th c. CE. Dhruva's 1930 edition gives the Sanskrit original; this is the original composition, no earlier layer.
note: Śaṅkarasvāmin mid-6th c.; cur_med 550 correct.

title: Pramāṇavārttika
consensus_date: late 6th-mid 7th c. CE (authored text)
basis: Tillemans, Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika ch.4 (2000); Nakamura (1980)
source_file: cleaned_DHARMAKIRTI'S PRAMANAVARTTIKA. Translation of the 4. chapter  (pararthanumana)_TOM J. F. TILLEMANS, 2000.txt
recension_note: Single-author verse treatise by Dharmakīrti. Sāṅkṛtyāyana's 1938-40 ed. is based on the Sanskrit MS recovered in Tibet, transmitted with Manorathanandin's vṛtti; this is the authored text, not a layered scripture. Traditional date c.600-660 (Xuanzang argument); Frauwallner/Tillemans allow latter half of 6th c.
note: c.600-660 trad., poss. late 6th c.; cur_med 610 fine

title: Pramāṇavārttikālaṃkāra
consensus_date: c. 800 CE (around the turn of the 8th-9th c.)
basis: Seton, Defining Wisdom: Ratnākaraśānti's Sāratamā, 2015 (dating Prajñākaragupta ca. 800)
source_file: cleaned_Defining Wisdom. Ratnakarasanti’s Saratama_Gregory Max Seton, 2015.txt
recension_note: Authored work of Prajñākaragupta (ca. 800 CE), the great commentary on Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika. Our edition (Sāṅkṛtyāyana, TSWS 1, 1953) transmits this c.-800 śāstra; no earlier-layer issue.
note: cur_med 824 (nb750/na899) matches ca. 800 consensus.

title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi Vyatirekātmikā
consensus_date: first half 11th c. CE
basis: Kajiyama, Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy (Tarkabhāṣā), 1966
source_file: cleaned_Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy. An Annotated Translation of the Tarkabhasa of Moksakaragupta_Kajiyama, Yuichi, 1966.txt
recension_note: Single-author śāstra by Ratnakīrti, direct pupil of Jñānaśrīmitra (elder contemporary of Atīśa) and refuted by Udayana; the work is fixed to c. 1000-1050 CE, with no earlier layer involved. Shāstrī's 1910 Bibliotheca Indica edition reproduces this 11th-c. text.
note: cur_med 1048 within bounds; high confidence Ratnakīrti dating.

title: Vādarahasya [Udayananirākaraṇa]
consensus_date: first half 11th c. CE
basis: Kajiyama, Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy (Tarkabhāṣā), 1966 (Ratnakīrti dating)
source_file: cleaned_Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy. An Annotated Translation of the Tarkabhasa of Moksakaragupta_Kajiyama, Yuichi, 1966.txt
recension_note: Attributed to Ratnakīrti (attribution uncertain); the title Udayananirākaraṇa (refutation of Udayana) fits Ratnakīrti's milieu of dispute with the Naiyāyika Udayana, so a c. 1000-1050 CE date is reasonable. Pandey's 1984 edition prints this 11th-c. text. If the attribution is wrong it could be slightly later, hence medium confidence.
note: cur_med 1089 lies in cur range 1000-1200; my tighter Ratnakīrti window is 1000-1050, off by <75y so not flagged.

title: Saṃtānāntarasiddhi
consensus_date: late 6th–mid 7th c. CE (Dharmakīrti's floruit; autograph treatise)
basis: Tillemans, Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika ch.4 (2000), intro on Dharmakīrti's date
source_file: cleaned_DHARMAKIRTI'S PRAMANAVARTTIKA. Translation of the 4. chapter  (pararthanumana)_TOM J. F. TILLEMANS, 2000.txt
recension_note: Dharmakīrti's autograph (only the Tibetan survived integrally; Negi's 1997 Sanskrit is a modern back-reconstruction). The dated object is Dharmakīrti's original composition c. 550–660, not the reconstructed Sanskrit string.
note: Dharmakīrti floruit; cur_med 607 within range. Sanskrit is modern reconstruction.

title: Pramāṇavārttikaṭīkā
consensus_date: late 7th-early 8th c. CE (Śākyabuddhi, 660-720)
basis: library notice giving Śākyabuddhi's dates; Dunne, Foundations of Dharmakīrti's Philosophy (2004) on the commentarial stratum
source_file: cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
recension_note: Śākyabuddhi (660-720), pupil of Devendrabuddhi (himself a direct pupil of Dharmakīrti), forms with Devendrabuddhi the 'earliest commentarial stratum' on the Pramāṇavārttika (Dunne 2004). The Inami/Matsuda/Tani edition (Studia Tibetica 23, 1992) prints the Sanskrit recovered from a Kathmandu (National Archives) MS — i.e. Śākyabuddhi's own commentary, no later layer involved. Best placement c. 660-720; cur_med 750 is ~30-40y late but within the 75y tolerance, so not flagged.
note: Śākyabuddhi 660-720; cur_med 750 slightly late but inside tolerance

title: Dharmasaṃgraha
consensus_date: uncertain; compilation likely 4th-6th c. CE (extant Sanskrit, Vaidya ed.)
basis: traditional attribution to Nāgārjuna (rejected); Vaidya (BST 17, 1961); general consensus a later glossary
recension_note: A numerical glossary of Buddhist technical terms transmitted in Nepalese Sanskrit MSS (Vaidya's edition). The Nāgārjuna attribution is spurious; it is a later doctrinal lexicon rather than a 2nd-c. śāstra. The library holds only passing mentions, no recension-specific dating; broad 4th-6th c. range for the compiled Sanskrit text.
note: No recension-specific scholarship in library; wide range, cur_med 440 inside it.

title: Buddhist Yoga Manual (Yogalehrbuch)
consensus_date: 5th c. CE (extant Turfan/Qizil Sanskrit manuscript)
basis: Vignato & Howard, Archaeological and Visual Sources of Meditation in the Ancient Monasteries of Kucha (2015), following Lore Sander's palaeographic dating; ed. Schlingloff 1964
source_file: cleaned_Archaeological and Visual Sources of Meditation in the Ancient Monasteries of Kucha_Vignato and Howard,2015.txt
recension_note: Our text is the actual Sanskrit manuscript (a unique Sarvastivadin/pre-Mahayana meditation manual) recovered from Qizil/Turfan and edited by Schlingloff 1964; it is not a translation from an earlier layer, so the manuscript's own 5th-c. palaeographic date dates the recension directly. No earlier extant version exists.
note: Turfan MS, 5th c. by script (Sander); cur_med 510 within ~50y of midpoint.

title: Kalpanāmaṇḍitikā
consensus_date: 2nd c. CE (Kumāralāta; the Lüders Qizil Sanskrit fragments are this same recension)
basis: Buswell, Encyclopedia of Buddhism; Nakamura, Indian Buddhism (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Buswell_Encyclopedia of Buddhism.txt
recension_note: Composed by Kumāralāta, Aśvaghoṣa's junior contemporary (2nd c. CE); the Chinese 'Sūtrālaṃkāra' translated by Kumārajīva c. 405 CE gives a terminus ante quem. Our edition (Lüders 1926) IS the early Sanskrit recension itself — the actual Qizil/Kucha (Turfan) manuscript fragments — so no later expanded recension is involved here; the physical MS is 3rd-4th c. but the text it carries is the 2nd-c. composition.
note: Kumāralāta = 2nd c. CE (Buswell/Nakamura). Current median 234 vs corrected ~150; difference <75y, no flag.

title: Prasādapratibhodbhava [Śatapañcāśatka]
consensus_date: 2nd c. CE (Mātṛceṭa, contemporary of Kaniṣka)
basis: Nakamura, Indian Buddhism: A Survey with Bibliographical Notes (1980)
source_file: cleaned_Indian Buddhism. A Survey With Bibliographical Notes_Hajime Nakamura, 1980.txt
recension_note: Single-author hymn; the Shackleton Bailey edition gives the extant Sanskrit (Central-Asian fragments) matching the Tibetan and Chinese (Yijing) versions. No earlier Prakrit layer at issue — the Sanskrit is the original composition by Mātṛceṭa, dated 2nd c. CE by his being Kaniṣka's contemporary (Kaniṣka II per Nakamura).
note: cur_med 254 sits in the late-2nd-c. Mātṛceṭa range; consistent.

title: Śabdabhedaprakāśa
consensus_date: early 12th c. CE (c. 1111 CE, by the Viśvaprakāśa's author Maheśvara)
basis: Lexicographical dating by identity with Maheśvara of the Viśvaprakāśa (Śaka 1033 = 1111 CE); Winternitz, History of Indian Literature II, 1933
source_file: cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
recension_note: Single-author homonymic Sanskrit lexicon by the same Maheśvara who composed the Viśvaprakāśa (self-dated 1111 CE), hence c. early 12th c. CE. No layered-recension issue; the Yashovijaya/Bhavnagar 1940 edition (with Jñānavimalagaṇi's ṭīkā) reproduces this dated text. The commentary is later than the root.
note: cur_med 1134 within early-12th-c. window; correct.

title: Viśvaprakāśa
consensus_date: early 12th c. CE (Śaka 1033 = 1111 CE, internal date)
basis: Standard lexicographical dating (internal colophon Śaka 1033); library: skt-en-mono / Winternitz, History of Indian Literature II, 1933
source_file: cleaned_skt-en-mono.txt
recension_note: Single-author Sanskrit lexicon (kośa) by Maheśvara, internally self-dated to Śaka 1033 = 1111 CE. No multi-layer recension problem; the Chowkhamba 1911 edition reproduces this dated 12th-c. text. Not a canonical sūtra/āgama; date is the composition date.
note: cur_med 1107 essentially equals the internal date 1111; correct.

title: Bauddhatantrakośa I
consensus_date: modern digital compilation (2013); contents = mature Buddhist tantra (post-8th c. CE)
basis: edition metadata: Milan Shakya, Nagarjuna Institute / DSBC 2013; Gray (in Powers 2016) on the notional Tantrakośa
source_file: cleaned_Buddhist World_John Powers, ed., 2016.txt
recension_note: Not a historical work: per its own edition metadata this is a modern (2013) digital anthology/compilation of tantric materials assembled by Milan Shakya for the Nagarjuna Institute / Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon. There is no single ancient recension to date; the underlying tantric texts it gathers are mature Buddhist tantra (broadly 8th c. and later). The cur_med of 748 is meaningless for a modern compilation, but the contents are post-8th-c., so the work is not 'off by >75y' in the sense of an antedated ancient text — it has no historical composition date. Confidence none.
note: Modern 2013 anthology; no historical date assignable

title: Mahāvyutpatti
consensus_date: 814 CE (compilation, firm)
basis: Apple, Re-evaluation of Pelliot tibétain 1257; Deleanu 2006
source_file: cleaned_Apple_A Re-evaluation of Pelliot tibétain 1257_An Early Tibetan-Chinese Glossary from Dunhuang.txt
recension_note: The Mahāvyutpatti is a bilingual Sanskrit-Tibetan terminological compendium compiled by an imperial committee in 814 CE under Khri lDe srong btsan (Sad na legs), as part of the standardisation of translation terminology (linked to the sGra sbyor bam po gnyis pa). Firm dated compilation; the Sakaki 1916 edition reproduces this work.
note: cur_med 811 within 25y of firm 814 CE compilation.

title: Sabhikasaugataśāsanapravrajyāvratacaraṇaparivarta
consensus_date: 3rd-4th c. CE (received Mahāvastu recension)
basis: Roe 2014; Powers 2009; Winternitz/Jones tradition
source_file: cleaned_Bull of a man. Images of Masculinity, Sex, and the Body in Indian Buddhism_John Powers, 2009.txt
recension_note: This is the Sabhika chapter of the Mahāvastu-Avadāna (Mahāsāṃghika-Lokottaravāda Vinaya), edited by Senart 1882-97. The work has strata going back to the 2nd c. BCE, but its received/final form — the version Senart edited — is dated to the 3rd-4th c. CE. We date the extant recension, not the earliest oral layer. NOT an 8th c. text.
note: cur_med 783 is grossly wrong: this is the early Mahāvastu, received form 3rd-4th c. CE, off by ~450y.

title: Tarkasopāna
consensus_date: 11th c. CE
basis: Kajiyama, Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy (Tarkabhāṣā), 1966; ed. Tucci, Minor Buddhist Texts I, 1956
source_file: cleaned_Introduction to Buddhist Philosophy. An Annotated Translation of the Tarkabhasa of Moksakaragupta_Kajiyama, Yuichi, 1966.txt
recension_note: Single-author late-Buddhist epistemology manual (Pramāṇa) by Vidyākaraśānti, a Vikramaśīla scholar of the late Pāla period placed in the 11th c. (Kajiyama links him with the Mokṣākaragupta/Atīśa milieu). No earlier layer; Tucci's 1956 critical edition gives the text. Bounds widened to cover the 11th-c. uncertainty.
note: cur_med 1147 (cur range 1100-1199); my 11th-c. window is earlier but overlaps and is <75y from 1147 at the boundary, so not flagged.
